tune_name: There Will Never Be Another You
form: ABAC, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Overall in Eb major; harmony features extended circle-of-fifths ii-V chains with brief tonicizations but no large-scale key change. Common bandstand variations include tritone substitutions on dominants and a backdoor ii-V (Fm7-Bb7) resolving to Eb in the final 4 bars.
history_and_facts:
Written by Harry Warren with lyrics by Mack Gordon for the 20th Century-Fox film Iceland (1942), the song was introduced on screen by Joan Merrill with Sammy Kaye's orchestra. It quickly migrated from the screen to bandbooks and was taken up by swing and early bop players.
Chet Baker's 1954 vocal version helped cement the tune as a modern-jazz staple, while instrumental readings by top improvisers made it a jam-session favorite. Its chord progression-an elegant chain of ii-V movements in a clear ABAC design-makes it a classic vehicle for bebop phrasing and substitute dominants.
On contemporary bandstands the tune is commonly called in Eb major, usually at medium swing but sometimes from ballad to bright tempos. It’s often among the first standards improvisers learn.

Joan Merrill with Sammy Kaye - 1942 (Film Version - Iceland)

form: 12-bar blues; chorus form (no bridge). Most commonly performed in F. Typical bebop-blues harmonies; common lead sheets show quick IV in bar 2, ii-V to IV around bars 4-5, and a turnaround chain in bars 9-12. A widely used version is: | F7 | Bb7 | F7 | Cm7 F7 | Bb7 | Bdim7 | F7 D7 | G7 C7 | F7 | D7 G7 | C7 | C7 (or turnaround) |. Variants simplify to basic I-IV-V blues.
history_and_facts:
Thelonious Monk's riff-driven 12-bar blues was first recorded in 1951 for Blue Note during his early Genius of Modern Music period. Its pithy, repetitive line and sly rhythmic displacements make it one of Monk's most immediately recognizable heads.
Miles Davis's 1958 Milestones version, with John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley, helped cement the tune as a jam-session staple and a core item in the modern jazz repertoire. Monk continued to revisit it throughout his career, notably titling his 1967 Columbia album Straight, No Chaser.
The title borrows a bartending phrase-“straight, no chaser” meaning spirits served neat-typical of Monk's wry naming. The tune's form invites either basic I-IV-V blues changes or the more common bebop-blues substitutions heard on many lead sheets and recordings.

form: ABCD, 36 bars total (8-8-8-12). Final A includes a 4-bar tag. Harmony moves through a chain of ii-V-I progressions with modulatory shifts (including major-third relations) that tonicize multiple centers before returning to the home key; common analyses note tonicizations of Ab and C in the A sections and a bridge that moves to distant keys before resolving back to Ab. Some lead sheets omit or shorten the tag.
standard_key: Ab major (concert); also commonly performed by vocalists in C or F
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1939 Broadway musical Very Warm for May, where it was introduced by Frances Mercer, Hollace Shaw, Ralph Stuart, and Hiram Sherman, the song outlived its short‑run show to become one of the most enduring standards in jazz. Its memorable lyric and long-lined melody sit over a famously modulatory progression that cycles through several key centers in elegant ii-V-I chains.
Bebop musicians embraced the tune for its harmonic richness. Charlie Parker's contrafact Bird of Paradise (1947) is based on its changes, and Parker's 1950 recording with strings helped cement the piece in the modern jazz repertoire. Vocal and instrumental versions proliferated through the 1950s-60s, with Oscar Peterson, Art Tatum, and Ella Fitzgerald contributing widely cited renditions.
On bandstands, it's commonly called in Ab, and the last A is often treated with a 4‑bar (or longer) turnaround tag back to Ab, making it a favorite vehicle for both ballad interpretations and medium swing choruses in jam-session settings.

Ella Fitzgerald - 1963 (Ella Fitzgerald Sings the Jerome Kern Songbook)

chord_progression_notes: Key centers: Ab→C (A), Eb→G (B), G→E (C/bridge), back to Ab (D). The B section transposes A down a perfect 4th. Bar 24's C7b13 functions as V7/vi returning home. Bar 32 uses Bdim7 as a passing diminished (iii°7). Final 4-bar tag (33-36) is a ii-V-I turnaround with the last bar setting up the next chorus. Common subs: tritone sub Db7 for G7 (bar 6); #IVdim7 passing chord in bar 32; altered dominants throughout. Charlie Parker's 'Bird of Paradise' is a famous contrafact on these changes.

form: AABC, 44 bars (14-14-8-8). Extended form with unusual 14-bar A sections instead of the typical 8. A sections feature minor key harmony; B section (bridge) modulates through sequential ii-V movement; C section returns to tonic. Tag ending commonly used. Many recordings resolve with a Picardy-third (major) cadence.
standard_key: D minor (concert) is most common on bandstands; also called in C minor; vocal charts frequently in G minor.
chord_progression_notes: Extended AABC form with 14-bar A sections (44 bars total). A sections feature the characteristic Em7b5-A7b9 turnaround in D minor and Am7b5-D7b9 (ii-V to iv). Bridge modulates through sequential ii-V movement (Bm7-E7, Gm7-C7) leading to Fmaj7. Common substitutions: tritone subs on dominant chords (Eb7 for A7b9), Dm(maj7) for Dm6. Many versions end with a Picardy third resolution to Dmaj7. Tag endings are common in performance.
history_and_facts:
Written by Arthur Schwartz with lyrics by Howard Dietz for the 1932 Broadway revue Flying Colors, Alone Together quickly attracted bandleaders; Leo Reisman had an early hit that same year. Its dark, minor-key character and elegant lyric made it a natural for both dance bands and jazz improvisers.
Artie Shaw's 1939 recording is widely cited for cementing the tune's standard status, and modern jazz players embraced it for its rich harmonic motion, especially the sequential ii-V movement in the bridge. In the hard‑bop era, influential versions by Chet Baker (on the 1959 album Chet) and Kenny Dorham (Quiet Kenny, 1959) helped fix the tune in the small‑group repertoire.
Instrumentalists most often call it in D minor, and many arrangements end with a Picardy‑third resolution to the parallel major-an expressive touch that highlights the tune's bittersweet mood.

Ella Fitzgerald - 1961 (Ella Swings Brightly with Nelson)

form: 32-bar AABA. Each A section: 8 bars of D Dorian; B section: 8 bars of Eb Dorian; final A returns to D Dorian. Common performance practice includes an open D pedal vamp before/after the head and extended modal solos; some bands omit or delay the bridge during solos.
standard_key: D minor (D Dorian; bridge in Eb Dorian)
history_and_facts:
Coltrane's ‘Impressions' is a cornerstone of early-1960s modal jazz, first captured live in 1961 and issued prominently on his 1963 Impulse! album of the same name. Its harmonic plan mirrors Miles Davis's ‘So What'-a D Dorian tonality with a half‑step shift to Eb Dorian on the bridge-but Coltrane's version is typically faster and a vehicle for marathon improvisations.

The tune became a defining jam piece for Coltrane's groups, often exceeding 10-15 minutes in performance and showcasing intense, scalar modal development over a static bass pedal. Its stark two-mode design and minimal melody made it a staple in jazz education and a rite of passage on bandstands.

Despite sharing the modal architecture with ‘So What,' ‘Impressions' is regarded as a distinct Coltrane original with its own head and performance ethos, cementing its status as a modern standard.

form: 32-bar ABAC (8-8-8-8); A sections in I; B often tonicizes IV; C uses a cycle-of-fifths dominants resolving back to I. Common lead-sheet variants add backdoor ii-V and tritone-sub dominants.
history_and_facts:
Duke Ellington composed In a Mellow Tone in 1940 with later lyrics by Milt Gabler. The tune is a contrafact on the 1917 hit Rose Room (Art Hickman/Harry Williams), borrowing its harmonic framework while supplying a fresh Ellington melody. Ellington's orchestra recorded it on September 5, 1940 (often titled In a Mellotone), and it became a staple of the Blanton-Webster band book.

The song quickly entered the common jam-session repertoire and is one of the most-called Ellington swing vehicles. Ella Fitzgerald's 1957 recording on her Duke Ellington Songbook helped popularize the vocal version. Lead sheets typically present it in Ab with a 32-bar ABAC form, and many players incorporate circle-of-fifths turnarounds and backdoor ii-V motions to set up the return to the tonic.

chord_progression_notes: Contrafact on 'Rose Room' (Art Hickman, 1917). A sections feature II7-V7-I (Bb7-Eb7-Ab) followed by ii-V-I to IV (Ebm7-Ab7-Db). B section tonicizes IV (Db6-Gb7) before returning to I. C section uses cycle-of-fifths dominants. Common variations: tritone subs on the II7-V7 (E7-A7), backdoor ii-V approaches (Bbm7-Eb7 as iv-bVII7), passing diminished chord (Ddim7) in bar 26. The F7 turnaround at bar 8/16/32 sets up the II7 (Bb7).

composer: Duke Ellington, Billy Strayhorn (lyrics: Johnny Mercer)
form: AABA, 32 bars. Each A section is 8 bars in C major built from repeated ii-V progressions (Dm7-G7) with secondary dominants and chromatic approach chords; the 8-bar bridge is a circle-of-fifths chain of dominant 7ths (commonly E7-A7-D7-G7-C7-F7-Bb7-Eb7) that back-cycles to the final A. Common variations include tritone substitutions in the bridge and a 2-4 bar I-VI7-II7-V7 turnaround/tag at the end.
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1953 by Duke Ellington with crucial collaboration from Billy Strayhorn, Satin Doll quickly became a signature Ellington theme and a frequent concert closer. Johnny Mercer added the widely sung lyrics several years later, helping the piece cross over from an instrumental band feature to a vocal standard.
The tune is prized by musicians for its elegant, back-cycling dominant movement in the bridge and the idiomatic Ellington/Strayhorn ii-V writing in the A sections. On bandstands, players often use tritone substitutions through the bridge and tag the final A with a short I-VI-II-V turnaround.
Satin Doll's first recordings by the Ellington Orchestra set the template; later vocal and trio versions-particularly those by Ella Fitzgerald and Oscar Peterson-cemented its status as a must-know standard for jam sessions and big band books alike.

form: 32-bar AABC. Four 8-bar sections built on alternating ii-V-I progressions in the relative major and ii-V-i in the minor (commonly: start in the relative major, then cadence to the minor tonic). Frequent variations include a final ii-V-i turnaround tag in the minor; some lead sheets/performances begin directly on the minor i or reorder the initial ii-V.
standard_key: G minor (most common instrumental call), E minor (widely published in lead sheets/Real Book)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Joseph Kosma with Jacques Prévert’s lyric as Les feuilles mortes, first published in 1945 and used in Marcel Carné’s 1946 film Les Portes de la nuit, the song received English lyrics by Johnny Mercer in 1950 (often cited as 1947 in some sources) and soon became internationally popular. Its minor-key setting and elegant chain of ii-V progressions made it a favorite vehicle for jazz improvisation and harmonic study.
The canonical modern-jazz treatment is the Somethin’ Else (Blue Note, 1958) recording by Cannonball Adderley with Miles Davis, which helped cement the tune's minor-key hard-bop approach; Bill Evans’s trio recording (December 1959, released on Portrait in Jazz in 1960) further shaped the tune's modern voicing language. In the broader popular sphere, Roger Williams's 1955 instrumental was a No. 1 hit. The original French version includes a rarely performed verse/intro, while jazz performances typically begin directly on the 32-bar chorus.

form: 12-bar blues; typically in F. Common variants: with or without a quick IV (Bb7) in bar 2; bars 9-12 often use bebop turnarounds (e.g., Gm7-C7 | F7-D7 | Gm7-C7 | F7-C7). No bridge; no key changes.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker introduced Now's the Time at his landmark Savoy session of November 26, 1945-the same date that produced Billie's Bounce and Ko-Ko-helping define bebop on record. Despite its historical role in bop, the tune's head is a bluesy riff, making it approachable while opening into modern harmonic language in solos.
A notorious offshoot of its main riff became The Hucklebuck, a 1949 R&B hit for Paul Williams, widely acknowledged as being based on Parker's theme. Today the piece is a first-call medium-tempo F blues on bandstands and in classrooms, prized for its catchy head and classic bebop vocabulary over a 12-bar framework.

chord_progression_notes: Now's the Time follows a bebop-style 12-bar blues progression in F with several characteristic alterations from traditional blues: 1) Quick IV change in bar 2 (Bb7), creating earlier harmonic motion typical of bebop blues; 2) Passing diminished chord (Bdim7) in bar 6, functioning as a chromatic voice-leading device between IV7 and I7; 3) Secondary dominant approach in bar 8 (Am7-D7 functioning as iii-7 VI7), targeting Gm7 in bar 9; 4) Extended four-bar turnaround (bars 9-12) using ii-V-I-VI-ii-V progression instead of simple V-IV-I blues cadence. The progression maintains dominant 7th quality on the tonic (F7) throughout, preserving blues tonality while incorporating bebop harmonic sophistication.

form: 12-bar blues
history_and_facts:
Tenor Madness is a Bb blues composed by Sonny Rollins and first recorded as the title track of his 1956 Prestige album. The session famously paired Rollins with John Coltrane-the only known studio recording featuring the two tenor giants together-backed by members of the Miles Davis Quintet (Red Garland, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones).

Its riff-based head and straightforward blues form helped cement it as a jam-session staple and a common bandstand call. The original track is widely cited as a hallmark of 1950s hard-bop tenor playing, showcasing contrasting improvisational voices over a driving medium-up blues.

composer: Victor Young, Wayne King, Egbert Van Alstyne (lyrics: Haven Gillespie)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Typically in D minor for the A sections, with a bridge that moves to the relative major (F major) before cadencing back to D minor. Common variants include different turnarounds at the end of the last A (e.g., iiø-V7 or tritone-sub V back to i) and occasional treatment as a 3/4 waltz.
history_and_facts:
Published in 1931 with music by Victor Young, Wayne King, and Egbert Van Alstyne and lyrics by Haven Gillespie, Beautiful Love quickly became a popular song of the day, helped by a hit recording from Wayne King and his Orchestra. It subsequently entered the jazz repertoire and is included in major Real Book editions and standard tune lists.
The song is frequently performed in a minor key (most commonly D minor) and lends itself to both medium swing and ballad/waltz interpretations. Bill Evans' 1961 trio version on Explorations is especially influential among modern jazz players.
Beautiful Love also gained wider recognition through its early-1930s film use (notably in The Mummy, 1932), further cementing its status as a standard called on bandstands and in educational settings.

chord_progression_notes: D minor blues-influenced standard with sophisticated minor ii-V-i progressions. Each A section opens with Em7b5-A7b9 (ii-V in Dm), moves through Gm7-C7 to Fmaj7 (ii-V-I in relative major F), then returns to D minor. Middle portion features Dm-Gm7-Bb7-A7b9 (i-iv-bVI7-V7), where Bb7 functions as a tritone substitution for E7 (V/V). Final A section contains chromatic turnaround: Dm-B7-Bb7#11-A7b9, creating stepwise descending bass motion (D-B-Bb-A) back to tonic. Can be performed in 3/4 waltz or 4/4 swing.

tune_name: The Girl from Ipanema (Garota de Ipanema)
composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English: Norman Gimbel)
form: AABA, 40 bars. A sections in the home key; B section modulates up a semitone before returning to the original key for the final A. Common performance practice includes a short vamp intro and an optional tag/vamp at the end.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); Db major (vocal-Getz/Gilberto version)
history_and_facts:
Composed in Rio de Janeiro in 1962 by Antonio Carlos Jobim with Portuguese lyrics by Vinícius de Moraes, the song was inspired by the beach neighborhood of Ipanema and famously linked to muse Helô Pinheiro. Norman Gimbel's English lyrics (added soon after) helped the tune cross over globally.
The 1964 hit recording from the album Getz/Gilberto, featuring Astrud Gilberto's understated vocal, became a worldwide phenomenon: the single won the 1965 Grammy for Record of the Year and the album won Album of the Year, cementing bossa nova's place in jazz history. The tune's bridge characteristically moves up a half step, a hallmark arrangers and improvisers exploit for contrast.
Often cited among the most recorded standards of all time, it remains a core jam-session staple. Instrumentalists typically call it in F, while many vocal versions (including the iconic Getz/Gilberto track) are in Db.

chord_progression_notes: A sections use a progression similar to 'Take the A Train' with tritone subs (Gb7 instead of C7). The 16-bar bridge is unique—twice the typical length—modulating through three key centers using backdoor dominants (bVII7 chords): Db area (F#maj7→B7), E area (F#m7→D7), F area (Gm7→Eb7), then a standard iii-VI-ii-V turnaround (Am7-D7-Gm7-C7) to return home. Jobim's harmonic ambiguity—never landing on expected tonic chords in the bridge—is a hallmark of this tune.

form: AABA, 32 bars — sections A1–A2–B–A2 (8 bars each). A sections in Bb major; the bridge commonly shifts to the relative minor (G minor) via repeated minor iiø–V (Am7♭5–D7 → Gm), a “false” key change, before returning to Bb for the final A. A-section bar-3/4 variations often use III7–VI7 or bVII7(#11)–VI7 (e.g., Bb–Eb7–D7–G7 or Bb–Eb7–Ab7(#11)–G7).
history_and_facts:
Published in 1936, There Is No Greater Love was one of Isham Jones's last big hits before he disbanded his orchestra, with lyrics by Marty Symes. The tune's graceful melody and cycle-of-fifths harmony made it attractive to both swing-era bands and later small-group modernists.
The song became a jam-session staple in the bebop and hard-bop eras; Miles Davis's 1956 quintet version is especially influential for its relaxed medium-swing treatment and streamlined changes. Vocalists embraced it as well-Dinah Washington's 1954 jam-session performance helped cement its place in the repertoire.
On bandstands the tune is commonly called in Bb for instrumentalists, while singers often favor C. Its straightforward AABA design and back-cycling ii-V chains make it a go-to vehicle for teaching and soloing.

form: AABA', 32 bars (also analyzed as ABCA' or ABCD)
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); also commonly G or Bb major for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Published in 1926 by Tin Pan Alley team Ray Henderson and Mort Dixon, Bye Bye Blackbird was an immediate pop hit (notably for crooner Gene Austin) and soon entered the jazz repertoire. The song's sturdy 32-bar AABA design and conversational melody made it a favorite for both instrumental improvisers and singers.
Miles Davis's 1956 recording with his first great quintet (issued on 'Round About Midnight in 1957) reintroduced the tune to modern jazz audiences and helped cement its status as a post-bop jam-session staple. Vocal interpretations by artists such as Carmen McRae further broadened its reach across jazz styles.
After Miles Davis's passing in 1991, Keith Jarrett's Standards Trio recorded the album Bye Bye Blackbird as a tribute, underscoring the tune's enduring association with Davis and its continuing life as a touchstone of the jazz canon.

chord_progression_notes: Simple diatonic progression centered on F major with ii-V-I motion. Bars 1-8 (A): Fmaj7 tonic with Gm7-C7 motion and Am7b5-D7b9 tonicizing ii. Bars 9-16 (A): Gm tonicization via D7, resolves to F6. Bars 17-24 (B): Chromatic descending dominants F7-E7-Eb7-D7b9, then Db7 (tritone sub for G7) to C7. Bars 25-32 (A): Returns to opening material with turnaround tag. Common subs: extended Fmaj7 voicings in A sections, D7alt for D7b9, additional chromaticism in bridge. Miles Davis 1956 version is definitive for harmonic interpretation.

form: AABA, 32 bars; contrafact on I Got Rhythm (rhythm changes). Typically in B-flat: A sections over Bb rhythm-changes harmony; bridge uses III-VI-II-V (D7-G7-C7-F7) leading back to Bb. Common bebop substitutions and turnarounds appear in many lead sheets.
history_and_facts:
Composed by tenor saxophonist Lester Young, Lester Leaps In is a riff-based head on the rhythm-changes progression and one of the quintessential small-group Basie-era anthems. Its first and most celebrated recording was by Count Basie and His Kansas City Seven in 1939, with Young's relaxed yet driving choruses helping define his cool, linear approach to swing-era improvisation.

The tune quickly became a favored jam vehicle and a staple at Jazz at the Philharmonic in the mid-1940s, where its up-tempo rhythm changes provided an open canvas for extended tenor battles. Today it remains one of the most-called rhythm-changes heads on bandstands and in education, prized for its simple, catchy melody and direct link to Young's influential phrasing and time feel.

composer: George Gershwin (lyrics: DuBose Heyward, Ira Gershwin)
form: 16-bar AA' (8+8) in minor; no bridge. Typical harmonic outline i-iv-i with V at bars 7-8; A' often reharmonized with iiø-V to V (e.g., Bm7b5-E7) and other minor-key substitutions. Commonly played as a slow swing or rubato ballad; some modern versions use a modal vamp on Am.
standard_key: A minor (instrumental); D minor, E minor (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Gershwin's lullaby-aria from Porgy and Bess (premiered 1935), “Summertime” is sung by the character Clara and functions as a leitmotif throughout the opera. Its pentatonic-inflected minor melody and folk-spiritual character made it immediately adaptable to jazz performance.
Billie Holiday's 1936 recording helped establish the tune as a jazz standard, and subsequent landmark treatments by Ella Fitzgerald and Louis Armstrong (1957) and Miles Davis with Gil Evans (1958) cemented its place in the repertoire. The song is among the most frequently recorded standards and is often treated with reharmonizations or modal vamps while retaining its 16-bar minor form.
Instrumentalists typically call it in A minor, while vocal versions vary by range. Although commonly performed as a ballad, it appears across tempos and grooves, from slow swing to bossa and modal treatments.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Modal jazz composition with A sections in D Dorian (Dm11) and B section (bridge) in Eb Dorian (Ebm11). The form exemplifies modal jazz's static harmony approach, with each 8-bar section maintaining a single modal center. Common performance practice includes an open D pedal vamp introduction.
chord_progression_notes: Modal jazz masterpiece with only two chords. The iconic 'So What chord' voiced by Bill Evans uses quartal harmony (stacked fourths): Root-4th-b7-b3-5th. No functional harmony or ii-V-I progressions; instead, static modal centers allow melodic/scalar improvisation. The bridge modulates via parallel half-step motion (chromatic mediant relationship). The call-and-response between bass (ascending melodic phrase) and piano/horns (quartal voicings) defines the head. This harmonic approach became foundational for modal jazz, influencing compositions including John Coltrane's 'Impressions' which uses the identical form.
standard_key: D minor (D Dorian mode; bridge in Eb Dorian)
history_and_facts: So What is one of the most famous modal jazz compositions, written by Miles Davis and featured on his landmark 1959 album Kind of Blue. The tune is based on the Dorian mode and features a distinctive bass line that has become one of the most recognizable in jazz. The composition marked a significant departure from bebop's complex chord progressions, instead focusing on modal improvisation over simple harmonic structures.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically in C major; A sections stay in the tonic with II-V motion; the bridge is a circle-of-fifths chain of dominants (A7-D7-G7-C7-F7-Bb7-Eb7-Ab7), often using tritone substitutions, resolving via G7 back to the final A. Common variants include an optional 4-8 bar intro/vamp and a 2-bar tag or Ellington-style coda.
standard_key: C major (instrumental call key); also Ab and Bb major commonly used for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Billy Strayhorn wrote Take the 'A' Train after moving to New York, titling it after the Brooklyn-Manhattan A subway that ran to Sugar Hill in Harlem. In early 1941, during the ASCAP radio licensing dispute that limited airplay of Duke Ellington's existing repertoire, Ellington adopted Strayhorn's BMI-published tune as his new theme. The band's February 1941 recording became a major hit and cemented the piece as the Ellington orchestra's signature.
Trumpeter Ray Nance's opening solo on the 1941 side set an enduring performance template. A celebrated vocal/scat feature by Betty Roche on Ellington Uptown (1952) further popularized the tune, and Joya Sherrill's widely used lyrics are the basis for many vocal versions. The bridge's driving circle-of-fifths dominants and the catchy opening hook make it a perennial jam-session call.
Lead sheets and bandstand practice generally call the tune in C for instrumentalists, though singers frequently favor Ab or Bb. Many arrangements include a short vamp intro and a brief tag or Ellington-style shout-chorus coda.
famous_recordings: Duke Ellington and His Orchestra - 1941 (Victor Records - Grammy Hall of Fame)
Ella Fitzgerald with Duke Ellington - 1957 (Ella Fitzgerald Sings the Duke Ellington Song Book)
Oscar Peterson - 1952 (Oscar Peterson Plays Duke Ellington)
Duke Ellington Orchestra - 1962 (Goodyear Jazz Concert TV Special)
chord_progression_notes: The A sections feature the characteristic D7#11 (whole-tone flavor from the b5/Ab), a signature Strayhorn sound. Bridge stays on Fmaj7 (IV) for 4 bars before D7 (V/V) leads to Dm7-G7 turnaround. Common to add 4-bar intro/vamp and Ellington-style coda. Some charts use D9 instead of D7#11.

form: Form: 16 bars, often heard as AB (8+8). In the standard C– chart, • A (bars 1–8): in C minor – I–I–IV–IV (Cm7–Cm7–Fm7–Fm7), then a minor iiø–V–i (Dm7b5–G7(♭9)–Cm7–Cm7). • B (bars 9–16): modulates to Db major for 4 bars via ii–V–I (Ebm7–Ab7–Dbmaj7–Dbmaj7), then returns to C minor with a minor iiø–V–i (Dm7b5–G7(♭9)–Cm7), often adding an extra iiø–V (Dm7b5–G7) in bar 16 as a turnaround.
history_and_facts:
Kenny Dorham wrote Blue Bossa amid the early-1960s bossa nova wave that swept U.S. jazz, drawing on ideas he encountered after visiting Brazil. The tune debuted on tenor saxophonist Joe Henderson's Blue Note album Page One (recorded June 3, 1963), with Dorham in the quintet-an outing that cemented the composition's status in the modern repertoire.
Blue Bossa's minor-key bossa groove, concise 16-bar structure, and brief detour to Db major make it a foundational vehicle for learning Latin feel, minor ii-V language, and modal mixture. It quickly became a jam-session staple and appears in major lead-sheet anthologies, frequently called in C minor by instrumentalists.
Educators often use Blue Bossa to bridge hard bop vocabulary and Brazilian rhythm: the harmony invites both bebop lines over minor ii-V-i and lyrical phrasing over the bossa pattern, explaining its enduring popularity with students and seasoned players alike.
famous_recordings: Chet Baker & Wolfgang Lackerschmid — 1979 (from Ballads for Two).

form: 32-bar AABA: 8-bar A sections featuring circle-of-fifths motion (commonly in Eb major); 8-bar bridge built on sequential II-V progressions moving by step before resolving back to tonic. No permanent modulation; frequent use of tritone substitutions and back-cycling II-V's in authoritative lead sheets.
standard_key: Eb major (instrumental call key); also C major (common vocal), F major
history_and_facts:
Composed by Jimmy Van Heusen with lyrics by Johnny Burke, the song was published in 1943 and introduced on screen by Dorothy Lamour in the 1944 Paramount film And the Angels Sing. Early hit recordings by Bing Crosby and Jo Stafford quickly established it in the American popular repertoire.
By the mid-1950s it became a core jazz standard, with Chet Baker's 1954 vocal version and the Miles Davis Quintet's 1956 reading (Relaxin' sessions) shaping its modern-jazz profile. The tune's lyric warns against guarding too closely against love, while the music's elegant circle-of-fifths motion and singable melody make it a favorite for both ballad and medium-swing treatments.
On bandstands it's most often called in Eb for instrumentals, while vocalists frequently choose C or neighboring keys. The bridge's chain of II-V's invites colorful substitutions, making it a common vehicle for teaching harmonic flow and voice-leading in standards.

form: 16-bar strophic form (no separate bridge), typically repeated; many vocal arrangements modulate up between later choruses.
standard_key: Standard / common keys: Bb major (very common in jazz fake books and for vocalists); C major (also widely used, especially in some vocal/published editions and on some bandstands).
history_and_facts:
Originally titled Die Moritat von Mackie Messer, the song was written by Kurt Weill and Bertolt Brecht for Die Dreigroschenoper (The Threepenny Opera), which premiered in Berlin in 1928. Marc Blitzstein's 1954 English adaptation provided the lyrics most familiar to American audiences.
Louis Armstrong's 1955 recording was pivotal in introducing the tune to the jazz world, and Sonny Rollins's 1956 performance as Moritat on Saxophone Colossus helped cement it as a jazz vehicle. Bobby Darin's 1959 hit made the song a pop sensation with successive key changes, and Ella Fitzgerald's 1960 live version in Berlin-featuring improvised lyrics after she famously lost the words-became a landmark vocal performance.
In jazz settings the tune is frequently played in Bb major, though C major is also common (especially in some published and bandstand versions). Many arrangements, following Bobby Darin’s hit, climb by half-steps for later choruses.

chord_progression_notes:
This progression is a simplified diatonic version in Bb.
Common practice uses simple functional changes (I–vi–ii–V in the home key) with possible secondary dominants. Changes vary between theatre, pop, and jazz charts; players often simplify or embellish.

form: ABCD (8-8-8-8). Through-composed 32-bar form with four distinct 8-bar sections. A section (bars 1-8) opens with unresolved minor ii-V progressions away from the tonic; B section (bars 9-16) continues sequential ii-V movement through multiple key centers, finally stating the tonic Bbmaj7 in bar 9; C section (bars 17-24) features extended dominant and minor harmonies; D section (bars 25-32) begins with the same Em7b5-A7b9 opening as the A section but diverges harmonically with backcycling ii-V progressions resolving to the tonic. Note: Some sources label this ABCA' due to the shared opening gesture in sections A and D, but harmonically only 2 of 8 bars are identical, making ABCD more accurate. Notable for delayed tonic arrival, unresolved ii-V chains, and sophisticated voice leading.
standard_key: Bb major; also C major and Eb major (often for vocalists)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Victor Young as a haunting instrumental theme for the 1944 Paramount film The Uninvited, the piece acquired Ned Washington's lyrics in 1946 and quickly entered the pop and jazz repertoire. Early postwar recordings helped popularize it, and its cinematic origin explains the tune's striking, non‑tonic opening and dramatic harmonic motion.
In jazz, Stella by Starlight became a proving ground for advanced harmony: the melody rides over a web of ii-V progressions and deceptive cadences that obscure the home key until the final cadence. It is a staple in Real Book editions and on bandstands, often called in Bb for instrumentalists, though the original film score centered in a minor key and vocalists choose comfortable transpositions.
Defining jazz interpretations span decades, notably Miles Davis's classic studio cut with his sextet in 1958 and his ballad reading from the 1964 Lincoln Center concert. The tune's rich changes have made it a perennial for re-harmonization and pedagogy.

chord_progression_notes: Stella by Starlight is renowned for its harmonic sophistication and through-composed structure. Key features: (1) Delayed tonic - Bbmaj7 doesn't appear until bar 9; (2) Unresolved ii-V progressions - Em7b5-A7b9 opening never resolves to Dm in the A section, creating harmonic ambiguity; (3) Sequential descending motion - ii-V chains move down by whole steps (Em7b5-A7 to Dm7 to Cm7, etc.); (4) Multiple implied key centers - D minor, F major, Eb major, C minor all suggested without clear tonicization; (5) Extensive use of altered dominants (b9, #5, #11) and half-diminished chords; (6) 'Backcycling' in final D section with chromatic descending ii-V progressions (Em7b5-A7b9, Dm7b5-G7b9, Cm7b5-F7b9) leading to final tonic resolution. Common substitutions include tritone subs on bridge dominants and variations in bars 7-8 and 10. The Real Book version (reflected in this database) represents modern jazz interpretation; earlier lead sheets may differ slightly in specific chord voicings and extensions.

form: 12-bar blues in concert F. No sectional key changes. Common bebop variants include a quick change to Bb7 by bar 2, a passing diminished in bar 6 (e.g., Bdim/Bbdim), and an I-VI-ii-V turnaround in bars 9-12 (F7-D7-G7-C7). Many lead sheets also show ii-V movement approaching IV and a ii-V back to I in bar 8.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Billie's Bounce is a cornerstone bebop blues first recorded at the landmark Savoy session of November 1945, the same period that yielded Ko-Ko and Now's the Time. The tune helped codify the bebop approach to a 12-bar F blues, pairing Parker's angular melodic line with more sophisticated harmonic motion than a traditional blues.
Over time it became a jam-session staple and a teaching vehicle for bebop phrasing over blues changes. Lead sheets and recordings commonly incorporate bebop turnarounds and passing diminished chords, but the piece remains fundamentally a straightforward F blues at medium-to-up tempos.

form: 12-bar blues; typically played as a medium swing Bb blues. Common variations include a ii-V in bars 9-10 and a V (or turnaround) in bar 12; many authoritative lead sheets present basic blues changes with optional chromatic approach chords characteristic of Monk.
history_and_facts:
One of Thelonious Monk's most enduring blues heads, Blue Monk was first recorded in 1954 for Prestige and quickly became a jam‑session staple. Its singable, ascending chromatic line over a straightforward blues made it both accessible and unmistakably Monk.

The tune’s legacy was cemented by numerous landmark performances: the 1958 Five Spot recordings with Johnny Griffin (often cited as fixing the ‘definitive’ head), and the rediscovered 1957 Carnegie Hall concert with John Coltrane. Vocalese entered the tradition when Abbey Lincoln added lyrics and recorded a celebrated vocal version in 1961 (often titled ‘Monkery’s the Blues’), helping the piece enter the vocal jazz repertoire.

Blue Monk is widely taught as a gateway into Monk's language: simple blues form, but with subtle chromaticism and phrasing that invite players to balance tradition with Monk's quirky melodic contours.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinicius de Moraes)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antonio Carlos Jobim with Portuguese lyrics by frequent collaborator Vinicius de Moraes, O Grande Amor became known internationally through the bossa nova wave of the early 1960s. Its earliest widely documented recording appears on the Getz/Gilberto project, recorded in 1963 and released in 1964, which helped introduce many Jobim works to jazz audiences worldwide.

The tune is a lyrical bossa nova ballad prized for its sophisticated harmony and understated melodic contour, qualities that make it adaptable as either a gentle bossa or a straight ballad in jazz settings. While not as ubiquitous as Jobim hits like The Girl from Ipanema or Corcovado, it remains a respected selection in the Jobim canon and is called by instrumentalists and vocalists who favor his deeper catalog.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Mexican marimbist Alberto Domínguez, "Frenesí" originated as a Latin piece and was popularized in the United States by big bands. Artie Shaw's 1940 recording became a major hit and helped cement the tune's crossover appeal.
Charlie Parker recorded it in 1950 (with strings), and the song has since been treated as a Latin standard within the jazz repertoire, often performed in a rhumba/bolero feel.

history_and_facts:
Horace Silver introduced Filthy McNasty on his live Blue Note album Doin' the Thing: The Horace Silver Quintet at the Village Gate, recorded in May 1961. It quickly became one of his signature funky blues heads.
The tune is known for its earthy, riff-driven theme, stop-time hits, and hard-bop groove, making it a popular jam-session choice and a staple in the soul-jazz repertoire.
Vocalese versions later appeared, helping further cement its status among post-bop era standards.

form: 32-bar ABAC in 3/4; four 8-bar sections. Typically remains in the home key with brief tonicizations via secondary dominants and ii-V progressions; many lead sheets/performances add a short tag or extended ending.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Bill Evans as a gentle 3/4 piece for his niece Debby, the tune first appeared on his debut album New Jazz Conceptions (1956). It became one of Evans's signature works and a definitive modern jazz waltz.
Its status as a standard was cemented by the 1961 Village Vanguard performances with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian, later issued on the album Waltz for Debby. Gene Lees subsequently wrote English lyrics, and the song has been widely recorded by vocalists; a notable Scandinavian version with Swedish lyrics ("Monicas vals") was recorded by Monica Zetterlund with Evans in 1964. The piece remains a bandstand favorite for its lyrical melody, subtle harmonies, and flowing 3/4 feel.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). A sections are diatonic and riff-based, typically outlining I-vi-ii-V language; the bridge uses a cycle-of-fifths chain of dominants before returning to the tonic (e.g., in Db: A7-D7-G7-C7-F7-Bb7-Eb7-Ab7 → Db). Common bandstand variations include drum or ensemble breaks into A sections and a tagged last A for endings.
standard_key: Db major (concert); also commonly called in Ab major
history_and_facts:
Written by saxophonist-arranger Edgar Sampson while working with Chick Webb's orchestra, Stompin' at the Savoy took its name from Harlem's legendary Savoy Ballroom, the epicenter of Lindy Hop culture. Webb's band introduced it in 1934, and the tune quickly became a swing-era anthem.
Benny Goodman's hit 1936 recording propelled the piece into the mainstream, and it has since remained a jam-session staple for its catchy riff melody and friendly AABA changes with a classic circle-of-fifths bridge. Though often co-credited to Chick Webb (bandleader) and Benny Goodman (whose publisher added his name), authorship is widely recognized in jazz scholarship as belonging to Sampson; lyrics were later supplied by Andy Razaf.
The tune's enduring popularity owes much to the Savoy Ballroom's status as an integrated, high-energy dance hall-nicknamed the “Home of Happy Feet”-and to definitive recordings by swing luminaries that cemented its place in the repertoire.

tune_name: What Is This Thing Called Love?
form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). A sections in minor tonic; bridge runs a cycle of dominant 7ths moving by fifths that resolves to the dominant and returns to i. Common jazz variants add tritone substitutions and extended ii-V turnarounds.
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote both words and music for this song for the 1929 revue Wake Up and Dream, and it quickly entered the jazz canon for its dark minor tonality and propulsive harmonic motion. The tune's durable 32‑bar AABA design and cycle‑of‑fifths bridge make it a favorite for up‑tempo swing and reharmonization.
In the bebop era, Tadd Dameron's contrafact “Hot House” (1945) on these changes helped cement the progression's central place in modern jazz, even as performers continued to record Porter's original melody. Landmark interpretations range from Frank Sinatra's brooding 1955 ballad reading to Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 entry in her Cole Porter Song Book and virtuosic small‑group instrumentals.
On bandstands the tune is most often called in C minor, while vocal keys vary by singer. Its harmony invites tritone substitutions and back‑cycling, and many players treat the final A with an extended ii-V-i in minor before tagging the turnaround.

form: 12-bar blues in concert C; no bridge. Typically treated as a bebop blues with common variants: quick IV in bar 2, ii-V into IV around bars 4-5, and ii-V movement in bars 9-10 leading to a turnaround in bars 11-12. No key changes in the original.
history_and_facts:
Cheryl is a bebop 12-bar blues composed by Charlie Parker and first recorded for Savoy in 1947. It has since become a staple bebop blues head on jam sessions, commonly called in concert C.

The tune exemplifies Parker's approach to blues lines-compact, melodic, and designed to sit over bebop harmonizations without obscuring the core 12-bar form. Lead sheets and transcriptions generally reflect a straightforward blues structure with idiomatic bebop passing ii-V progressions added by players.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Small-group lead sheets condense material from the Miles Davis Nonet chart; the overall songform remains AABA across authoritative versions.
history_and_facts:
John Carisi, a trumpeter-arranger associated with the Gil Evans circle, wrote Israel for the Miles Davis Nonet's landmark Birth of the Cool sessions. The piece's linear writing and cool, contrapuntal texture helped define the nonet's modernist aesthetic and made it a study piece for arrangers and improvisers.

Bill Evans's 1961 trio version on Explorations reintroduced the tune to small-group repertory, solidifying its status as a working-band standard. While the nonet score is dense and orchestrally voiced, most lead sheets distill it to a 32-bar AABA form, preserving the distinctive melodic contours and modern harmony that made it a post-bop favorite.

standard_key: F minor (instrumental); G minor (common vocal); E minor (also encountered)
history_and_facts:
Written for the Broadway musical One Touch of Venus, which opened in 1943, Speak Low quickly outgrew the show to become a jazz standard. Ogden Nash's lyric opens with a line adapted from Shakespeare's Much Ado About Nothing: “Speak low if you speak love.”
Billie Holiday's postwar recording helped cement the tune's status among jazz singers, while Bill Evans's 1962 Interplay session made it a modern jazz staple for instrumentalists. On bandstands it is often treated with a Latin (bossa) feel on the main sections and a swing release, and it is commonly called in a minor key, most often F minor for instrumental settings.
The song's minor tonality and elegant, chromatic melody make it a favorite for reharmonization; many authoritative lead sheets reflect small but accepted variations, especially in turnarounds and tags.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Duke Ellington and introduced on the 1962 trio album Money Jungle with Charles Mingus and Max Roach. The piece is also known as African Flower (Little African Flower).
The tune is characteristically sparse and modal, often performed as a slow, evocative ballad. It has become a modern jazz standard, especially among pianists, and appears in contemporary real books.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Typically in a major key with rich chromatic harmony; no standard key modulation between sections. Common jazz reharmonizations include tritone substitutions and backdoor ii-V cadences into the final A.
history_and_facts:
Published in 1952 by British-born composer Guy Wood with lyrics by Robert Mellin, “My One and Only Love” began as a popular ballad that quickly crossed into the jazz repertoire. Frank Sinatra's 1953 Capitol single helped establish its profile, and it soon became a favored vehicle for singers and instrumentalists alike.

The definitive jazz reading for many musicians is the 1963 recording by John Coltrane and Johnny Hartman, celebrated for its luxuriant tempo, legato phrasing, and nuanced harmonic shading. The tune's lyric famously opens with the phrase “The very thought of you,” coincidentally echoing the title of a different 1934 standard, which occasionally causes confusion among newcomers. Its lush, chromatic harmony invites tasteful reharmonization while preserving the classic 32-bar AABA architecture.

form: 12-bar blues in F. Typical bebop-blues variant: I7-IV7 in bars 1-2; return to I7 through bar 4; IV7 in bar 5; common use of Bdim7 (tritone approach) in bar 6; I7 to VI7 (often F7 to D7) in bars 7-8; bars 9-10 frequently ii-V to I (Gm7-C7 to F7), and bar 12 a turnaround (I-VI-ii-V) back to the top. Lead sheets differ slightly but all retain a 12-bar blues chorus with bebop substitutions.
history_and_facts:
Au Privave is a quintessential Charlie Parker bebop blues, first recorded in 1951 for Norman Granz's Clef/Verve catalogue and later compiled on Swedish Schnapps: The Compositions of Charlie Parker. Its melody sits over a modernized 12-bar blues with bebop substitutions rather than Parker's more harmonically dense “Bird blues” chain of ii-V's.

Collectors note that an alternate take has appeared on some releases under the mis-titled name “Apres Vous,” which has fueled decades of confusion about the tune's French-looking title. Despite the orthography, the “French” is generally considered playful rather than literal. The tune is widely included in major fake books and educator syllabi and is a common call on jam sessions in the key of F.

A seminal bebop contrafact on the chord changes of the 1920 standard “Whispering,” “Groovin' High” helped define the new bop language on 52nd Street. It is typically performed with a distinctive arranged intro and coda.
The first famous recording was cut by Dizzy Gillespie with Charlie Parker in 1945, and the piece quickly became a jam-session staple and a core item in bebop repertoires and Real Book indexes.

form: AABA, 36 bars (commonly 10-10-8-8). Chromatic voice-leading and secondary dominants throughout; typically no modulation between sections. Some authoritative lead sheets note slight phrase-length variants in the A sections.
standard_key: C major (instrumental call key); Ab or Bb major common for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Composed by David Raksin as the principal theme for Otto Preminger's 1944 film Laura, the melody became instantly recognizable in cinemas before Johnny Mercer added lyrics the following year. Raksin famously wrote the theme under intense deadline pressure, and its haunting, chromatic contour helped define the film-noir sound of the era.
The tune quickly migrated into the jazz repertoire. Woody Herman's 1945 hit helped popularize it, and Charlie Parker's lush 1950 With Strings version cemented its status among improvisers. Clifford Brown's 1955 strings recording further showcased its lyrical, harmonically rich canvas for ballad interpretation.
On bandstands, Laura is typically called as a ballad in C, with its 36-bar AABA design and distinctive chromatic approach chords inviting nuanced reharmonization. Vocalists frequently favor flatter keys (Ab or Bb), and Mercer's enigmatic lyric-written after the film's release-has become as iconic as Raksin's original theme.

history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote this ballad for the 1944 Broadway revue Seven Lively Arts. It quickly entered the Great American Songbook and became a staple for both vocalists and instrumentalists.
The lyric “how strange the change from major to minor” is mirrored by the harmony, with a notable modulation at that moment-a characteristic touch often highlighted by performers.
Its 32‑bar AABA form and lyrical poignancy have made it a core jazz standard frequently appearing in Real Book indexes and standard repertoires.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English: Norman Gimbel)
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1962 by Antônio Carlos Jobim with Portuguese lyrics by Vinícius de Moraes, the song later received English lyrics by Norman Gimbel under the title “Jazz 'n' Samba (I Only Dance Samba).” Its title literally means “I only dance samba,” and it became one of the signature bossa nova numbers to cross into the jazz repertoire.
The piece gained international recognition through the Getz/Gilberto collaborations, most notably the 1964 studio album that helped cement the bossa nova-jazz connection worldwide. Subsequent live performances by the same artists kept the tune in circulation, and it has since been widely covered by both instrumentalists and vocalists, remaining a common set piece for a bright, samba-feel closer.
In fake books and discographies the tune often appears under both titles-“Só Danço Samba” and “Jazz 'n' Samba”-reflecting the dual-language life it has enjoyed since the early 1960s.

Bill Evans's 3/4 ballad “Very Early” is widely regarded as one of his signature originals, first documented by the Bill Evans Trio in 1962 on the Riverside album Moon Beams. Its lyrical melody and sophisticated harmonic motion exemplify Evans's post-bop language and his penchant for shifting tonal centers within an elegant song form.

Evans revisited the piece throughout his career; an influential later studio version was recorded in 1968 with Eddie Gómez and Jack DeJohnette (released decades later as Some Other Time: The Lost Session from the Black Forest). The tune has since become a staple for jazz pianists and small groups, often performed as a reflective waltz that invites reharmonization and subtle metric rubato.

form: AABA
history_and_facts:
Composed by Brazilian guitarist Luiz Bonfá for the 1966 film The Gentle Rain, with English lyrics by Matt Dubey, the tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire. It is commonly performed as a medium bossa nova or ballad.
The song is typically treated as a 32-bar AABA form in a minor key (often E minor), featuring classic minor ii-V movement. It appears in Real Book indexes and is widely covered by jazz guitarists and pianists.

form: AABA
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1938 Broadway musical The Boys from Syracuse, where it was introduced by Muriel Angelus. The song quickly entered the American popular song repertoire and later became a jazz standard.
Jazz musicians often take it at a bright tempo; while commonly played in 4/4 swing, it is also frequently adapted as a jazz waltz in 3/4.
Lorenz Hart's lyric provides a characteristically ironic counterpoint to Rodgers's buoyant melody, warning against idealized romance.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Typically in C major; A sections use functional, diatonic bebop harmony with cycle-of-fifths motion; bridge moves through a sequence of dominant/ii-V progressions that circle back to the tonic. Common variants include substituted dominants in the turnaround back to A and altered dominants in bar 7-8 of each A.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Charlie Parker and first recorded for Dial Records in Los Angeles on March 28, 1946, Yardbird Suite quickly became one of the defining bebop heads. The title nods to Parker's nickname, “Bird,” and the piece showcases hallmark bebop melodic language over a concise 32‑bar AABA structure.
Notable later interpretations helped cement its status across styles: Gil Evans arranged it for Cannonball Adderley on New Bottle Old Wine (1958), reframing the tune's bebop lines with orchestral color, while Art Pepper's Marty Paich arrangement on Art Pepper + Eleven (1959) became a West Coast classic. Saxophonist Sonny Stitt's Stitt Plays Bird (1963) also kept the tune central to post‑bop repertoire.
On bandstands and in pedagogy, Yardbird Suite is frequently called in C concert, serving as a go‑to vehicle for studying bebop phrasing, enclosure devices, and the interplay of chromatic approach tones over functional harmony.

composer: Frank Foster (lyrics: Ella Fitzgerald; Jon Hendricks)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Commonly played in F major; no standard modulation across sections. Basie/Foster big-band arrangements often add an 8-bar intro, interludes, and an extended shout chorus, but the core head is 32-bar AABA.
history_and_facts:
Frank Foster wrote Shiny Stockings in 1955 while he was a tenor saxophonist and arranger in Count Basie's “New Testament” band. The piece quickly became one of the Basie book's signature medium-swing numbers, showcasing Foster's knack for elegant riffs, poised ensemble writing, and space-hallmarks of the band's postwar style. The definitive early studio recording was by the Count Basie Orchestra in 1956, and the chart remained a Basie staple for decades.

Lyrics were later added by both Ella Fitzgerald and Jon Hendricks, helping the tune cross over from instrumental feature to vocal favorite. Lambert, Hendricks & Ross popularized Hendricks's vocalese version in 1957, and Ella recorded it with the Basie band in 1963, further cementing its status as a standard.

form: 32-bar AABA. Each A is 8 bars in minor (commonly i with bVI-bVII-i motion and chromatic approaches); B brightens to the relative major before returning to the minor A. Common lead-sheet variants use altered dominants and tritone substitutions on turnarounds; some arrangements add a brief tag/coda.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Jerome Kern with lyrics by Otto Harbach for the 1933 Broadway musical Roberta, Yesterdays quickly joined Smoke Gets in Your Eyes as one of the show's enduring standards. Though written as a theater ballad, its minor-key melancholy and rich harmonic motion made it a favored vehicle for jazz interpretation.
Charlie Parker's 1950 Bird With Strings reading helped cement the tune in the post-bop repertoire, while Art Tatum's solo versions showcased its pliable harmony for pianistic reharmonization. Clifford Brown's 1955 strings date offered a lyrical trumpet perspective, and Ella Fitzgerald enshrined it in the 1963 Jerome Kern Song Book.
Players often treat it as a reflective ballad or a relaxed medium swing. The commonly used chorus (without the verse) emphasizes the descending harmonic color of the A sections and a contrasting bridge that momentarily lifts to the relative major before returning to the minor home key.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English: Norman Gimbel)
form: AABA
history_and_facts:
A bossa nova standard by Antônio Carlos Jobim with original Portuguese lyrics by Vinícius de Moraes; Norman Gimbel wrote the widely performed English lyrics as "How Insensitive." The Portuguese title "Insensatez" translates roughly to "foolishness" or "insensibility." The harmony is famously influenced by Chopin's Prelude in E minor (Op. 28, No. 4), reflected in its descending minor-key progression.
It entered the international jazz and pop repertoire in the mid-1960s and appears in major fake books. The tune is typically performed as a lyrical bossa-tinged ballad and is a staple of Jobim-focused recordings.

form: 16-bar riff blues. Canonical Miles Davis arrangement stays on I (F7) for the first 8 bars with a chromatic/riff-based head; then IV (Bb7) for 4 bars; back to I (F7) for 2 bars; V-IV (C7-Bb7) for 2 bars. Many bands keep the 16-bar form for solos; some use standard blues substitutions or quick-IV in bar 2.
standard_key: F (concert), sometimes called in F minor (blues inflection)
history_and_facts:
Walkin' is a riff-based blues by Richard Carpenter that became a signature vehicle for the Miles Davis All Stars on the April 3, 1954 Prestige session. The track's swaggering, medium-tempo groove and concise, shout-chorus head helped define the emerging hard-bop aesthetic.
Prestige later used the tune to title the 12-inch LP Walkin' (issued in 1957, compiled from 1954 sessions), a release widely cited by jazz historians as a landmark in the transition from bebop to hard bop. The piece quickly became a jam-session staple thanks to its straightforward yet distinctive 16-bar blues form and memorable chromatic hook.
Although the melody leans heavily on the minor/blues inflection, the underlying progression is commonly treated as an F blues, and the tune is most often called in concert F on the bandstand.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Commonly performed as the refrain only; some vocal arrangements include a short introductory verse that is often omitted in instrumental settings. No standard key change within the form.
standard_key: F major (also commonly in Eb major for vocal settings)
history_and_facts:
The classic American popular ballad was originally written by Jimmy Van Heusen with lyrics by comedian Phil Silvers in 1942 with a different title and lyric. It was rewritten in 1944 as "Nancy (with the Laughing Face)" to celebrate the fourth birthday of Frank Sinatra's daughter, Nancy. Frank Sinatra made the first commercial recording of the song on December 3, 1944, which was released the following year. Therefore, 1944 is cited as the definitive composition year for the standard as it is known today. This song quickly became associated with Frank Sinatra, for whom it became a signature number.

Although widely linked to Sinatra's daughter Nancy, Silvers later recounted that the lyric predated that association; nonetheless, Sinatra's mid-1940s recording made it a staple of his repertoire and cemented the song's popularity.

Jazz instrumentalists have also embraced the tune: John Coltrane's spare, lyrical reading on the album Ballads helped establish it on bandstands as a vehicle for melodic ballad playing. Lead sheets typically present the 32-bar AABA refrain; the introductory verse sometimes found in vocal anthologies is rarely performed in jazz instrumental contexts.

form: AABA (32-bar; contrafact of 'What Is This Thing Called Love?')
history_and_facts:
A flagship bebop head by Tadd Dameron, “Hothouse” is a contrafact built on the chord progression of Cole Porter's “What Is This Thing Called Love?”. It quickly became a jam-session staple among beboppers.
Dizzy Gillespie and Charlie Parker popularized the tune with landmark recordings and performances in the mid-1940s, cementing it as a core bebop standard. The title often appears as “Hot House.”
famous_recordings: Dizzy Gillespie Sextet feat. Charlie Parker - 1945

composer: Bernie Miller (lyrics: Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically in a minor key; A sections center on the tonic minor with a characteristic chromatic bass line; bridge employs dominant-cycle motion and brief tonicizations before returning to the tonic minor for the last A.
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1952 by pianist Bernie Miller, Bernie's Tune became emblematic of the West Coast cool-jazz sound after the Gerry Mulligan Quartet's hit 1952 recording. Mulligan's pianoless arrangement with Chet Baker foregrounded contrapuntal lines and a lean, swinging feel that helped cement the tune's place in the modern jazz repertoire.

Lyrics were later added by Jerry Leiber and Mike Stoller, creating a vocal version, although the tune remains primarily performed as an instrumental. The piece is most often called in D minor, with its minor-key A sections featuring a chromatically descending bass figure that gives the melody its distinctive, sly character.

Early documentation also cites a 1952 recording by Jerry Wald and His Orchestra, but it was Mulligan's version that popularized the tune among jazz musicians and audiences, ensuring its status as a standard.

form: 12-bar blues; riff head. Common bebop changes: quick IV in bar 2; bars 5-6 on IV; bar 8 often VI7 leading to ii-V in bars 9-10; turnaround in bars 11-12 (I-VI-ii-V or V-IV-I-V). No key changes.
history_and_facts:
Oscar Pettiford's riff blues became a jam‑session staple and is frequently called under its alternate title, Collard Greens and Black‑Eyed Peas. While composer Oscar Pettiford first recorded the tune on August 12, 1954, the actual premiere recording was by the Stan Getz Quintet on August 15, 1952, under the alternate title "Collard Greens and Black-Eyed Peas." The exact composition date is unknown.
Bud Powell's 1956 trio version helped cement the tune's status and led to occasional misattribution of the piece to Powell. On bandstands it's typically treated as an up- to medium‑up bebop blues, most often in C, with common bebop substitutions (quick IV, VI7 to ii-V, and a turnaround in bars 11-12).

history_and_facts:
Red Clay first appeared as the title track of Freddie Hubbard's 1970 CTI Records debut, produced by Creed Taylor and recorded at Van Gelder Studio. The studio band-Hubbard on trumpet, Joe Henderson on tenor saxophone, Herbie Hancock on electric piano, Ron Carter on bass, Lenny White on drums, and Airto Moreira on percussion-helped define the early CTI sound that fused hard bop vocabulary with funk grooves and modal harmony.

Built on a driving E‑minor vamp and a pentatonic-inflected melody, Red Clay quickly became a jam-session favorite and one of Hubbard's signature tunes. He revisited it frequently in concert, notably on the CTI “In Concert” dates with Stanley Turrentine in the early 1970s, further cementing its status as a modern jazz standard.

form: 32-bar AABA contrafact on Back Home Again in Indiana. Typically in concert Ab: A sections (8 bars each) follow the Indiana cycle (I-VI7-II7-V7) with common bebop substitutions (e.g., secondary dominants, tritone subs); the bridge (8 bars) moves to IV (Db) and cadences back via VI-II-V to the final A. Common lead-sheet variants include iii-VI-ii-V turnarounds in bars 7-8 and tritone substitutions on the dominants.
history_and_facts:
A blistering bebop head, Donna Lee is now widely regarded as a Charlie Parker composition, though the original Savoy release credited Miles Davis-likely due to contractual or publishing issues. The tune was first recorded by the Charlie Parker All-Stars in 1947 and quickly became a proving ground for postwar improvisers.
Built as a contrafact on the chord changes to Back Home Again in Indiana, the line is dense with chromatic enclosures, rapid arpeggiation, and rhythmic displacement-hallmarks of Parker's vocabulary. Its technical demands have made it a staple audition and jam-session test piece.
Jaco Pastorius' 1976 unaccompanied-bass rendition helped cement the tune's cross-generational fame, showcasing its adaptability beyond the bebop context while reaffirming its status as a core jazz standard.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8); most lead sheets in concert Eb. Bridge commonly moves through cycle-of-fifths ii-V progressions before returning to I; turnarounds often use tritone substitutions.
standard_key: Eb major (instrumental call key); also C or Db major for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Written by Jule Styne with lyrics by Sammy Cahn for the 1948 Warner Bros. film Romance on the High Seas (Doris Day's screen debut), It's You or No One quickly crossed from film music into the jazz repertoire. Doris Day introduced it on screen, and the tune was widely picked up by band singers and small-group jazz players soon after its release.
Instrumentalists favor the song for its bright, propulsive AABA design and classic circle-of-fifths motion, which invite uptempo swing treatments and reharmonization (especially on the turnaround back to the A). On bandstands it is most often called in concert Eb, while vocal arrangements frequently appear in C or Db to suit range.
Alongside other Styne/Cahn collaborations of the era, the piece has endured as a jam-session staple, balancing a memorable melody with changes that reward bebop vocabulary and modern substitutions.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Jimmy Van Heusen with lyrics by Johnny Burke for the Bing Crosby-Bob Hope film Road to Rio (Paramount, 1947), the song was introduced on screen by Bing Crosby, who also recorded it that year. Its introspective lyric-cataloging love's contrasts-and long, arching melody quickly made it a favored vehicle for jazz ballad interpretation.

Billie Holiday's 1958 rendition on Lady in Satin is widely cited by jazz historians as a landmark vocal reading, and the tune has remained a staple for singers and instrumentalists alike. The 1974 live performances by Stan Getz and Bill Evans (later issued as But Beautiful) further underscored the song's harmonic richness for modern improvisers.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). A sections in the home key with blues-inflected harmony (frequent minor iv and backdoor motions); bridge commonly moves to IV and sequences through secondary dominants before a ii-V-I return. Many lead sheets include a brief 2-4 bar tag on the last A.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); also Bb major (vocal) and Eb major
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1946 Broadway musical St. Louis Woman, Harold Arlen's gospel-tinged melody and Johnny Mercer's plainspoken, devoted lyric helped the song outlive the show to become a core American standard. Multiple 1946 singles quickly popularized it, and it entered the jazz repertoire soon after.
Ray Charles's 1959 big‑band reading transformed it into a soul‑jazz touchstone and remains one of the most referenced versions by arrangers. Ella Fitzgerald's rendition on her Harold Arlen Song Book (1961) and Frank Sinatra's lush Reprise-era take helped cement its status for both singers and instrumentalists.
On bandstands it's typically treated as a slow ballad or slow swing, with performers leaning into Arlen's blues colors, minor‑iv/backdoor turns, and a short tag on the final A; the theatrical verse is often omitted in jazz settings.

composer: Leslie Bricusse, Anthony Newley (lyrics: Leslie Bricusse, Anthony Newley)
history_and_facts:
Written by Leslie Bricusse and Anthony Newley for the 1971 film Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory, the song was introduced on screen by Gene Wilder. Its memorable melody and sophisticated harmonic motion helped it migrate from film repertoire into the jazz world. 

By the 1990s the tune was being recorded and performed by jazz artists, notably pianist Eric Reed (who titled a 1998 album after it). The song's chromatic voice-leading and evocative lyric content have made it a frequent choice for ballad treatments and reharmonization in modern jazz contexts.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Antonio Carlos Jobim; English lyrics: Gene Lees)
form: 32-bar AABA. Each section is 8 bars. Most commonly in F major. A sections feature I-#idim-ii-V voice-leading (e.g., Fmaj7-F#dim7-Gm7-C7) repeated. The bridge (B) cycles through secondary dominants in a circle-of-fifths (often notated E7-Am7-D7-Gm7-C7) before returning to the final A. Common variants include a 2-4 bar Fmaj7 vamp intro/outro, tritone substitution on the C7, and optional tag endings.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); C major and Eb major (common vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Jobim wrote Corcovado in the early bossa nova era, titling it after Rio de Janeiro's Corcovado mountain. He penned the original Portuguese lyric; Gene Lees later supplied the English lyric Quiet Nights of Quiet Stars, which helped the tune travel globally.
The song first circulated on Brazilian recordings at the start of the 1960s and quickly became a core bossa nova standard. Jobim's own 1963 instrumental version on The Composer of Desafinado, Plays and the 1964 Getz/Gilberto rendition (with Astrud Gilberto singing in English) cemented its international status. Frank Sinatra's 1967 collaboration with Jobim further established it in the American songbook.
Musicians prize Corcovado for its serene melody, subtle chromatic bass motion via the #idim approach, and a bridge that moves through a compact cycle of fifths-hallmarks of Jobim's elegant voice-leading and harmonic economy.
famous_recordings: Stan Getz & João Gilberto feat. Astrud Gilberto - 1964

tune_name: You'd Be So Nice to Come Home To
form: AABA, 32 bars; minor-key A sections typically in D minor; bridge modulates to the relative major (F major) before returning to D minor. Common variations include different ii-V turnarounds back to D minor.
standard_key: D minor (instrumental call key); C minor (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote this song for the 1942 film Something to Shout About, and it was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Song (losing to Irving Berlin's White Christmas). Its wartime imagery and yearning lyric helped make it an immediate popular success.

Jazz musicians quickly adopted it for its memorable minor-key A sections and contrasting major bridge, and it became a staple in the hard-bop era. The Clifford Brown/Max Roach Quintet's fiery 1954 rendition and Helen Merrill's 1954 performance with Brown are especially influential; Ella Fitzgerald further canonized it on her 1956 Cole Porter Songbook.

On bandstands the tune is most often called in D minor, though singers frequently choose C minor. The harmony invites reharmonization, and many charts employ varied ii-V turnarounds to return to the tonic minor.

form: AABA (32-bar)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1940 Broadway musical Pal Joey, where it was introduced by Gene Kelly and Leila Ernst (as Joey Evans and Linda English). The show helped cement several Rodgers & Hart songs as standards.
The tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire and is frequently played as a medium swing number. It follows a classic 32-bar AABA form and is common in jam-session books and Real Book editions.
famous_recordings: André Previn, Shelly Manne & Red Mitchell - 1957

form: 32-bar AABA; four 8-bar sections. Commonly notated in concert F major. Bridge typically uses cycle-of-fifths dominants and brief tonicizations to return to the A section; tritone substitutions on turnarounds are common in modern lead sheets.
standard_key: F (instrumental call key); Eb (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Donald Kahn with lyrics by Stanley Styne in 1956, A Beautiful Friendship quickly entered the jazz singer's book. Both writers were second-generation Tin Pan Alley royalty-Kahn the son of lyricist Gus Kahn, and Styne the son of composer Jule Styne-bringing classic songcraft to a mid-century standard that vocalists and small groups continue to favor.

Ella Fitzgerald's 1961 recording helped cement the tune's stature, and later hard-swinging versions-such as Mel Tormé's with Buddy Rich-demonstrated how well the song adapts to brisk tempos. On bandstands it's most often counted as a medium swing at 32 bars AABA, typically in F for instrumentalists, with singers frequently opting for Eb.

The tune's straightforward AABA design, singable melody, and cycle-of-fifths bridge make it a popular choice for both jazz vocal features and instrumental blowing vehicles.

form: 32-bar ABAC contrafact on Sweet Georgia Brown; 8-bar sections; harmony built on a circle-of-fifths chain of dominants with common bebop reharmonizations (e.g., tritone substitutions, backdoor ii-V) and a turnaround back to the tonic. Variants in authoritative lead sheets retain the ABAC layout while adjusting passing dominants and approach chords.
standard_key: F major (concert); also Ab major (less common)
history_and_facts:
Dig is an early bebop/early hard-bop milepost by Miles Davis, first recorded for Prestige on October 5, 1951 in New York. The session-later issued as the LP Dig-featured an intergenerational frontline of Davis (trumpet), Sonny Rollins (tenor saxophone), and a teenage Jackie McLean (alto saxophone), with Walter Bishop Jr., Tommy Potter, and Art Blakey in the rhythm section. 

The tune is a contrafact on the changes to Sweet Georgia Brown, retaining the 32-bar ABAC framework while supplying a new bop line. Most bands call it in concert F, though some play it in Ab. While official composer credit is to Miles Davis, some jazz histories and interviews have noted claims that Jackie McLean contributed the melody; the recorded and publishing credits, however, consistently list Davis. 

The 1951 Prestige recording (and its alternate take) became the definitive version, helping to introduce McLean on record and document Rollins in an early, formative setting. The title track later anchored the mid-1950s 12-inch Prestige release Dig, which compiled material from that landmark session.

standard_key: Db major (concert); also commonly in C major and Eb major for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Billy Strayhorn began writing Lush Life as a teenager and is widely reported to have completed it around 1938, just as he was meeting Duke Ellington. A harmonically rich, lyrically sophisticated ballad, it became one of Strayhorn's signature works and a proving ground for singers and instrumentalists comfortable with its chromatic voice-leading and quick modulations.

The song circulated in the Ellington/Strayhorn orbit before entering the broader repertoire; Nat King Cole's 1949 recording helped establish it beyond the band. Ella Fitzgerald's 1957 reading and John Coltrane's celebrated 1958 extended performance cemented its status, while the 1963 Coltrane-Johnny Hartman version is often cited as a definitive vocal interpretation.

Notoriously difficult, Lush Life's long, prose-like lyric and intricate harmonies have made it a touchstone of jazz sophistication. Frank Sinatra famously attempted it in 1958 but abandoned the session-an anecdote that underscores both the song's allure and its formidable demands.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Typically in minor: A sections in i; bridge modulates to the relative major (e.g., to Bb major when in G minor). Common practice adds circle-of-fifths dominants; frequent use of tritone substitutions on VI7 and altered Vs; typical ii-V-i tags at the end.
standard_key: G minor (concert); also common: A minor
history_and_facts:
Written by British composer Philip Braham with lyrics by Douglas Furber for the London revue A to Z, Limehouse Blues quickly crossed from the stage into the jazz repertoire. The lyric references London's Limehouse district and its historic Chinatown; because of dated orientalist stereotypes, modern performances are usually instrumental.
By the mid-1930s it had become a virtuosic showpiece: the Quintette du Hot Club de France cemented its status as a fast swing vehicle, and later the Oscar Peterson Trio made it a barnburner on concert programs. Although it has words, jazz musicians almost always call the tune instrumentally in a minor key at brisk tempos.

form: 12-bar blues; riff head over bebop-blues changes (often quick IV in bar 2, ii-V to IV in bar 4, ii-V in bars 9-10, turnaround bars 11-12). No sectional key changes; common variations include tritone substitutions and altered dominants in the turnaround.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Cool Blues is a compact bebop riff over a 12‑bar blues that Bird introduced in the late 1940s. Its first documented appearances date to 1947 performances, where Parker's taut, blues-infused line exemplified his knack for fusing bebop language with earthy blues phrasing.
The tune later found a strong second life in the organ-combo scene; Jimmy Smith's live Blue Note recordings from 1958 (later issued under the title Cool Blues) helped cement it in the repertoire. On bandstands, it's typically called as a medium-up F blues, leaving ample room for blowing while keeping the melody punchy and memorable.
chords: unknown

form: 32-bar AABA. Each section 8 bars. A sections in the tonic (commonly Ab), using a I-VI7-II7-V7 circle-of-fifths motion; the bridge typically continues a dominant-cycle progression (e.g., C7-F7-Bb7-Eb7) before resolving back to the final A. Variants in authoritative lead sheets maintain the AABA design with minor reharmonization in the bridge.
standard_key: Ab major; also C major (common in Gypsy-jazz settings)
history_and_facts:
Pianist-bandleader Earl Hines introduced Rosetta in the early 1930s, with lyrics later supplied by Henri (Henry) Woode. It quickly became one of Hines's most enduring tunes and a popular swing-era vehicle for improvisation.

Django Reinhardt's Hot Club reading and Art Tatum's dazzling solo piano versions helped cement the tune as a standard beyond Hines's own orchestra. Musicians favor Rosetta for its straightforward 32-bar AABA design and propulsive circle-of-fifths motion, which invites clear, melodic soloing at medium to up tempos.
famous_recordings: Django Reinhardt & Quintette du Hot Club de France - 1938

form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. A sections in the home key; bridge uses cycle-of-fifths ii-V chains with brief tonicizations before returning to I. Common variations include tritone-sub dominants and backdoor turnarounds in A sections.
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1929 MGM film Marianne, Just You, Just Me quickly migrated from the screen to the bandstand and became a durable jazz standard. Early popular recordings helped cement its status during the transition from the dance-band era to small-group swing.
Its 32-bar AABA design, with a bridge that cycles through ii-V chains, makes it a flexible blowing vehicle. Thelonious Monk famously wrote the contrafact Evidence on these changes-originally punningly titled Justice ("Just Us")-which further linked the tune to the bebop and modern-jazz repertoire.
The piece remains common at jam sessions, typically called at a medium swing tempo, and is often notated in B-flat in major lead-sheet collections.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English lyrics: Ray Gilbert)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). Commonly performed straight-8 bossa; many lead sheets include a short 2-bar tag/coda on the final A. No obligatory key change in the form, though some arrangements add an ending tag.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); also Eb major (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antônio Carlos Jobim with original Portuguese lyrics by Vinícius de Moraes, Amor em Paz entered the bossa nova repertoire in the early 1960s and quickly migrated into mainstream jazz. Ray Gilbert's English lyric, Once I Loved, helped the song reach a wider international audience.
João Gilberto's early-1960s recording introduced the tune, and Jobim's own 1963 Verve album The Composer of Desafinado, Plays cemented its harmonic and melodic blueprint for jazz players. Astrud Gilberto's mid-1960s version popularized the English lyric, and the song has since been embraced by jazz singers and instrumentalists alike, often rendered as a slow, intimate bossa.
Musicians prize the tune for its elegant, voice-leading-rich progressions and lyrical, understated melody-hallmarks of Jobim's craft-making it a staple on bandstands and in Real Book-style collections.
