form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). A sections commonly harmonized with a I-VI7-II7-V7 circle-of-fifths; bridge uses sequential secondary dominants and related tonicizations before a II-V-I return. Typically no permanent key change; common variations include dominant substitutions and backdoor II-V in the turnaround.
standard_key: G major (instrumental); also F major; Ab major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1924 Broadway musical Lady, Be Good!, the song was introduced on stage by Fred and Adele Astaire. Under its original title “Oh, Lady, Be Good!,” it quickly became a favored vehicle for improvisers because of its propulsive circle-of-fifths harmony and clear AABA design.
Lester Young's 1936 small‑group performance with Count Basie became a landmark in jazz phrasing and linear improvisation, while Django Reinhardt and Stéphane Grappelli helped popularize it in Europe. Ella Fitzgerald's 1947 Decca single-featuring a virtuosic scat chorus-cemented the tune as a jam‑session staple and a showcase for up‑tempo swing.
famous_recordings: Count Basie (Jones-Smith Incorporated, feat. Lester Young) - 1936
chords: unknown

form: AABA (32 bars)
history_and_facts:
Published in 1926 by Jack Palmer and Spencer Williams, this tune quickly became a staple of both traditional jazz and the Swing era. Its driving circle-of-fifths motion in the A sections and a contrasting bridge make it a favored jam-session vehicle.
Charlie Christian's celebrated performances with the Benny Goodman Sextet helped cement its status, and Django Reinhardt's Hot Club interpretations remain widely referenced.
famous_recordings: Django Reinhardt & Stéphane Grappelli (Quintette du Hot Club de France) - 1936

form: AABA, 32 bars total (8-8-8-8). A sections use rapidly cycling ii-V progressions and bebop substitutions; the bridge is a cycle-of-fifths chain of dominants resolving back to the tonic. Variants in authoritative lead sheets differ slightly in specific tritone substitutions and turnarounds.
history_and_facts:
Dizzy Gillespie wrote Woody 'n You for Woody Herman in the early 1940s; although Herman didn't initially record it, the piece entered history when Coleman Hawkins and His Orchestra cut it in 1944, often cited as one of the first bebop compositions captured on record. Gillespie soon recorded it himself, helping cement the tune in the nascent bebop repertoire.
The tune appears in discographies and bandbooks under the alternate title Algo Bueno (Woody 'n You), reflecting arrangements that sometimes bring a Latin tinge to sections of the form. Its hallmark is a dense web of ii-V motion and tritone substitutions that showcase the bebop language while remaining a reliable jam-session staple.
Over the decades, Woody 'n You has been interpreted by numerous modernists; its challenging harmony and classic AABA structure make it a proving ground for bebop vocabulary and rhythmic agility.

form: AABA
history_and_facts:
Written by Cole Porter for the MGM film Broadway Melody of 1940. It quickly entered the American songbook and has remained a staple of the jazz repertoire.
The tune is commonly interpreted as a lyrical ballad, though notable bossa nova renditions (especially Sinatra with Jobim in 1967) helped popularize a Latin approach. Its 32‑bar AABA design supports both vocal and instrumental interpretations.

form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Typically in tonic minor with prominent tonicization of bII in the A sections (e.g., Eb major over a D minor tonal center). Bridge sequences through related dominant/minor areas before returning to tonic minor. Common lead-sheet variations include minor-maj7 to minor 7 motion and altered dominants.
history_and_facts:
Invitation was written by Bronisław Kaper for the 1952 MGM film of the same name, with lyrics by Paul Francis Webster added for vocal versions. Though conceived for cinema, it quickly migrated into the jazz canon, prized for its dark, minor-key color and distinctive modal/tonal shifts that evoke an "exotic" atmosphere.
On bandstands the tune is commonly treated as a Latin in the A sections (often with a switch to swing on the bridge, depending on the arrangement). Its hallmark move to the flat-II area in the A sections and the minor-maj7 sonority make it a favorite vehicle for exploring altered dominants and minor-key voice-leading.
Electric bassist Jaco Pastorius's large-ensemble arrangement in the early 1980s helped re-popularize the piece with a new generation of players, while vocal and instrumental renditions across decades have cemented its status as a core minor Latin standard.

composer: Ben Bernie, Maceo Pinkard (lyrics: Kenneth Casey)
form: ABAC, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). A sections: cycling dominants starting on VI7 (e.g., in F: D7–G7–C7→F), classic circle-of-fifths motion. B section: continues functional back-cycling/tonicization (often analyzed as i–V7–i motion in minor relative areas). C section: two bars of vi–V/vi alternation (e.g., Dm7–A7 repeated) leading to a chromatic descent I–VII7–♭VII7–VI7 and a II–V–I turnaround (e.g., F–E7–E♭7–D7 → G7–C7–F). Some sources also describe the overall form as A1–A2 (16+16) depending on phrase grouping; the verse is rarely performed. No standard mid-form key change; tempo commonly bright.
standard_key: F major (most common instrumental); Also heard in A♭ major (original/published key; common in gypsy/manouche circles) and G major (manouche/guitar circles)
history_and_facts:
Written in 1925 by bandleader Ben Bernie and composer Maceo Pinkard with lyrics by Kenneth Casey, Sweet Georgia Brown quickly became a hit in the dance-band era and a durable jazz vehicle thanks to its propulsive circle-of-fifths harmony. Early popular versions by Ben Bernie and Ethel Waters helped establish the song's wide appeal.

Django Reinhardt and Stéphane Grappelli's 1937 Hot Club rendition cemented the tune as a jam-session staple, and it has remained a favorite for uptempo swing and virtuoso improvisation. Miles Davis's contrafact Dig and Thelonious Monk's Bright Mississippi are both based on Sweet Georgia Brown's changes, underscoring its importance to bebop and modern jazz vocabulary.

In 1949, Brother Bones's whistling-and-bones recording was adopted as the Harlem Globetrotters' theme, making the melody one of the most recognizable in American popular culture.

Educators often flag the last four bars’ chromatic I–VII7–♭VII7–VI7 descent as essential turnaround vocabulary; many players call it in F on bandstands, though gypsy-jazz circles favor A♭ or G.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Standard chorus typically remains in the home key with no internal modulation. Many charts include an optional short verse intro and a final tag; some vocal arrangements add a last-A key lift.
standard_key: F major (most common call for instrumental/vocal); C major (common male vocal alternate)
history_and_facts:
Written by Harry Warren with lyrics by Mack Gordon for the 1941 20th Century-Fox film Sun Valley Serenade, “At Last” quickly became associated with Glenn Miller's orchestra and was featured again in the 1942 film Orchestra Wives. The Miller recording helped establish the song in the American songbook.
Etta James's 1960 recording for her debut album At Last! (arranged by Riley Hampton) transformed the tune into an enduring soul-jazz ballad and became her signature performance. It remains a staple at weddings and torch-song sets. Her version was later inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame.
The tune is widely treated as a classic 32-bar AABA standard on bandstands; vocal charts often include an introductory verse and may use a climactic key change on the final A.

form: 32-bar AABA; often analyzed as a reharmonization drawing on Tadd Dameron's “Lady Bird” turnaround; charts vary in the exact bridge changes
standard_key: G major (also commonly called in F major)
history_and_facts:
Composed and first recorded by John Coltrane on his 1957 Blue Note album Blue Train, Lazy Bird is one of the key hard-bop originals from his pre-Atlantic period. The track features Lee Morgan, Curtis Fuller, Kenny Drew, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones.
The title is widely regarded as a nod to Tadd Dameron's Lady Bird, and many analysts hear clear harmonic kinship-especially the use of the Dameron turnaround embedded in Coltrane's more elaborate changes. While not as ubiquitous as Giant Steps, it has become a favored vehicle among advanced improvisers for its brisk tempo and sophisticated cycle-based harmony.
Lead sheets and educator analyses sometimes differ in the exact details of the bridge and passing harmonies, but the tune is generally treated as a 32-bar AABA form and is most commonly called in concert G, with F also appearing in some bandstand and educational sources.

history_and_facts:
Written by Jimmy McHugh with lyrics by Frank Loesser for the 1943 Paramount film Happy Go Lucky, where it was introduced by Mary Martin. The song quickly entered the American popular songbook and has since been adopted by jazz musicians.
Trumpeter-vocalist Chet Baker's mid-1950s version helped cement the tune's jazz-standard status, and the title later became synonymous with him via Bruce Weber's 1988 documentary Let's Get Lost. The song is frequently performed either as a relaxed medium swing or as a tender ballad.

tune_name: Things Ain't What They Used to Be
form: 12-bar blues; typically in F. Common variants include a quick change to IV in bar 2; bars 9-12 often use V-IV-I-V turnaround or a ii-V to I (e.g., Gm7-C7-F7-C7 in F). Riff-based head; no bridge; frequently arranged with shout-chorus hits.
history_and_facts:
Mercer Ellington wrote this riff-based 12-bar blues in the early 1940s, and it quickly became a staple of the Duke Ellington orchestra book-often mistaken as a Duke composition but officially credited to his son. Lyrics were later added by Ted Persons, though the tune is most often performed instrumentally.
A defining early recording by Duke Ellington's orchestra in 1942 helped cement the piece in the swing canon, commonly serving as a relaxed, groove-oriented feature for soloists (notably alto saxophonist Johnny Hodges). The tune's durability across eras is reflected in later landmark recordings, including the 1959 Ellington-Hodges collaboration and Oscar Peterson's widely known version on Night Train.
On the bandstand it's commonly called in F as a straight-ahead blues with classic swing phrasing, and it serves as a jam-session favorite due to its memorable riff and flexible, solo-friendly harmony.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). A sections in the home key; bridge features common ii-V motion and brief tonicizations; many lead sheets include a turnaround/tag on the last A.
history_and_facts:
Also known as “Just Squeeze Me (But Please Don't Tease Me),” the song was written by Duke Ellington with lyrics by Lee Gaines of The Delta Rhythm Boys. It entered the Ellington band's book in 1941 and was introduced on record that year by Ellington's orchestra featuring vocalist Ivie Anderson, becoming one of the era's elegant, blues-tinged pop-jazz hits.
The tune's memorable, singable A-section and functional, ii-V-driven bridge made it a natural for improvisers, helping it endure as a jam-session staple. It has been interpreted across tempos-from intimate vocal readings to medium-up instrumental swing-by leading jazz figures.
Notable later versions that cemented its status include Miles Davis's mid-1950s quintet treatment, Ella Fitzgerald's 1957 Ellington Songbook rendition, and Oscar Peterson's 1959 Ellington tribute, all of which helped keep the piece active in postwar jazz repertoire.
famous_recordings: Duke Ellington and His Famous Orchestra (vocal: Ivie Anderson) - 1941

history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote Anything Goes for the 1934 Broadway musical of the same name, where it was introduced by Ethel Merman. Its witty, topical lyric satirizes contemporary fads and shifting social mores, and Porter's own lyric is as celebrated as the song's buoyant, syncopated melody.

The tune has remained a jazz favorite, especially among vocalists, with Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Cole Porter Songbook helping to cement its place in the jazz repertoire. Instrumental versions by Oscar Peterson and the Dave Brubeck Quartet further carried it into mainstream jazz sets, and the song continues to be revived in stage revivals and modern recordings.

form: 32-bar AABA. Four 8-bar sections; riff-driven A sections in the home key, bridge cycles dominant chords to set up the return to I; typically no key change. Big-band charts often add a shout chorus and a tagged coda.
standard_key: C major (instrumental); also F major (some lead sheets/vocal charts)
history_and_facts:
Freddie Green, Count Basie's long-time rhythm guitarist, wrote Corner Pocket in the mid-1950s for the Basie orchestra; it quickly became a signature Basie swinger in the band book. The earliest classic recording is on Basie's April in Paris era sessions, where it's treated as a sleek, riff-based feature for the ensemble and soloists.
Don Wolf later supplied lyrics, and the tune gained a parallel life as Until I Met You. Vocal versions by Ella Fitzgerald and others helped cement its status beyond the big-band world, while small groups adopted it as a medium-up swing vehicle.
The title Corner Pocket nods to billiards, a wink at Basie's cool, unhurried aesthetic. Though born as a big-band chart, the AABA 32-bar structure makes it easy to call on bandstands, and it remains a favorite for its clean, blues-tinged lines and amenable blowing changes.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Composed by Ralph Rainger with lyrics by Leo Robin for the 1937 Paramount film Easy Living, the song functioned as the film's romantic theme. Although not performed vocally in the movie, it quickly entered the jazz repertoire.
Billie Holiday's 1937 recording with Teddy Wilson and His Orchestra is widely credited with popularizing the tune among jazz musicians and audiences, establishing it as a go-to ballad. It has remained a staple for both instrumentalists and vocalists, valued for its lyrical melody and harmonically rich but singable changes.
Later landmark versions by artists such as Paul Desmond (with Jim Hall) and Sonny Rollins helped cement its status across generations and styles, from cool-inflected interpretations to modern post-bop readings.

tune_name: I've Got You Under My Skin
form: 64-bar AABA (16-bar sections). Commonly preceded by an 8-16 bar vamp intro; many vocal arrangements (notably Sinatra/Riddle) modulate up a half-step for the final chorus.
standard_key: E-flat major; D-flat major (common vocal/Frank Sinatra)
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote both words and music for this song for the 1936 MGM film Born to Dance, where it was introduced on screen and quickly became a hit; contemporary dance-band recordings (notably Hal Kemp's) helped establish it beyond the movie. The tune's durable 64-bar AABA design and elegant lyric made it a natural for jazz interpretation.

Frank Sinatra's 1956 studio recording with Nelson Riddle (Songs for Swingin' Lovers!) is the definitive version for many listeners: a famous slow-burn arrangement that builds over the bridge to Milt Bernhart's soaring trombone solo. Sinatra frequently revived it, including his 1966 Live at the Sands performance with Count Basie, reinforcing its status as a core standard.

Jazz and pop vocalists have kept it in regular rotation-Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Cole Porter Song Book reading is a benchmark-while instrumentalists often call it in E-flat. Vocal charts frequently appear in D-flat, and many arrangements add a half-step lift for the final chorus.

form: 32-bar AABA contrafact on “How High the Moon”; 8-bar A sections and 8-bar bridge. Typically in concert G major with cycle-of-fifths movement and frequent secondary dominants; common variations include tritone substitutions on dominant chords and a ii-V turnback in the final two bars to loop the form.
history_and_facts:
A signature bebop head, “Ornithology” is co-credited to alto saxophonist Charlie Parker and trumpeter Benny Harris. The title nods to Parker's nickname, “Bird,” and the tune is a contrafact built on the chord changes to “How High the Moon.” It quickly became a jam-session staple in the late 1940s, prized for its fleet, angular melody and chord-driven lines that exemplify the bebop language.

The piece was first recorded by the Charlie Parker Septet in 1946 (Dial), in Los Angeles during Parker's seminal West Coast period. Over time, musicians have introduced small harmonic tweaks-especially tritone substitutions and turnaround variations-but the underlying 32-bar AABA framework and “How High the Moon” harmony remain standard.

While originally an instrumental, later vocalese versions appeared, further cementing the tune's status across generations. It remains a core study piece for improvisers learning bebop phrasing over circle-of-fifths progressions and rapid ii-V movement.

form: 32-bar AABA. A sections (8 bars each) built over an Eb pedal with non-functional maj7/add9 sonorities above; B section (8 bars) over a Bb pedal; final A returns to the Eb pedal. Many lead sheets include a short tag/cadence at the end.
history_and_facts:
Composed by John Coltrane in 1959 and named for his first wife, Juanita Naima Grubbs, Naima was first recorded during the Giant Steps sessions and issued on the Atlantic album Giant Steps in 1960. It quickly became one of Coltrane's signature ballads.
The tune is renowned for its pedal-point design: the A sections sustain an Eb bass pedal while upper structures move through luminous major‑seventh/add9 harmonies; the bridge shifts to a Bb pedal. This non-functional but strongly centered harmony, combined with the tender melody, makes it a perennial bandstand ballad and a staple in modern jazz pedagogy.
Beyond Coltrane's definitive version, the piece has been widely interpreted across generations and instrumentations, and it remains a core entry in Real Book and educator repertoires.

form: 32-bar AABA: each A section is 8 bars in Eb major; the 8-bar bridge modulates up a major third to G major before returning to Eb for the final A. Common lead-sheet variants include backdoor ii-V motion and tritone substitutions in cadential bars; Garner's rubato intro is frequently emulated.
standard_key: Eb major (instrumental call key); also commonly C major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Erroll Garner composed Misty in 1954 as an instrumental; Johnny Burke soon supplied lyrics. Garner's own 1955 recording helped establish it, and the tune became a quintessential jazz ballad with its signature major-third modulation on the bridge.
Johnny Mathis's 1959 vocal version turned Misty into a popular hit and broadened its reach beyond jazz. The song's cultural profile rose further with its central role in Clint Eastwood's 1971 film “Play Misty for Me.” On bandstands, it is most often called in Eb major, with vocal performances frequently transposed to suit range.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). A sections in minor (commonly D minor) with a characteristic descending tonic line (Im-Im(maj7)-Im7-Im6) and chromatic dominant motion; bridge (B) tonicizes the parallel major before returning to minor for the final A. Many arrangements use the theatrical verse as an intro/outro.
standard_key: D minor (most common concert); also C minor in some bandstand/fake-book versions; vocal keys vary by range.
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote So In Love for the 1948 Broadway musical Kiss Me, Kate, where it is sung by the character Lilli Vanessi (originally Patricia Morison). Its dark-hued, minor-key lyricism and chromatic inner lines made it a favorite among jazz singers and instrumentalists alike.
Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Cole Porter Song Book helped cement the tune's status in the jazz repertoire, pairing Porter's sophisticated harmony with a definitive jazz phrasing approach. On the instrumental side, Art Pepper's 1979 rendition (and album of the same name) showcased the tune's depth as a modern jazz ballad vehicle, often stretching its harmonic colors in extended improvisations.

form: 16-bar (AA): two 8-bar phrases; not a 12-bar blues. Frequently features secondary-dominant motion (I-III7-VI7-II7-V7-I) and often a 4-bar tag or vamp added in performance.
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1928 by New Orleans-born Spencer Williams, Basin Street Blues pays homage to Basin Street, the thoroughfare bordering Storyville, the city's former red‑light district. The lyric evokes a nostalgic return to New Orleans and quickly became a signature of the traditional/Dixieland repertoire.
Louis Armstrong's 1928 recording helped canonize the tune and shaped its relaxed, vocal‑like phrasing for generations of players. Trombonist Jack Teagarden popularized a warmly crooned version that became closely associated with him, and the song has remained a staple for traditional jazz bands, often opening with a slow, hymn‑like intro before settling into a medium swing chorus.
Although bluesy in sound, the piece is not a 12‑bar blues; its 16‑bar chorus moves through a chain of dominants back to the tonic. It's commonly called in F in instrumental settings, with Eb also encountered, especially in renditions modeled on early New Orleans recordings.

tune_name: Softly, as in a Morning Sunrise
form: 32-bar AABA. A sections (8 bars each) in tonic minor with typical minor iiø-V cadences back to i; bridge (8 bars) commonly modulates to the relative major (Eb major in the key of C minor) via a cycle-of-fifths dominant sequence, returning to the final A in the tonic minor. Common variations include a minor line-cliché in the A sections and tritone substitutions on the bridge.
history_and_facts:
Composed for the 1928 Broadway operetta The New Moon, Sigmund Romberg and Oscar Hammerstein II's "Softly, as in a Morning Sunrise" quickly transcended its theatrical origins. Its minor tonality and clear AABA architecture made it an ideal vehicle for improvisers from the swing era onward.
By the 1950s-60s it had become a jam-session staple: Sonny Rollins's 1957 Village Vanguard performance, the Modern Jazz Quartet's concert renditions (notably in 1960), and John Coltrane's 1961 Village Vanguard version helped cement its status. While typically played as a medium or up-tempo swing in a minor key (often C minor), many bands alternate Latin feel on the A sections with swing on the bridge, highlighting its contrasting tonal areas.
famous_recordings: John Coltrane - 1961 (Live at the Village Vanguard)
Sonny Rollins - 1957 (A Night at the Village Vanguard)
Art Pepper - 1957 (Art Pepper Meets the Rhythm Section)

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Typically remains in the home major key; the bridge moves through a circle-of-fifths sequence via secondary dominants before resolving back to I. Many authoritative lead sheets note an optional introductory verse performed rubato before the chorus.
standard_key: F major (common instrumental call key); E-flat major (frequent vocal key); G major also encountered
history_and_facts:
Composed by George Gershwin with lyrics by Ira Gershwin for the RKO film A Damsel in Distress (1937), “A Foggy Day (in London Town)” was introduced on screen by Fred Astaire. It was one of George Gershwin's final popular songs before his death in July 1937 and quickly entered the American songbook.

The tune's 32-bar AABA chorus, often preceded by a theatrical verse, became a favored medium-tempo swing vehicle for both singers and instrumentalists. Frank Sinatra's 1954 Nelson Riddle-arranged version helped cement its modern crooner profile, while Ella Fitzgerald's and Oscar Peterson's 1959 Gershwin Songbook albums reinforced its status among jazz educators and performers.

On bandstands the song is commonly called in F for instrumentalists, with many vocalists opting for E-flat. Its bridge's chain of secondary dominants provides classic standards-era harmonic motion that improvisers exploit before the return to the final A section.

history_and_facts:
John Lewis wrote Django in 1954 as an elegy for guitarist Django Reinhardt, who had died the previous year. The Modern Jazz Quartet made the first and most definitive recordings, and the piece quickly became their signature tune.
The composition is renowned for its contrasting sections: a solemn, hymn-like opening and closing around a more mobile, swinging middle used for improvisation-an ideal showcase for the MJQ's chamber-jazz aesthetic. The Prestige album titled Django helped cement its status, and the group revisited it on numerous live recordings across the 1960s and 1970s.

composer: Gene de Paul (lyrics: Patricia Johnston, Don Raye)
form: 32-bar ABAC; 8 bars per section. A sections in the home major; B section commonly modulates to the relative minor (iiø-V-i motion); C returns to major with a turnaround. Bandstand convention often uses Latin feel on A sections and swings on the bridge/last A.
standard_key: G major (most common instrumental call); F major also common; Eb major often for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Written by Gene de Paul with lyrics by Patricia Johnston and Don Raye, the song was published in 1941 and introduced in the 1942 Abbott and Costello film Ride 'Em Cowboy, where it was performed by Dick Foran. Its sophisticated harmony and memorable melody quickly attracted bebop musicians, and it became a favored jam-session vehicle in the 1940s and 1950s.
Players often alternate Latin and swing feels, a performance practice that has become part of the tune's identity on bandstands. The form moves from a bright major key to a minor bridge before returning, offering rich ii-V motion and modulatory turns that challenge improvisers.
Among influential jazz interpretations are Miles Davis's Prestige-era reading (with a notable Dave Schildkraut solo) and virtuosic solo and small-group versions by Art Tatum; early 1950s tenor takes by Stan Getz also helped cement its place in the repertoire.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8); typically performed as a slow ballad; no standard modulation (arrangements may include brief tonicizations and turnarounds back to I).
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); C major, Bb major (common vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Hoagy Carmichael composed The Nearness of You in 1938 with lyrics by Ned Washington. The tune quickly entered the American songbook and became a favored ballad among jazz singers and instrumentalists for its direct melody and intimate lyric imagery.
Glenn Miller's 1940 recording helped popularize the song nationwide, and later readings by Ella Fitzgerald and Louis Armstrong cemented its standing as a jazz standard. The piece has remained in the active repertoire, resurfacing on landmark standards projects such as Willie Nelson's Stardust (1978) and reaching new audiences through Norah Jones's 2002 rendition.
On the bandstand it is typically called as a hushed ballad, often with rubato introduction before settling into tempo. The 32-bar AABA design supports lyrical phrasing and subtle reharmonizations without departing from the tune's gentle, romantic character.
chords: unknown

form: 12-bar blues; typically medium swing feel; often uses a quick IV in bar 2 and a I-VI-ii-V or V-IV-I-V turnaround; no bridge; occasional tag ending on recordings.
history_and_facts:
Centerpiece is a riff-based, medium-swing blues by trumpeter Harry “Sweets” Edison that later gained wide popularity after Jon Hendricks added lyrics. The tune's straightforward 12‑bar structure makes it a favored vehicle for blowing choruses, reflecting Edison's Basie-school roots in concise, bluesy phrasing.
Lambert, Hendricks & Ross helped cement the song's place in the vocal jazz repertoire with their 1959 recording, after which it was frequently billed as “My Centerpiece” in vocal settings. Joni Mitchell famously interpolated Hendricks's lyric into her 1975 track “Harry's House/Centerpiece,” bringing the tune to a broader audience beyond jazz circles.
Instrumentalists still call Centerpiece as a classic swing blues, while vocalists gravitate to Hendricks's witty lyric. The melody and changes are commonly treated flexibly on bandstands, with players inserting quick‑IV and classic turnarounds and sometimes tagging the last chorus.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Composed by Wayne Shorter and recorded on December 24, 1964 for his Blue Note album of the same name, Speak No Evil quickly became one of the defining post-bop tunes of the 1960s. The original session featured Shorter (tenor saxophone), Freddie Hubbard (trumpet), Herbie Hancock (piano), Ron Carter (bass), and Elvin Jones (drums).

The piece is celebrated for its enigmatic, modal-leaning harmony and haunting melodic contour-hallmarks of Shorter's mid-1960s writing. Its sophisticated yet singable line and non-functional harmonic motion have made it a staple in modern jazz curricula and on bandstands.

The album Speak No Evil is often cited among Shorter's greatest Blue Note releases and remains a touchstone of the era's advanced small-group writing and improvisation.

form: Wayne Shorter's "Speak No Evil" has a two-part form, alternating between a modal, vamp-like A section and a more harmonically dense B section. The A section is characterized by a repeating pattern of Cm7 and Dbmaj7 chords, creating a mysterious, floating feel. The B section introduces more chromatic and distant chords, such as dominant sevenths, to add tension and variety before returning to the A section. The tune's overall form is typically A-B-A-B-A', with the solo section following this same structure.

12-bar jazz blues; typically called as a riff-based head over a standard 12-bar blues chorus. Most common changes follow a bebop/jazz-blues scheme (often including quick IV in bar 2 and a VI-II-V-I turnaround); no bridge.
history_and_facts:
Sonny Clark wrote Cool Struttin' for the January 5, 1958 Blue Note session that produced his landmark album of the same name (BN 1588), featuring Art Farmer, Jackie McLean, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones. The title track's laid‑back, bluesy riff and crisp hard‑bop swagger made it Clark's signature tune.
The album's iconic Blue Note presentation-Francis Wolff's photography and Reid Miles' design-helped the track achieve enduring popularity, particularly in Japan, where both the album and tune became cult favorites and spurred numerous tributes. On bandstands, Cool Struttin' is commonly treated as an F jazz blues with bebop-inflected changes and a relaxed medium-to-up tempo groove.
chords: unknown

form: 12-bar minor blues
history_and_facts:
Wayne Shorter composed Footprints and first recorded it for his Blue Note album Adam's Apple in 1966. It soon became a major jazz standard.
The Miles Davis Quintet's version on Miles Smiles (released 1967) cemented its status, featuring a 12-bar minor blues framework with modal harmony and a signature 6/8-3/4 feel against a 4/4 overlay.
Commonly played in C minor, the tune's bass ostinato and metric ambiguity are hallmarks. Shorter later titled his 2002 live album Footprints Live! after the piece.

An African American spiritual also known by its refrain lines "Ain't Gonna Study War No More" and "Gonna Lay Down My Burden," Down by the Riverside predates the jazz era and entered the New Orleans brass band and traditional jazz repertory early in the 20th century. It remains a staple of parade and second-line literature and is frequently adapted for small-group trad jazz performance.

The song's flexible strophic structure and call‑and‑response character make it ideal for collective improvisation, and it is often performed with a joyous, gospel-inflected feel. Numerous jazz and gospel artists have recorded it, helping solidify its place in the overlapping spiritual and traditional-jazz canons.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section), typically preceded by a 16-bar verse; most instrumental performances omit the verse. No standard modulation between sections; some arrangements add a brief 2-4 bar tag on the final A.
standard_key: Eb major (common instrumental call key); also Db major and C major (frequent vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Herman Hupfeld wrote both words and music in 1931 for the Broadway revue Everybody's Welcome, where it was introduced by Frances Williams. Several versions charted in 1931, notably Rudy Vallée's.
The song became an enduring standard after Dooley Wilson's performance in the 1942 film Casablanca, where it functions as the film's emotional leitmotif. Wilson, a drummer and singer by trade, mimed the on-screen piano while a studio pianist accompanied off-camera. The famous line “Play it again, Sam” is a popular misquote; in the film the request is “Play it, Sam. Play ‘As Time Goes By.'”
On bandstands the 32-bar AABA chorus is most often performed without the rarely sung 16-bar verse. The tune's lyric-driven, nostalgic character and elegant Tin Pan Alley harmony have made it a staple ballad for vocalists and instrumentalists alike.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Willard Robison in 1938 with lyrics by Dedette Lee Hill, Old Folks began as a popular song and soon entered the jazz repertoire as a reflective, elegiac ballad. Early big-band recordings helped establish its profile before it was taken up by jazz singers and instrumentalists.
Miles Davis's 1961 recording further cemented the tune as a modern jazz standard, and the song has since remained a favored vehicle for expressive ballad playing. The lyric's intimate portrait of an elderly man reflects Robison's characteristic Americana storytelling, which has appealed to both vocalists and instrumentalists.
The song is included in major jazz fake books and standard lists, and is widely called on bandstands as a slow ballad.

history_and_facts:
Sam Rivers wrote Beatrice for his wife, Beatrice, and first recorded it for his Blue Note debut album Fuchsia Swing Song, cut in late 1964. The tune's tender, lyrical melody and subtly shifting harmonies helped it become his most widely played composition.

Joe Henderson's live Village Vanguard rendition in the mid-1980s (State of the Tenor) reintroduced the piece to a new generation and cemented its status as a modern ballad standard on bandstands and in conservatories.

Beatrice is commonly called in F major in instrumental settings; players value its spacious melody and elegant, modern harmonic motion as a vehicle for lyrical improvisation.

tune_name: I Fall in Love Too Easily
form: AB, 16 bars (8+8). Predominantly in E♭ major; the B section briefly tonicizes C minor via ii–V–i before returning to E♭. Many authoritative lead sheets show a common backdoor move in bars ~13–14 (e.g., D♭7(#11)→C7) before Fm7–B♭ and cadence to E♭.
history_and_facts:
Written by Jule Styne with lyrics by Sammy Cahn for the MGM film Anchors Aweigh, the song was introduced on screen by Frank Sinatra. Published in 1944, the film premiered in 1945.
The song was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Song. It became a jazz ballad staple, prized for its lyrical melody and introspective mood.
Commonly performed at slow tempos, it's unusually compact 16-bar architecture is noted by Cahn himself, who said he intentionally stopped the lyric early because “I had said all there was to say.” 

The tune became a lasting ballad vehicle in jazz: Chet Baker’s 1954 version helped cement its cool-jazz cachet; Bill Evans’ luminous 1962 reading on Moon Beams is a pianist’s touchstone; and Miles Davis adopted it in 1963, keeping it in his repertoire through the mid-’60s.
standard_key: E♭ major (most common instrumental call key)

composer: Dizzy Gillespie, Chano Pozo, Gil Fuller
form: Hybrid riff-based head with contrasting swing bridge; commonly arranged with Afro-Cuban A sections (often 8 bars each over a Bb mixolydian/dominant vamp) and a 16-bar swung bridge; total chorus length varies by arrangement (approximately 32-40 bars). Alternates Latin feel on A with swing on the bridge; 2-3 son clave underpinning is standard.
history_and_facts:
Co-written in 1947 by Dizzy Gillespie, Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo, and arranger Gil Fuller for Gillespie's big band, Manteca became a landmark of Afro-Cuban jazz. Pozo contributed the iconic guajeo/riff and conga patterns, Fuller shaped the big-band arrangement, and Gillespie integrated a bebop-influenced, swung bridge.

The piece helped mainstream the conga in U.S. big-band jazz and popularized the Latin-swing alternation that later became a template for Afro-Cuban jazz arrangements. Performances often include the shouted line "I'll never go back to Georgia," a chant associated with the band's experiences touring the Jim Crow South.

First recorded by Dizzy Gillespie and His Orchestra in 1947, Manteca quickly became one of Gillespie's signature works. Subsequent live versions with Chano Pozo in 1948 and later revivals-such as with Gillespie's United Nation Orchestra-cemented its status as a jazz standard and a touchstone of cross-cultural innovation.
famous_recordings: Dizzy Gillespie Big Band featuring Chano Pozo - 1948
Dizzy Gillespie and the United Nation Orchestra - 1989

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). A sections in minor (commonly D minor) featuring a descending chromatic bass line; bridge (B) typically moves to the parallel major (e.g., D major) or the relative major (F major) before returning to minor for the final A. Common jazz variations include bossa-nova feel, tritone substitutions on dominants, and occasional 2-bar tags.
standard_key: D minor (instrumental call); also E minor and G minor
history_and_facts:
Composed by Mexican pianist-composer Consuelo Velázquez, Bésame Mucho is widely reported to have been written in 1940; Velázquez often noted she wrote it before she had ever been kissed. The melody draws inspiration from Granados's piano piece Quejas, o la Maja y el Ruiseñor (from Goyescas). Sunny Skylar supplied the widely sung English lyric that helped the song circulate internationally in the mid-1940s.
In jazz, Bésame Mucho became a staple Latin bolero that players often render as a bossa nova. Its minor-key A sections and a bridge that brightens to major make it a friendly vehicle for reharmonization and lyrical improvisation. The tune has been recorded across eras-from hard-bop guitar treatments to contemporary vocal interpretations-cementing its status as a cross‑over standard in both Latin and mainstream jazz repertoires.

composer: Axel Stordahl and Paul Weston (lyrics: Sammy Cahn)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). No overall key change; bridge typically uses circle-of-fifths secondary dominants.
standard_key: F major; also common in Eb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Written in 1945 with music by Axel Stordahl and Paul Weston and lyrics by Sammy Cahn, Day by Day quickly became a postwar hit and entered the jazz repertoire. Its best-known early versions appeared in 1946, notably Frank Sinatra's recording (arranged by Stordahl) and Les Brown's version featuring Doris Day, along with a popular take by Jo Stafford.

The tune is a flexible medium-swing standard often rendered either at a relaxed tempo or as a ballad, and it's frequently called by vocalists and small jazz groups. A bit of trivia: Doris Day later titled a 1956 album “Day by Day,” which includes the song and helped cement its status among Great American Songbook staples.
famous_recordings: Les Brown and His Orchestra (vocal: Doris Day) - 1946

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8); typically in a minor key for A sections, with the bridge often moving toward the relative major before returning to minor. Some lead sheets include a brief tag/extension at the end.
standard_key: D minor; also commonly in C minor (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Co-written by Billie Holiday with her frequent collaborator Arthur Herzog Jr., Don't Explain was composed in 1944 during Holiday's Decca period. The lyric-reportedly sparked by Holiday confronting her husband's infidelity-became one of her most personal statements, reflected in lines like “Skip that lipstick, don't explain.”

Holiday's first 1944 recording established the song as a signature ballad, and she revisited it in 1956, further cementing its place in the repertoire. The tune has since become a touchstone for jazz vocalists, prized for its intimate, confessional lyric and minor-key melancholy; notable later interpreters include Abbey Lincoln and Nina Simone. On bandstands it's commonly performed as a very slow ballad, often opened rubato before settling into time.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Wayne Shorter and recorded on December 24, 1964 for his Blue Note album Speak No Evil (released 1966), Infant Eyes is a haunting, harmonically ambiguous ballad that has become a modern standard. The original features Shorter with Freddie Hubbard, Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, and Elvin Jones, and is celebrated for its lyrical melody and impressionistic harmony.

Shorter has stated the tune was inspired by and dedicated to his newborn daughter, lending the piece its intimate, reflective character. It is frequently studied and performed from The Real Book and other canonical lead-sheet collections.

A notable early cover is the 1971 Black Jazz Records album Infant Eyes by Doug Carn featuring Jean Carn, which introduced a vocal interpretation and helped further cement the tune's status in the repertoire.

composer: Cole Porter (lyrics: Cole Porter, Robert Fletcher)
form: AABA, 32 bars
history_and_facts: The song was originally written by Cole Porter in 1934, based on a poem by Robert Fletcher. It was intended for an unproduced musical film. The song gained immense popularity a decade later when it was featured in the 1944 film *Hollywood Canteen* and subsequently recorded by Bing Crosby and The Andrews Sisters. Their version became a massive hit, selling over a million copies and topping the charts for eight weeks. While not originally a jazz composition, its enduring popularity and straightforward AABA form led to countless jazz interpretations, cementing its status as a standard. Porter famously claimed it was his least favorite of his own compositions, despite its commercial success.
famous_recordings: Bing Crosby and The Andrews Sisters - 1944

form: 32-bar AABA (rhythm changes). Typically in B-flat: A sections use I-VI-II-V with common bebop passing dominants/substitutions; bridge cycles dominant chords (III7-VI7-II7-V7) returning to B-flat.
history_and_facts:
A bebop contrafact on Gershwin's I Got Rhythm, Dexterity encapsulates Parker's fleet, arpeggio-driven melodic language over rhythm changes. It is widely used as a vehicle for medium-up and up-tempo bop improvisation, demanding the ‘dexterity' suggested by its title.

The first recording was made by Charlie Parker's quintet for Dial in 1947, with Miles Davis, Duke Jordan, Tommy Potter, and Max Roach-an ensemble that defined much of the bebop small‑group sound. The tune remains a common jam-session call and appears in modern lead‑sheet collections and educator syllabi.

composer: Earle Hagen and Dick Rogers (lyrics: Dick Rogers)
form: AABA
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1939 by Earle Hagen and Dick Rogers while both were associated with the Ray Noble Orchestra, "Harlem Nocturne" quickly became an alto saxophone feature noted for its dark, noir-ish mood.
It was first recorded by Ray Noble in 1940 and later gained wide recognition as the theme for the TV series Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer (late 1950s), cementing its place in popular culture.
Typically performed in E minor and at ballad tempo, it is a staple of big band and small-group repertoires and is widely regarded as a jazz standard.

history_and_facts:
E.S.P. is the title track of Miles Davis's 1965 Columbia album that inaugurated the studio legacy of his Second Great Quintet (with Wayne Shorter, Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, and Tony Williams). Credited to Wayne Shorter, the tune encapsulates the group's telepathic, exploratory interplay-its title nodding to extra-sensory perception.

The composition is an archetypal mid-'60s post-bop vehicle: angular melody, fast-shifting harmonic colors, and an open, conversational rhythm-section concept. The original Davis/Shorter front-line reading remains the point of reference for later performances and study.

history_and_facts:
Bud Powell wrote and first recorded Dance of the Infidels in 1949 for Blue Note during a landmark session later issued on The Amazing Bud Powell. The date is historically notable for featuring a young Sonny Rollins and the brilliant trumpeter Fats Navarro alongside Powell.

The tune has since become a bebop staple among pianists and small groups, prized for its agile line and Powell's idiomatic bebop language. It remains a common study piece in modern jazz education and is frequently programmed on recordings and tributes to Powell's oeuvre.

composer: Axel Stordahl, Paul Weston (lyrics: Sammy Cahn)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Typically performed as a ballad; some vocal arrangements include an introductory verse and/or a brief tag coda. No inherent key change in the form.
standard_key: C (common instrumental call key); Eb (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Written in 1944 by the team of Sammy Cahn (lyrics) and Axel Stordahl/Paul Weston (music), I Should Care was introduced the following year in the MGM film Thrill of a Romance and almost immediately entered the American popular and jazz repertoire. Early pop versions by Frank Sinatra helped cement its profile beyond the screen.
The tune's straightforward AABA design and rich, adaptable harmony made it a favorite vehicle for jazz interpretation. Thelonious Monk's solo treatments in the late 1950s reframed the ballad with striking reharmonization and spacious phrasing, while later readings by Bill Evans explored its lyrical possibilities and inner-voice motion at slow tempos.
Today it remains a widely called ballad on bandstands and appears in major fake books and curricula, prized for its singable melody and room for harmonic color.

form: 12-bar blues; typically a sparse, straight-ahead blues progression emphasizing I-IV-V with common bebop turnaround options (I-VI7-II7-V7) in bar 11-12; no bridge; often performed at a medium tempo. Notable for minimal written melody and motivic development over the form.
history_and_facts:
Composed and first recorded by Sonny Rollins in 1956 on the landmark Prestige album Saxophone Colossus (recorded June 22, 1956). The track features Rollins with Tommy Flanagan, Doug Watkins, and Max Roach and is an extended performance that became a touchstone of post-bop blues playing.
Blue Seven is celebrated for its rigorous thematic (motivic) improvisation. Gunther Schuller's influential essay Sonny Rollins and the Challenge of Thematic Improvisation (published in The Jazz Review, 1958) used this track as a case study, helping cement its place in jazz pedagogy and discourse.
Though harmonically a straightforward 12-bar blues, the piece's enduring significance lies in Rollins's development of a small motif across the entire chorus structure, making it a model for thematic improvisation studied by generations of musicians.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically played as a standard 32-bar song form; the verse is usually omitted in jazz settings.
standard_key: F major (common instrumental call); also Eb major
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1928 Broadway musical Present Arms, You Took Advantage of Me quickly crossed from the stage to the popular and jazz repertoires. Its witty Hart lyric and buoyant Rodgers melody made it a hit almost immediately, spawning multiple successful recordings in 1928.
In the jazz canon, the tune is commonly treated as a medium-up swinger in a classic 32-bar AABA form. Vocal and instrumental versions have been staples for decades, with Ella Fitzgerald's Rodgers & Hart Song Book helping cement its status among postwar jazz musicians.

composer: Charles Trenet (lyrics: Charles Trenet; English lyrics: Jack Lawrence)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Typically performed in a major key. Many band and vocal arrangements add a key change (often up a half- or whole-step) for the final chorus and/or a short tag/vamp on the last A.
history_and_facts:
Charles Trenet wrote La Mer in 1943 (music and original French lyrics), reportedly completing much of it while traveling by train in wartime France; his own 1946 recording became a major French hit. In 1946 Jack Lawrence penned new English lyrics-Beyond the Sea-which are not a direct translation but a fresh romantic narrative set to Trenet's melody.

Bobby Darin's 1959 big-band swing version, arranged by Richard Wess, transformed the song into a durable American standard and remains the defining English-language rendition. The tune has since been embraced by jazz vocalists and bands, often at a medium-up swing tempo, and it is frequently arranged with a late-key modulation and a show-closing tag.

form: AABA, 32 bars: A sections (8 bars each) in Latin feel centered on minor tonic with a characteristic 2-bar stop-time break; B section (8 bars) swings through ii-V cycles before returning to the A; commonly preceded/followed by an 8-bar vamp intro/outro.
standard_key: D minor (concert); also common: C minor (instrumental/voice), Eb minor (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Dizzy Gillespie around 1941-42 and initially titled “Interlude,” the piece fused bebop harmony with Afro‑Cuban rhythmic ideas-an early and influential example of Latin-jazz cross‑pollination. Frank Paparelli received co‑credit in publication, though accounts suggest his role was primarily as a copyist/arranger for the lead sheet.
The first recordings appeared in 1944, notably Sarah Vaughan's “Interlude,” and the tune quickly became a bebop anthem. Charlie Parker's 1946 version produced the legendary alto break, a rite‑of‑passage lick for generations of improvisers. Art Blakey's 1960 Jazz Messengers rendition cemented it as a hard‑bop concert staple.
On bandstands, the A sections are typically played with an Afro‑Cuban or straight‑8ths feel and the bridge in swing, with the iconic stop‑time break and vamp intro/outro serving as signature performance features.

Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers - 1960

tune_name: Cast Your Fate to the Wind
history_and_facts:
Vince Guaraldi wrote this breezy instrumental for his 1962 album Jazz Impressions of Black Orpheus. Issued as the B‑side to "Samba de Orfeu," it became a surprise hit when DJs flipped the single; the tune went on to win the 1963 Grammy Award for Best Original Jazz Composition.
Its crossover success led to numerous covers, including a 1965 pop hit by Sounds Orchestral and a big‑band treatment by Quincy Jones. The tune's popularity also helped bring Guaraldi to the attention of TV producer Lee Mendelson, paving the way for Guaraldi's seminal music for the Peanuts specials.

composer: Sherman Edwards, Donald Meyer (lyrics: Elise Bretton)
form: AABA
history_and_facts:
Published in 1946 with words by Elise Bretton and music by Sherman Edwards and Donald Meyer, this song quickly entered the jazz repertoire as a tender ballad.
Widely performed by vocalists, Billie Holiday's 1958 rendition on Lady in Satin helped cement its status. It is commonly found in Real Book indexes and called at ballad tempos.
Typical performances are in a 32-bar AABA form, with singers often choosing keys to suit range.

tune_name: Gee Baby, Ain't I Good to You
form: 12-bar blues
history_and_facts:
Written by Don Redman with lyrics by Andy Razaf in 1929, the song was first recorded by McKinney's Cotton Pickers and quickly entered the jazz and pop repertoire. Its enduring appeal rests on a tender lyric set over a relaxed, bluesy groove.
Revived prominently by Nat King Cole in the 1940s, it has since been a staple for singers and small-group jazz settings, typically performed as a slow 12-bar blues in F.

form: 24-bar AAB (8-8-8). A sections: riff-based vamp on i (concert C minor). B section: brief move to IV (Fm7) and V (G7) before returning to i. Common variations simplify harmony in solos while retaining the 24-bar cycle.
history_and_facts:
Recorded for Blue Note on December 21, 1963 (Lee Morgan, Joe Henderson, Barry Harris, Bob Cranshaw, Billy Higgins) and released in 1964, The Sidewinder became a crossover hit whose funky boogaloo groove helped revive Blue Note's finances and introduced a wave of boogaloo-inflected hard bop on the label. The title track's success propelled the album to become one of Blue Note's best sellers.

The tune's head is a catchy riff over a vamp-based 24-bar form, typically called in C minor on bandstands. Its popularity extended beyond jazz circles, and a high-profile TV advertisement briefly used the track before the spot was withdrawn, a much-retold episode in Blue Note lore. The Sidewinder remains a go-to jam-session vehicle and a quintessential example of 1960s soul-jazz/boogaloo.
chords: unknown

composer: J. Fred Coots (lyrics: Sam M. Lewis)
form: AABA (32-bar)
history_and_facts:
A popular ballad published in 1934 by composer J. Fred Coots with lyrics by Sam M. Lewis, it quickly entered the American songbook and has since been covered by numerous jazz and pop artists. It is widely treated as a slow, lyrical standard in jazz settings.
Not to be confused with the 1970 pop song of the same title associated with the Carpenters, which was written by Fred Karlin, Robb Royer, and Jimmy Griffin.

form: 12-bar bebop blues in F (often called 'Bird Blues'). Typical progression: | Fmaj7 | Em7b5-A7 | Dm7-G7 | Cm7-F7 | Bb7 | Bbm7-Eb7 | Am7-D7 | Abm7-Db7 | Gm7-C7 | Gb7-F7 | turnaround varies |. Common turnarounds include: E7-A7-D7-G7 or Fmaj7-D7 | Gm7-C7 back to F. Key-center remains F with descending cycle-of-fifths ii-V motion and tritone substitutions; bar 1 commonly uses Fmaj7 rather than F7.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Blues for Alice is the archetype of the bebop or 'Bird' blues, compressing a chain of chromatic ii-V progressions into a 12-bar form. First recorded in 1951 during Parker's Verve period, it quickly became a benchmark for advanced blues harmony and a required study piece for modern improvisers.
Rather than a conventional I7-IV7-V7 layout, the tune starts on Fmaj7 and moves through a descending cycle of fifths, creating rapid tonicizations and voice-leading that epitomize bebop language. The head's compact, fluid line sits over these 'Bird changes,' which have since become a common harmonic template independent of the original melody.
Educators and players often refer to the underlying progression simply as 'Bird Blues,' and lead sheets in major real-book collections present it in F. The piece is frequently performed at medium-up to fast tempos and is a staple of jam sessions and bebop repertoire lists.
famous_recordings: Red Rodney Quintet with Dick Oatts - 1988 (One For Bird)

form: AABA
history_and_facts:
A popular song by composer Jack King with lyrics by Dorothy Parker, it entered the jazz repertoire and is widely treated as a ballad. Dorothy Parker wrote only a handful of song lyrics; this is among the best known in jazz circles.
The tune is commonly performed in a 32‑bar AABA form and appears in standard jazz fake books. It has been interpreted by both vocalists and instrumentalists since the mid‑20th century.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically stays in the home major; bridge departs via circle-of-fifths/secondary dominants before returning. Lead sheets vary slightly in dominant approach tones and turnaround choices.
standard_key: Eb major (common instrumental call key); also C major and Db major (frequent vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
George Gershwin sketched the melody in 1930; Ira Gershwin completed the lyric in 1937. The song was introduced on screen in The Goldwyn Follies (1938), released after George's death, where tenor Kenny Baker popularized it. Several 1938 pop and jazz recordings helped establish the tune quickly in the repertoire.
In jazz, Love Walked In became a favored medium-swing standard, appreciated for its singable AABA melody and straightforward harmonic motion with a bright, diatonic bridge. Swing-era band and small-group versions by Louis Armstrong helped cement its status, and it remained in circulation through later canonical treatments by Ella Fitzgerald and Oscar Peterson in their 1959 Gershwin projects.
On bandstands the tune is commonly called in Eb for instrumentalists, with vocalists often opting for C or Db depending on range. Arrangers frequently introduce reharmonized turnarounds or tritone substitutions in the bridge, but the underlying 32-bar Tin Pan Alley structure remains consistent.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Harmonically symmetrical and highly chromatic, with whole‑tone/tritone motion rather than functional key centers; commonly performed with Monk's 4‑bar drum-and-horns closing tag/coda used as a set theme.
history_and_facts:
Co-written by Thelonious Monk and drummer Kenny Clarke around 1941 during the formative Minton's Playhouse period, Epistrophy is one of bebop's earliest and most distinctive originals. Its title (a play on the rhetorical device “epistrophe”) hints at the tune's patterned repetitions and mirrored, whole‑tone symmetry.
Coleman Hawkins's 1944 recording, with Monk at the piano, is widely cited as the piece's first major document and helped introduce Monk's language to a broader audience. Monk recorded it many times thereafter, and it became his signature club theme-frequently used to close sets with a characteristic drum-and-horn tag. Early sources sometimes list the tune under the alternate title “Fly Right.”
Epistrophy's spiky intervals, chromatic voice-leading, and irregular accenting make it both a hallmark of Monk's compositional voice and a rite of passage tune for modern jazz players. Its persistent presence in live Monk recordings and later tributes (notably Steve Lacy's 1958 Monk program) cemented its status as a core jazz standard.

history_and_facts:
A lyrical ballad by tenor saxophonist-composer Benny Golson, Fair Weather emerged in the late 1950s hard-bop era and became associated with trumpeter Art Farmer.
The tune is performed as a reflective ballad and is counted among Golson's notable, less frequently recorded standards alongside his better-known works like Whisper Not and I Remember Clifford.

form: AABA, 32 bars in 3/4
history_and_facts:
Composed by Earl Zindars and introduced to the jazz world by Bill Evans, the piece became widely known as the title track of Evans's 1962 Riverside album "How My Heart Sings!" recorded with Chuck Israels and Paul Motian. It is typically performed as a lyrical jazz waltz and has become closely associated with Evans's repertoire.
Zindars, a close friend of Evans, contributed several originals that Evans popularized; this tune is among the most enduring. While often played instrumentally, it has circulated primarily in modern jazz settings and is a staple for pianists influenced by Evans's approach to touch and time in 3/4.

form: ABAC (32-bar)
history_and_facts:
Written by Bart Howard in 1954 as a waltz titled "In Other Words"; first recorded by Kaye Ballard that year. Peggy Lee helped popularize it and encouraged the publisher to adopt the more memorable opening line as the title.
The tune became a standard, often played as a bossa or medium swing in 4/4. Frank Sinatra's 1964 version with the Count Basie Orchestra became definitive and was famously associated with NASA's Apollo era; it was reportedly played during Apollo missions in 1969.
famous_recordings: Frank Sinatra with Count Basie (arr. Quincy Jones) - 1964

form: 32-bar AABA on rhythm changes in B-flat. A sections (8 bars each): I-VI-ii-V turnarounds with common bebop passing ii-V chains; bridge (8 bars): cycle of dominants (D7-G7-C7-F7). Last A often ends with a I-VI-ii-V tag; tritone substitutions are frequently used in modern lead sheets.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Bloomdido was introduced at the famous Bird and Diz session recorded June 1950 for Norman Granz (issued on Clef/Verve) with Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk, Curly Russell, and Buddy Rich. It opens the album and is one of the session's definitive bebop statements.
The tune is a classic bebop head on rhythm changes and appears in authoritative sources such as Parker's Omnibook and modern Real Book editions. On bandstands it is commonly called in B-flat, with players inserting characteristic bebop ii-V passing chords and occasional tritone substitutions on the bridge.
The session's unusual lineup-Monk's spare comping alongside Parker and Gillespie, with Buddy Rich on drums-adds a distinctive flavor to this reading, making the original cut a touchstone for studying bebop ensemble interplay on rhythm changes.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Richard Rodgers with lyrics by Lorenz Hart for the 1930 London musical Ever Green, “Dancing on the Ceiling” was introduced by Jessie Matthews. The song gained wider exposure when Matthews performed it again in the 1934 film adaptation, Evergreen, helping to cement it in the Great American Songbook.

The tune later entered the jazz repertoire through interpretations by leading vocalists and instrumentalists, with Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Rodgers & Hart Songbook further solidifying its status as a standard. Typical performances lean toward a graceful swing feel, though it also adapts well as a ballad.

Note: This song is unrelated to the 1986 pop hit of the same title by Lionel Richie.

form: 16-bar AABA; 4 bars per section; typically medium swing groove; no key changes. Common bandstand variations include an 8-bar intro vamp and a tagged ending/shout figure as in big-band arrangements.
history_and_facts:
Composed by tenor saxophonist Benny Golson and first recorded by the Art Farmer-Benny Golson Jazztet on Meet the Jazztet (Argo, 1960), Killer Joe quickly became one of Golson's signature tunes. Its relaxed medium-swing pulse, memorable two-note hook, and riff-based writing made it a favorite on bandstands and in jazz education.
Quincy Jones' 1969 big-band version on Walking in Space helped propel the tune into wider popular awareness, with a slick groove and catchy production that brought it beyond the jazz world. The piece has also been adapted with lyrics in vocalese settings-most notably by The Manhattan Transfer on their 1985 album Vocalese-further cementing its status in the repertoire.
Although harmonically simple and largely centered on the tonic, Killer Joe is frequently used to teach articulation, dynamics, and ensemble riff phrasing. Many charts include a vamp intro and a shout-chorus style tag inspired by big-band performances.

form: AABA, 32 bars total (8-bar sections). Common lead sheets show diatonic A sections with secondary dominants; bridge typically tonicizes the IV area before cycling back to I. Minor variations in turnarounds appear across Real Book and educator charts.
standard_key: C major; also Bb major and Ab major are common (instrumental call key most often C)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1933 Columbia film of the same name, Let's Fall in Love quickly entered the American popular songbook and, over time, the jazz canon. Harold Arlen's buoyant melody and Ted Koehler's playful lyric made it a natural for swing-era bands and later small-group jazz settings.
Ella Fitzgerald's 1961 Harold Arlen Song Book and Frank Sinatra's 1961 Ring-A-Ding-Ding! helped cement the tune as a modern standard, while Oscar Peterson's Arlen songbook (1959) gave it instrumental credibility among jazz improvisers. Diana Krall's 1999 recording reintroduced the song to a new generation, reinforcing its status on bandstands.
The title is often confused with Cole Porter's Let's Do It (Let's Fall in Love); despite the similar refrain, they are distinct songs. In jazz practice, the tune is most commonly called in C for instrumentals, with vocalists selecting keys to suit range.

form: 12-bar major blues. Typical I7-IV7-V7 changes; commonly played with a quick change to IV in bar 2 and a I-VI7-II7-V7 turnaround in bars 9-12. Riff-based head; no bridge; 12 bars per chorus, repeated.
history_and_facts:
Sonny Rollins introduced this tune on his landmark live album A Night at the Village Vanguard, recorded November 3, 1957. Framed as a spare, riff-driven head over a straightforward 12-bar blues, it quickly became a jam-session staple and a calling-card blues for Rollins.
The title is a play on words (“honeymoon for two” turned into “Sonnymoon”), and the piece showcases Rollins's penchant for developing motivic, conversational solos over simple structures. While many performances use basic blues harmony, bebop-oriented substitutions (quick IV in bar 2, II-V to IV, and a I-VI-II-V turnaround) are also widely heard.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections); often performed with the short intro and ending tag from the 1954 Brown/Roach arrangement
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1954, Joy Spring quickly became one of Clifford Brown's signature tunes alongside Daahoud. The first recording by the Clifford Brown-Max Roach Quintet (with Harold Land, Richie Powell, and George Morrow) helped cement it as a hard-bop staple, admired for its buoyant melody and harmonically rich, bop-informed changes. The title is widely reported to be Brown's affectionate nickname for his future wife, LaRue Anderson.

Jon Hendricks later wrote a celebrated vocalese lyric, and the piece entered the vocal jazz repertoire through performances by Hendricks and other singers. Today it's a common call on bandstands and in jazz education, prized for balancing lyrical, memorable lines with sophisticated harmony that challenges improvisers.

form: 32-bar AABA on rhythm changes (8-8-8-8). Typical bebop A sections use ‘Bird changes'/substitutions; the bridge cycles through dominants (e.g., III7-VI7-II7-V7 in B-flat). Common variations include tritone substitutions and passing ii-V's in the A sections depending on the edition/lead sheet.
history_and_facts:
A high-energy bebop contrafact on George Gershwin's I Got Rhythm, Wee-also circulated as Allen's Alley-is credited to drummer-composer Denzil Best. It became a popular jam-vehicle among late-1940s beboppers for up-tempo cutting sessions, with a line that sits idiomatically over rhythm-changes harmony.

Best, a key figure in the bebop era who also penned Move, wrote several linear, horn-friendly heads that entered the modern jazz canon. In lead sheets and bandstand practice, Wee is commonly called in B-flat and is treated at breakneck tempos, making it a benchmark test of rhythmic precision and articulation for improvisers.

tune_name: You Stepped Out of a Dream
standard_key: C major (instrumental call key); also Bb major (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Written by Nacio Herb Brown with lyrics by Gus Kahn, the song was introduced by Tony Martin in the MGM film Ziegfeld Girl (1941). The lavish number-long associated with Lana Turner's glamorous entrance-helped make the tune a popular standard in both the pop and jazz repertoires.
Jazz musicians adopted it for its lyrical melody and functional harmony that lends itself to reharmonization and medium-up swing tempos. Dexter Gordon's 1962 Blue Note version is a widely cited modern-jazz reading that helped cement the tune on bandstand set lists.
Although originally a film song, it has since become a common jam-session choice, typically called in concert C for instrumentals, with singers often opting for more comfortable keys.

form: 16-bar (nonstandard/through-composed)
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1959 and first recorded by John Coltrane for his Atlantic album sessions, it was issued on the 1960 album "Giant Steps." The tune's harmony introduced the now-famous "Coltrane changes," cycling through three key centers a major third apart (B, G, and Eb).
Its compact 16-bar form and rapid tempo made it a technical benchmark for improvisers. The original studio take featured Tommy Flanagan (piano), Paul Chambers (bass), and Art Taylor (drums); Flanagan's cautious solo became a noted example of the tune's difficulty.
Often performed in B major (concert), "Giant Steps" is widely regarded as a cornerstone modern jazz standard and a rite of passage for saxophonists and harmony students.

history_and_facts:
Written by British songwriter Billy Reid in the mid-1940s, the song soon acquired U.S. lyrics by Buddy Kaye and crossed over from British popular music into the American jazz repertoire. Early British recordings helped establish the tune before it was adopted by jazz singers and instrumentalists in the 1950s.
Among hard‑bop players, it became a favored lyrical ballad and is often called at medium or ballad tempos. The melody's clear AABA design and functional harmony make it a comfortable vehicle for song‑form improvisation, and it is commonly called in Eb for instrumental settings, with singers frequently opting for C.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). In a major key; bridge typically moves to the IV (often IVmaj to iv) and then cycles back to I via ii-V progressions. Common lead-sheet variations include back-cycling dominants and a brief 1-2 bar tag at the end of the last A.
standard_key: C major; Bb major; Eb major (common for vocalists)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1935 Broadway musical Jumbo, My Romance became one of the most enduring Rodgers & Hart ballads. Jumbo-produced by Billy Rose-was staged as a full-scale circus at New York's Hippodrome Theatre and is remembered for its spectacular production values and Jimmy Durante's famous comedic bit with an onstage elephant.

Jazz artists quickly adopted the song for its singable melody and harmonically rich AABA structure. Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Rodgers & Hart Song Book helped cement it in the jazz canon, and Bill Evans's 1961 Village Vanguard versions became touchstones for modern piano trio interpretations. While often performed as a lyrical ballad, it is also commonly called at a relaxed medium swing tempo on bandstands.

form: 48-bar AAB. Each section is 16 bars. A sections center in the tonic major with prominent chromatic root movement under a repeated-note melody; the B section features an extended dominant pedal (V of the parallel minor) before cadencing back to the tonic. Common modern charts keep the 48-bar form; arrangements may vary reharmonizations while preserving the AAB layout.
standard_key: C major (instrumental call key); also Eb major or F major (common vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote Night and Day for the 1932 Broadway musical Gay Divorce, where it was introduced by Fred Astaire; it later appeared in the 1934 film The Gay Divorcee. Musicologists often note its unusual refrain: a repeated-note melody over chromatic root motion and a striking dominant-pedal middle section-elements Porter reportedly conceived after hearing the chant of a Moroccan muezzin.
Sinatra's 1957 Nelson Riddle arrangement and Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Cole Porter Song Book helped cement the tune in the jazz canon, while Bill Evans' 1956 trio version showcased modern reharmonization possibilities. On bandstands today it's frequently called as a medium bossa nova, though classic swing interpretations remain common.

form: AABA, 36 bars total (8-8-8-12). Typically in a minor key; the bridge (B) often tonicizes or moves to the relative major (e.g., Eb major when in C minor). The final A is commonly extended to 12 bars with a tag/turnaround. Authoritative lead sheets note frequent reharmonizations on the cadences (e.g., backdoor II-V and tritone-sub dominant).
standard_key: C minor; B-flat minor (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1937 Broadway musical Babes in Arms, the song was introduced onstage by Mitzi Green, sung to the character Valentine (“Val”) LaMar. Rodgers's haunting minor-key melody and Hart's wry, affectionate lyric-praising imperfections-helped the tune transcend its show origins and become a cornerstone of the American songbook.

Jazz musicians embraced it especially in the 1950s: the Gerry Mulligan-Chet Baker Quartet recorded a defining cool‑jazz version early on, and Chet Baker's 1954 vocal helped cement its status. Miles Davis made it a dramatic concert centerpiece, notably at Lincoln Center on February 12, 1964 (issued on the album My Funny Valentine). The piece is most often called as a ballad in C minor on bandstands, with numerous harmonically rich reharmonizations now considered part of the tune's performing tradition.
famous_recordings: Gerry Mulligan Quartet with Chet Baker - 1952

composer: Duke Ellington (lyrics: Bill Katz, Bob Thiele, Ruth Roberts for 'Duke's Place')
form: 12-bar blues in C; no bridge. Head is a single 12-bar chorus repeated. Melody famously uses only two pitches (C and G). Common variants: quick IV in bar 2; bars 9-12 often use jazz-blues turnarounds (e.g., ii-V-I-VI or V in bar 12). No key changes.
history_and_facts:
Ellington's “C Jam Blues” became an instant jam-session staple after its 1942 debut, prized for a head built from just two notes that leaves maximum space for soloists and riff-based call-and-response. The original Ellington orchestra recordings-featuring star soloists like Ben Webster and Ray Nance-helped cement it as a vehicle for the band's personalities.
In the early 1960s, lyricists Bill Katz, Bob Thiele, and Ruth Roberts added words and the song was retitled “Duke's Place” for vocal use, memorably introduced on the 1961 Armstrong-Ellington sessions. Today it remains one of the most called blues heads in jazz education and on bandstands, almost always in concert C.

form: AABC, 32 bars, minor blues form with a bridge
history_and_facts: The tune is the title track from McCoy Tyner's debut album as a leader, recorded in January 1962. At the time, Tyner was a key member of the legendary John Coltrane Quartet. 'Inception' serves as a clear statement of his individual musical identity, separate from his work with Coltrane. The tune's harmonic structure is a minor blues form, but its distinctive modal feel, combined with Tyner's powerful use of quartal harmonies and pentatonic scales, established his signature sound that would be incredibly influential for generations of jazz pianists. The original recording features an iconic rhythm section of bassist Art Davis and drummer Elvin Jones, both of whom were crucial to the classic Coltrane quartet sound.
chords: unknown

form: 32-bar AABA; four 8-bar sections; no modulation within the chorus. The Basie chart is frequently performed with a soft dynamic and a 4-8 bar tag/coda on the final A.
standard_key: F major (concert) most common for instrumental/bandstand calls; C major also seen in small-group fake books; Eb major often used for vocal/lyric versions
history_and_facts:
Neal Hefti composed Li'l Darlin' for Count Basie's Atomic Mr. Basie sessions recorded in October 1957. Although Hefti originally conceived it at a brighter tempo, Basie took it at an exceptionally slow, relaxed swing, creating the iconic, whisper-soft big band ballad that became a Basie signature.
Jon Hendricks later wrote lyrics-commonly titled “Don't Dream of Anyone but Me”-which helped the tune cross into the vocalese repertoire via Lambert, Hendricks & Ross. The piece is renowned among band musicians for its extraordinary demands on blend, intonation, and control at very soft dynamics, with Freddie Green-style quarter-note guitar and sparse piano comping defining its feel.
The original Basie recording, part of the Grammy-winning Atomic Basie project, remains the definitive version. Small groups (notably the Oscar Peterson Trio) also helped cement its status as a widely called jazz standard.
chords: unknown

composer: Harold Arlen (lyrics: E.Y. Harburg, Billy Rose)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). Typically remains in one key; bridge uses cycle-of-fifths/secondary dominants. Commonly ends with a ii-V-I turnaround; some arrangements add an optional tag.
standard_key: G major; also C major (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Harold Arlen with lyrics by E.Y. Harburg and Billy Rose, the song first appeared (as “If You Believed in Me”) in the 1933 film Take a Chance after being written for the Broadway show The Great Magoo. Though it saw modest attention early on, it became firmly established during the 1940s swing era.
The Nat King Cole Trio's 1943 recording helped transform it into a jazz staple, followed by widely heard versions by Ella Fitzgerald and major big bands. Its buoyant melody and functional harmony made it a favorite for medium-swing settings on bandstands and in classrooms alike.
Players often keep it in one key throughout and favor a straightforward 32-bar AABA chorus with a ii-V-I tag. Vocalists frequently choose keys like C to suit range, while instrumentalists commonly call it in G.
famous_recordings: Ella Fitzgerald and The Delta Rhythm Boys - 1945

composer: Doc Daugherty / Ellis Reynolds (lyrics: Al J. Neiburg)
form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Common lead sheets include chromatic approach chords and turnaround tags; performances often add a rubato intro or coda.
history_and_facts:
Published in 1930 with music by Doc Daugherty and Ellis Reynolds and lyrics by Al J. Neiburg, I'm Confessin' (That I Love You) quickly entered the jazz repertory. Louis Armstrong's 1930 Okeh recording-featuring his intimate vocal and lyrical trumpet-was especially influential and helped establish the song as a standard.

The tune's 32-bar AABA design and plaintive melody make it a favored ballad for singers and horn players alike. It is frequently performed rubato into slow swing, and many arrangements add a tag ending. Armstrong revisited the number in 1956 on his Satchmo: A Musical Autobiography sessions, further cementing its place in the canon.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Commonly in Bb; bridge moves through dominant cycles; many lead sheets include a short tag/turnaround at the end of the final A, and an optional Monk-style intro/outro vamp.
history_and_facts:
Composed in the mid-1940s, I Mean You quickly became one of Thelonious Monk's signature bebop heads. The first widely noted recording was by Coleman Hawkins with Monk at the piano in 1946, helping to introduce Monk's writing to a broader audience at a time when bebop ideas were spreading from 52nd Street into the mainstream of jazz.
The tune's angular, intervallic melody and sly rhythmic displacements epitomize Monk's voice. While concert lead sheets usually place it in Bb and present a 32-bar AABA design, many bands use Monk's characteristic vamp as an intro or tag, and some charts spell out a brief two-bar turnaround at the end of the final A.
Early publisher credits sometimes listed Coleman Hawkins alongside Monk, reflecting the tune's initial association with Hawkins's band; modern discographies and scholarship, however, consistently attribute the composition to Monk.

Originally composed by Victor Herbert in 1919 as an instrumental, Indian Summer was given lyrics two decades later by Al Dubin (1939). Tommy Dorsey's 1939 recording turned it into a major popular hit and brought the piece firmly into the jazz repertoire, where it's most often treated as a romantic ballad.

The tune's late-Tin Pan Alley harmony and sweeping melodic line have appealed to both singers and instrumentalists over the decades. While widely performed in jazz settings, authoritative sources often reference both the 1919 instrumental origin and the 1939 lyrical version when documenting the standard's history.
