form: AABA, 64-bar refrain (16-bar sections). Typically preceded by an optional verse not used on bandstands. The refrain usually stays in the home key; many arrangements add a tag or occasionally modulate up (e.g., whole-step) on later choruses.
standard_key: F major; also common in Eb major (vocal) and Bb major
history_and_facts:
Irving Berlin wrote Cheek to Cheek for the 1935 RKO film Top Hat, where Fred Astaire introduced it in the iconic dance sequence with Ginger Rogers. The number quickly became a major hit and a cornerstone of the Great American Songbook.
On the bandstand, it's commonly treated as a medium-to-up swing with a 64‑bar AABA refrain. While Berlin also wrote an introductory verse, most jazz performances start directly with the refrain. The tune's memorable lyric hook, “Heaven, I'm in heaven,” and its buoyant swing feel made it a jam-session favorite.
A famous bit of lore from Top Hat recounts Ginger Rogers's feathered dress shedding plumes all over the set during the Cheek to Cheek sequence-Astaire jokingly nicknamed her “Feathers.” The song has remained a staple across generations, from Astaire's original to classic jazz treatments by Ella and Louis and modern revivals like Tony Bennett and Lady Gaga.

history_and_facts:
Bassist-composer Sam Jones introduced Del Sasser with the Cannonball Adderley Quintet in 1960, during the group's peak hard-bop period. The tune quickly became part of the small-group repertoire associated with the Adderley band's blend of soulful swing and streamlined bebop language.

Del Sasser is widely called on bandstands and in jazz programs alongside other Jones staples such as Unit 7. While the tune is firmly in the hard-bop idiom, its exact form and commonly called key vary across sources and editions, and reliable primary lead sheets should be consulted for performance details.

form: 32-bar AABA; each section 8 bars. Typically in concert Eb major; A sections center on Eb with a characteristic backdoor cadence (Abm7-Db7) resolving to Eb. Bridge uses a chain of secondary dominants/temporary tonicizations and returns via ii-V to the home key. Common endings include a short tag/coda.
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1936 RKO film Swing Time, where Fred Astaire introduced it on screen, “The Way You Look Tonight” won the Academy Award for Best Original Song that year. Kern's elegant melody and Dorothy Fields's tender lyric-reportedly moving her to tears the first time she heard it-quickly propelled the tune into the American songbook.
Jazz musicians adopted it as a lyrical ballad and medium-tempo swinger. Its AABA design, rich with backdoor cadences and chromatic inner lines, invites reharmonization while preserving a memorable melodic arc. The song has been widely recorded across eras, from Billie Holiday's early swing treatment to Oscar Peterson's Kern Songbook reading and polished renditions by Ella Fitzgerald and Frank Sinatra.
On bandstands it is most often called in Eb, though vocalists frequently choose other keys to suit range. The tune remains a wedding favorite and a staple of ballad sets, prized for its singable melody and sophisticated yet approachable harmony.

tune_name: I Got It Bad (and That Ain't Good)
form: AABA
standard_key: G major (instrumental call key); also Eb major and F major (common vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Duke Ellington with lyrics by Paul Francis Webster for the 1941 Los Angeles revue Jump for Joy. It was introduced by Ivie Anderson with Ellington's orchestra and quickly became a signature Ellington ballad.
A 32‑bar AABA ballad, it has been recorded extensively by both vocalists and instrumentalists; in Ellington's band it was often spotlighted by alto saxophonist Johnny Hodges. The song remains a staple jazz standard.
famous_recordings: Duke Ellington and His Orchestra feat. Ivie Anderson - 1941
Ella Fitzgerald with Duke Ellington Orchestra - 1957

form: AABA
history_and_facts:
A widely called bebop standard, first recorded by Miles Davis in 1954 for Prestige. The tune is most commonly played in Eb major and follows a 32‑bar AABA form.
Authorship has been disputed: Eddie “Cleanhead” Vinson later claimed to have written both Four and Tune Up, though many sources and lead sheets continue to credit Miles Davis.
Miles's 1964 live performance (issued on the album Four & More) helped cement the tune's status in the modern jazz repertoire.

form: Verse + 32-bar chorus (AABA). Typical performance begins with a 16-bar verse (“And now the purple dusk of twilight time”) played rubato, followed by a 32-bar AABA refrain (“Sometimes I wonder...”) at ballad tempo. Each A and the B (bridge) are 8 bars; no overall key change in the chorus, though the bridge commonly tonicizes through a circle-of-fifths sequence. Many jazz charts omit the verse or use it as an intro.
standard_key: C major (common instrumental call key); also: Db major (vocal), Bb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Hoagy Carmichael composed Stardust in 1927 as an instrumental (originally titled “Star Dust”); Mitchell Parish added the now-famous lyrics in 1929. The Isham Jones Orchestra's 1930 hit recording helped establish the tune's slower ballad reading and propelled it into widespread popularity.
Louis Armstrong's 1931 version further cemented the song in the jazz canon, and Artie Shaw's 1940 orchestral recording became one of the era's defining instrumental interpretations. Nat King Cole's 1957 recording with Gordon Jenkins introduced the standard to a new generation of listeners.
Stardust is among the most recorded American songs of the 20th century. On bandstands it's often performed with a rubato verse leading into the 32-bar AABA refrain; many instrumentalists call it in C, while vocal keys frequently gravitate to flat keys such as Db or Bb.

history_and_facts:
Canadian songwriter-pianist Ruth Lowe wrote this mournful ballad in 1939. The definitive recording by Tommy Dorsey and His Orchestra featuring a young Frank Sinatra with the Pied Pipers (1940) became a watershed moment: it was the first No. 1 on Billboard's national chart and stayed there for 12 weeks, launching Sinatra to national prominence.

Long associated with vocal stylists and big bands, the song remains a staple ballad in the Great American Songbook. Lowe later penned “Put Your Dreams Away (For Another Day),” which became Sinatra's long‑time closing theme-further linking her legacy to his career.
famous_recordings: Tommy Dorsey and His Orchestra featuring Frank Sinatra and The Pied Pipers - 1940

history_and_facts:
Wayne Shorter introduced Yes or No on his Blue Note album JuJu, recorded in 1964 at Van Gelder Studio with McCoy Tyner, Reggie Workman, and Elvin Jones. The tune quickly became part of the post-bop repertoire and is frequently included in modern jazz set lists and lead-sheet collections.

Shorter's writing on JuJu, including Yes or No, reflects the influence of John Coltrane's rhythm section and the harmonic/melodic explorations of the mid-1960s, while retaining Shorter's concise, motivic compositional voice.
chords: unknown

form: AABA, 32 bars total in F major. Each A section is 8 bars featuring rapid ii-V movement and bebop chromatic approach tones; the 8-bar bridge is a descending cycle of dominant sevenths by fifths (commonly notated as D7-G7-C7-F7-Bb7-Eb7-Ab7-Db7 with frequent tritone substitutions), returning via a ii-V to the final A. Common variations include substituted dominants and a tagged turnaround in the last 2 bars.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Confirmation is one of the quintessential bebop test pieces-an original, not a contrafact-written in the mid-1940s. Its melody is a model of bebop language, full of enclosures, chromatic approach tones, and lines that outline upper chord extensions, making it a staple for improvisation study.

The tune's bridge cycles dominant chords by fifths before resolving back to F, and the A sections feature swift ii-V motion that rewards careful voice-leading. Parker's recorded performances helped cement the piece in the repertoire; Bud Powell's versions, in particular, became touchstones for pianists, and later tributes (such as Sonny Stitt's Bird-focused recordings) kept it in active circulation on bandstands.

Confirmation is frequently included in major lead-sheet collections (Real Book and Omnibook) and educator syllabi, and it is often called as a benchmark bebop tempo and language study for jam sessions and juries.

tune_name: Nice Work If You Can Get It
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Chorus is the standard 32-bar form; bridge uses a circle-of-fifths sequence with secondary dominants but typically remains in the home key. Many vocal versions include an introductory verse before the chorus; instrumental performances usually start at the A of the chorus.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call), Eb major (also common; vocal settings vary by singer)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1937 RKO film A Damsel in Distress, this was one of George Gershwin's final songs; he died in July 1937, months before the movie's release. Fred Astaire introduced it on screen, and the tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire.
Billie Holiday's 1937 recording helped establish it as a jazz standard, and later interpretations by Ella Fitzgerald (in her 1959 Gershwin Songbook) and Sarah Vaughan cemented its status. The catchy title phrase and Ira Gershwin's witty lyric made it a favorite for both vocalists and instrumentalists.
The song has remained culturally prominent, lending its name to the 2012 Broadway musical “Nice Work If You Can Get It,” built around the Gershwin catalog.

form: 12-bar blues in 6/8; no key changes. Common concert-G scheme: | I7 (G7) | I7 | I7 | I7 | IV7 (C7) | IV7 | I7 | I7 | V7alt (D7#9) | bVI7 (Eb7) → V7 (D7) | I7 (G7) | V7alt (D7#9) turnaround |. Frequently includes a 4-bar vamp intro/outro on G7 and between choruses; many charts omit the Eb7 passing bar or use D7sus/alt in bars 9-12.
history_and_facts:
Composed and recorded by Miles Davis for the landmark album Kind of Blue, All Blues was tracked at Columbia's 30th Street Studio on April 22, 1959, with John Coltrane, Cannonball Adderley, Bill Evans, Paul Chambers, and Jimmy Cobb. Its rolling 6/8 ostinato, spacious harmonies, and modal treatment of a blues form made it a cornerstone example of modal jazz.
The piece's simplicity-largely Mixolydian on I and IV with a brief V7 area-opened wide improvisational space while retaining the blues' identity. A characteristic passing move, bVI7 to V7 in bar 10, appears in many lead sheets and performances. Oscar Brown Jr. later wrote lyrics, helping the tune enter vocal repertory as well.
All Blues remains a bandstand staple and a pedagogical favorite for exploring modal phrasing, 6/8 feel, and long-form motivic development within a blues framework.

composer: Antônio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Antônio Carlos Jobim)
history_and_facts:
Double Rainbow is the English title of Chovendo na Roseira, composed by Antônio Carlos Jobim. The piece first appeared on Jobim's 1970 CTI album Stone Flower, recorded at Van Gelder Studio with arrangements by Eumir Deodato. Its title in Portuguese literally means “Raining on the Rosebush,” and the English lyric version became known as Double Rainbow in English-language performances.

The tune's sophisticated harmony and gentle rhythmic feel have made it a favorite among jazz musicians exploring Jobim's post-1960 bossa nova and MPB period. Joe Henderson later honored Jobim with the Blue Note album Double Rainbow: The Music of Antonio Carlos Jobim (1995), helping to cement the piece's place in the modern jazz repertoire.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Originally an instrumental by pianist Eddie Heywood, “Canadian Sunset” became a major pop and easy‑listening hit in the mid‑1950s through a widely circulated orchestral recording with Hugo Winterhalter. Lyricist Norman Gimbel soon supplied words, leading to a successful vocal version by Andy Williams.
Over time the tune migrated into the jazz repertoire, particularly favored by guitar- and organ-led groups for its singable melody and accessible harmonic movement. Its crossover popularity keeps it in the mix for vintage pop/jazz sets and lounge-oriented programs.
Although born as a pop instrumental, it is now often treated as a medium swing feature, with many bands adapting the original groove to a straight-ahead feel.
chords: unknown

composer: Antônio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English lyrics: Ray Gilbert)
history_and_facts:
Written by Antônio Carlos Jobim with Portuguese lyrics by Vinícius de Moraes, the song is widely known in English as "Favela" with lyrics by Ray Gilbert. Jobim recorded it as an instrumental on his 1963 Verve album "The Composer of Desafinado, Plays" in Claus Ogerman's orchestral bossa nova style, helping establish it in the international repertoire under the English title.

Under its original title, "O Morro Não Tem Vez" (roughly, "The Hill Doesn't Get a Chance"), the song became associated with Rio's favelas and, after Brazil's 1964 military coup, was noted for its subtle protest undertones. It has since been recorded by leading Brazilian and jazz artists, and is commonly called on bandstands in both Portuguese and English titles.

form: AABA, 32 bars; tonal centers shift by major thirds across sections; common lead sheets show ii-V motion between distant key centers and alternate turnarounds on the last A.
history_and_facts:
Composed by pianist John Lewis, “Afternoon in Paris” quickly became a staple of the postwar modern jazz repertoire and remains a frequently called jam-session tune. The title reflects Lewis's deep connection to Europe in the late 1940s and 1950s.
Its harmonic design-moving through distant key centers via ii-V progressions and major-third relationships-offers a clear, elegant primer in modern tonal motion that predates later, more complex third-cycle explorations. The melody is spare and lyrical, a hallmark of Lewis's writing.
Defining recordings include Lewis's 1956 Paris collaboration with guitarist Sacha Distel and subsequent Modern Jazz Quartet versions, which cemented the tune's status on bandstands and in pedagogy.

form: 12-bar blues
history_and_facts: Lou Donaldson composed and first recorded 'Blues Walk' for his landmark 1958 album of the same name. The tune is a classic example of the burgeoning soul jazz movement of the late 1950s, combining a traditional 12-bar blues form with a funky, swinging groove and a memorable, singable melody. The original recording features an all-star lineup with trumpeter Herman Foster, organist Laymon Jackson, and drummer David Bailey, creating a relaxed, bluesy atmosphere. The tune's accessibility and infectious feel have made it a favorite for jam sessions and a foundational piece for students of jazz. While it is rooted in the blues, its sophisticated melodic phrasing and call-and-response patterns make it a prime vehicle for improvisation within the hard bop tradition.
chords: unknown

form: 32-bar AABA on “rhythm changes” (concert Bb). A sections: 8 bars each built on I-VI-ii-V with common bebop substitutions; bridge: 8 bars cycling dominants (D7-G7-C7-F7) returning to Bb. Variants include tritone substitutions and altered dominants; last A often ends with a I-VI-ii-V turnaround.
history_and_facts:
A flagship bebop contrafact on George Gershwin's I Got Rhythm, Anthropology was first recorded by Charlie Parker on November 26, 1945 for Savoy under the working title “Thriving on a Riff.” The tune was soon widely known as “Anthropology,” with composer credit commonly shared by Parker and Dizzy Gillespie.
Its head is a classic bebop unison line over rhythm changes in Bb, featuring characteristic chromatic approaches and rhythmic displacement. The bridge follows the standard dominant cycle (D7-G7-C7-F7) back to Bb, and players frequently employ bebop substitutions and turnarounds on the A sections.
Anthropology quickly became a jam‑session staple and a proving ground for bebop fluency. Early big‑band and small‑group versions by Gillespie and numerous live broadcasts by Parker helped cement its status in the repertoire; it remains a common call on bandstands and in pedagogy (e.g., Parker Omnibook) as a canonical rhythm‑changes head.
famous_recordings: Charlie Parker Quintet - 1945 (as “Thriving on a Riff”)

history_and_facts:
Composed by tenor saxophonist Eddie Harris in the mid-1960s; its first recording was by Harris and helped introduce the tune's funky, intervallic, riff-driven character to the jazz world. The piece quickly gained traction among modern players for its angular melody over a vamp-like foundation.
The Miles Davis Second Great Quintet popularized it with their version on Miles Smiles (recorded 1966, released 1967). Columbia later titled a Bootleg Series volume after the tune, reflecting its importance to that band's studio process. Vocalese lyrics were later written by Eddie Jefferson, further cementing the tune's status in the repertoire.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Charles Mingus wrote Nostalgia in Times Square in 1959, drawing on earthy R&B and blues grooves. It first appeared on his live album Jazz Portraits: Mingus in Wonderland (recorded in January 1959) and was associated with music he created around the same time for John Cassavetes' film Shadows. The tune's title tips its hat to the bustling midtown Manhattan scene of the 1940s-50s while the music channels Mingus's trademark mix of riff-based drive and soulful harmony.

Nostalgia in Times Square has since become a bandstand favorite and an entry point into Mingus's repertoire for many groups. It has been reinterpreted widely-from downtown New York's avant scene to modern organ trios-and was later adapted by Mingus's repertory ensembles; the Mingus Big Band even named a 1993 release after it. A vocal adaptation under the title Strollin' has also circulated, further broadening the tune's reach.

composer: Johnny Green (lyrics: Edward Heyman, Robert Sour, Frank Eyton)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). A sections in Db major; bridge commonly modulates up a semitone to D major before returning to Db via cycle-of-fifths motion. Lead sheets vary in A-section measures 5-8 (common tritone substitutions/altered dominants), but the overall AABA design is consistent.
standard_key: Db major; also commonly called in C major (vocal)
history_and_facts: Written in summer 1929 by Johnny Green at the request of British actress Gertrude Lawrence, who needed new material including a torch song. Lyrics were penned by Edward Heyman and Robert Sour, with Frank Eyton contributing additional verses for European performances. First recorded February 7, 1930 by Jack Hylton and His Orchestra. The song had already been recorded 22 times and was a hit in England before its Broadway premiere on October 15, 1930, in the revue "Three's a Crowd," where Libby Holman introduced it to American audiences. Louis Armstrong made the first jazz recording in October 1930. Coleman Hawkins' landmark October 11, 1939 recording—where he largely improvised over the chord changes rather than stating the melody—revolutionized jazz saxophone and was entered into the Library of Congress National Recording Registry in 2004. The tune has been covered more than 1,700 times and remains one of the most recorded standards in jazz history. Johnny Green was inducted into the Songwriters Hall of Fame in 1972.

chord_progression_notes: Classic 32-bar AABA ballad with sophisticated harmonic movement. A sections in Db major feature chromatic ii-V-I progressions and passing diminished chords (Edim7 in bar 4). The bridge (bars 17-24) contains one of jazz's most famous modulations: up a semitone to D major (bars 17-20), then to C major (bars 21-24), before returning to Db via chromatic descent (C7-B7-Bb7). Turnarounds vary between A sections—first and third A end Db6-Gb7-Fh-Bb7, while second A ends Em7-A7 to set up the D major modulation. Common substitutions include Coltrane changes over the bridge, tritone subs in bar 1 (Bb7b13), and reharmonizations of bars 5-8. The Gb7 in bar 3 is often voiced as Gb7#11. Advanced players frequently employ descending ii-V sequences through the bridge.

form: 32-bar AABA
history_and_facts:
Written in 1930 by Jimmy McHugh and Dorothy Fields, "Exactly Like You" comes from the Broadway show Lew Leslie's International Revue. It quickly crossed into the jazz repertoire and has remained a jam-session staple.
The tune is commonly played at a medium swing tempo, with a classic 32-bar AABA structure and functional harmony that encourages improvisation. It is often called in C major, though other keys are used for singers.

style: Latin (Afro‑Cuban feel on A sections; swing on bridge)
form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Typically centered in minor (commonly F minor) with no formal modulation; A sections are often played Latin, bridge swung; some bands add a short vamp/tag return to A.
history_and_facts:
Monk introduced Bye‑Ya in the early 1950s; the first recording dates to 1952 during his Prestige/Blue Note period, where the tune's hybrid of Latin pulse and Monk's angular harmony stood out among his early trio sides. The title is often read as a playful Monkism, and the composition became a vehicle for his characteristic rhythmic displacements and stark, percussive comping.
Monk revived Bye‑Ya throughout his Columbia years, notably on Monk's Dream (1962) and in concert settings with Charlie Rouse, helping cement it as a working-band staple. Performances typically render the A sections with a habanera/Latin feel before switching to swing on the bridge, a contrast that highlights Monk's interest in the “Spanish tinge.”
Educators and bandleaders often call Bye‑Ya in F minor, and it appears in major fake books and syllabi as a representative Monk Latin/AABA form. Its vamp-friendly character and clear sectional contrast make it a popular choice for arranging and for stretching out in live sets.
famous_recordings: Thelonious Monk Quartet (Live at the It Club) - 1964

form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Typically remains in the home key with no full-key modulation; bridge commonly tonicizes closely related areas (often IV and secondary dominants) before returning to A. Many arrangements add an outro/tag, but the core chorus is AABA.
standard_key: Bb major (instrumental); C major; Eb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Ray Noble wrote both words and music in 1934 for his British dance orchestra, and the song was introduced on record by vocalist Al Bowlly. Its lyrical directness and long, soaring melody quickly made it a favorite among singers and bandleaders on both sides of the Atlantic.

The tune has remained a staple Great American Songbook ballad, embraced by jazz musicians for its classic 32-bar AABA design and rich harmonic movement through the bridge. Nat King Cole's 1958 recording with Gordon Jenkins helped cement its mid‑century popularity and it continues to be called frequently on bandstands and in vocal sets.

Instrumentalists typically call it as a lyrical ballad, often in Bb, with occasional reharmonizations in the bridge and a tagged ending.
famous_recordings: Ray Noble & His Orchestra feat. Al Bowlly - 1934
chords: unknown

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Typically performed as a lyrical ballad; authoritative lead sheets present a straightforward AABA chorus with common modern reharmonizations (e.g., tritone substitutions) but no standard key change built into the form.
standard_key: Bb major (instrumental call key); also C major; vocalists often in Eb major
history_and_facts:
Composed by Victor Young with lyrics by Ned Washington for the 1949 film of the same name, My Foolish Heart was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Song. The film was adapted from J.D. Salinger's short story “Uncle Wiggily in Connecticut,” a production he famously disowned-ironically leaving the song as the most enduring artifact of the project.

Early popular recordings in 1949 helped establish the tune, but it became a core jazz ballad largely through Bill Evans, whose 1961 Village Vanguard performance is widely cited for its spacious phrasing and harmonic nuance. The standard has since been embraced by both pianists and vocalists, with notable later interpretations by the Tony Bennett/Bill Evans duo and the Keith Jarrett Standards Trio, further cementing its status on bandstands.

form: 16-bar AABA (4-4-4-4). Stays in concert Bb; harmony is based on Bob Carleton's 1918 “Ja-Da.” Common lead-sheet variants use II-V-I in the last 4 bars and occasional tritone-sub dominants in the turnaround; no key change.
history_and_facts:
Sonny Rollins wrote Doxy in 1954; its first recording was at the June 29, 1954 Prestige session led by Miles Davis, issued on the 10-inch LP “Miles Davis with Sonny Rollins” and later compiled on the 12-inch album “Bags' Groove.” The tune quickly became a jam-session staple thanks to its catchy melody and compact 16-bar form.

Doxy's harmony closely follows the earlier popular song Ja-Da (1918) by Bob Carleton, making it a contrafact-a new melody over pre-existing changes. Along with Airegin and Oleo from the same period, Doxy cemented Rollins's reputation for crafting durable, swinging heads that are ideal vehicles for improvisation.

Rollins revisited Doxy on later recordings (notably in extended live settings), and it remains a common first-call number on bandstands and in pedagogy for practicing 16-bar song forms and straightforward bebop-era turnarounds.

history_and_facts:
Pianist Bobby Timmons wrote Dat Dere in 1960 during his peak hard-bop period, shortly after penning other gospel-inflected hits like Moanin' and This Here. The tune was first popularized instrumentally by Art Blakey & The Jazz Messengers, with Timmons at the piano, and quickly entered the hard-bop repertoire.
Lyricist-singer Oscar Brown Jr. wrote words to the melody the same year, transforming it into Dat Dere Vocal-a witty, child's-eye narrative (“Hey daddy, what dat dere?”) that became a favorite among jazz vocalists. Brown's recording on Sin & Soul helped cement the song's identity as both a soulful instrumental and a clever vocal feature.
The piece has remained a bandstand staple, covered widely by instrumentalists and singers alike; later renditions such as Rickie Lee Jones's 1991 version introduced the lyric to new audiences, underscoring the tune's crossover appeal.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Original 16-bar verse often omitted in jazz settings. No permanent key change; bridge features secondary dominants/temporary tonicizations. Common variants include a 2-bar tag or turnaround ending.
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1924 by bandleader Isham Jones with lyrics by Gus Kahn, It Had to Be You quickly became a Tin Pan Alley hit through multiple recordings that year, notably Jones's own orchestra. The song's memorable, arching melody and lyric's wry resignation made it a natural for both dance bands and later jazz interpreters.
Billie Holiday's 1944 recording helped cement the tune's place in the jazz canon, and it has since been covered by countless singers and instrumentalists. The chorus is a classic 32-bar AABA form, often performed without the original verse in modern jazz contexts.
The standard enjoyed a major revival via Harry Connick Jr.'s 1989 version for the film When Harry Met Sally..., introducing it to a new generation and reaffirming its status as a Great American Songbook staple on both bandstands and bandbooks.

history_and_facts:
Composed for the Ziegfeld Follies of 1936, this song (also known as "I Can't Get Started (With You)") quickly entered the jazz repertoire. Vernon Duke wrote the music with lyrics by Ira Gershwin.
Bunny Berigan's 1937 recording became a smash hit and his signature piece, cementing the tune as a jazz standard and frequent trumpet feature. The song is typically performed as a ballad; many versions include a verse before the 32-bar refrain.

form: AABA (32 bars)
history_and_facts:
Wes Montgomery introduced Four on Six on his album The Incredible Jazz Guitar of Wes Montgomery (1960), and it quickly became one of his signature tunes. A widely celebrated live version appears on Smokin' at the Half Note (1965) with the Wynton Kelly Trio.
The piece is commonly regarded as a contrafact built largely on the chord changes to George Gershwin's Summertime, cast in a swinging hard-bop style. It is a staple of the modern jazz guitar repertoire.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Typically in C major: diatonic A sections with ii-V motion; bridge uses a cycle of dominants resolving back to V of I. Common lead sheets keep the original changes, with occasional tritone-sub dominants in the bridge.
history_and_facts:
Gerry Mulligan wrote Line for Lyons in 1952 during the height of his pianoless quartet with Chet Baker on the West Coast. The title is a wordplay tribute to San Francisco radio host and jazz impresario Jimmy Lyons, who later co-founded the Monterey Jazz Festival.

The tune became a signature of Mulligan's cool-jazz aesthetic: light, contrapuntal lines between baritone sax and trumpet, relaxed swing, and economical harmony. Its first recordings by the Gerry Mulligan Quartet helped cement the West Coast sound's emphasis on linear interplay rather than dense chordal accompaniment.

Mulligan revisited the piece frequently, including notable versions with Lee Konitz and in his 1957 reunion with Chet Baker. It remains a bandstand staple and a study in melodic counterpoint within a compact AABA form.

history_and_facts:
A jazz waltz by trombonist Frank Rosolino, Blue Daniel has become a bandstand favorite and appears in modern Real Book collections, helping cement its status as a post-bop standard. The title is widely understood as a playful nod to Johann Strauss II's The Blue Danube, reflecting its 3/4 feel.

Rosolino's own late-1950s recording introduced the piece to a wider audience, and the tune has since been adopted by many horn players for its lyrical melody and buoyant waltz groove. It remains a common call at jam sessions and in jazz education as an example of swing in 3/4.

history_and_facts:
Cedar Walton's Bolivia emerged in the mid-1970s and quickly became one of his signature modern hard-bop vehicles. The first widely noted recording was by Eastern Rebellion, Walton's all-star quartet with George Coleman, Sam Jones, and Billy Higgins, which helped establish the tune's bandstand profile. 

Bolivia is typically played in a minor key with an infectious ostinato and a Latin groove on the head, often opening up to swing during solos. Freddie Hubbard's fiery early-1980s live versions further cemented its status among improvisers. Today it is a staple at jam sessions and in conservatory curricula, prized for its driving vamp, clear minor tonality, and room for modal exploration.

tune_name: Guess I'll Hang My Tears Out to Dry
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Often preceded by a verse; the chorus typically remains in the home key without modulation. Some arrangements add a short tag at the end.
standard_key: E-flat major (common instrumental call key); also heard in F major and C major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Jule Styne with lyrics by Sammy Cahn in 1944, the song was written for the short-lived Broadway-bound musical Glad to See You, which closed out of town. Despite the show's fate, the torch ballad quickly found life on records and in the American songbook.
Frank Sinatra's 1946 recording helped establish the tune, and his 1958 reading on the album Only the Lonely is widely regarded as definitive among jazz singers and arrangers. He revisited it again in 1993 as a duet with Carly Simon, underscoring its enduring appeal.
On bandstands it's treated as a slow, lyrical ballad-often with the verse as a rubato prelude-valued for its classic AABA construction and expressive harmonic movement that invites nuanced phrasing.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Wayne Shorter and first recorded on the Blue Note album Speak No Evil at Van Gelder Studio on December 24, 1964, featuring Freddie Hubbard, Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, and Elvin Jones. It has since become a core post-bop standard frequently included in modern jazz repertoires and reference collections (e.g., the Real Book).

The title references the phrase from the fairy tale Jack and the Beanstalk, reflecting Shorter's penchant for evocative, whimsical titles paired with sophisticated, ambiguous harmony.

form: ABAC (32-bar), contrafact of "Just You, Just Me"
history_and_facts:
Monk's "Evidence" is a contrafact on the chord changes of the 1929 standard "Just You, Just Me." The title is a wordplay that traces from "Justice" to "Just Us," alluding to the source tune.
First recorded by Monk in 1948 for Blue Note, it became a staple of his repertoire and of modern jazz. The head features characteristic rhythmic displacement and syncopated hits that contrast with the relatively conventional underlying harmony.
It is widely played in F by instrumentalists, though the original standard "Just You, Just Me" is commonly performed in Bb.

form: 12-bar original (not a blues); three 4-bar phrases. Common lead sheets show a compact through-composed form with a return to the tonic in bars 11-12; measures 9-12 often vary slightly across authoritative editions.
history_and_facts:
Solar was first recorded by Miles Davis in 1954 and issued on the Prestige album Walkin', quickly entering the modern jazz repertoire. Its terse, angular melody and compact 12-bar design make it a favored vehicle for interactive small-group improvisation.

Authorship has been widely debated: guitarist Chuck Wayne maintained that Solar derives from his earlier composition Sonny (late 1940s). Musicologists and archival evidence have drawn strong parallels between the two, though U.S. copyright credits have typically listed Miles Davis. Most modern discographies and lead-sheet collections still credit Davis while noting the dispute.

Bill Evans's 1961 Village Vanguard performances helped cement the tune's canonical status among jazz musicians. Solar is most commonly called in concert C minor and is frequently reharmonized in the last four bars, leading to small but standard variants across respected lead-sheet sources.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Vinícius de Moraes; English: Jon Hendricks)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8); A sections in minor; bridge commonly moves to the relative major (e.g., A minor to C major or B minor to D major). Some Brazilian sources include a brief introductory verse in original recordings.
standard_key: A minor (concert); also B minor commonly used
history_and_facts:
A landmark Jobim-de Moraes collaboration, Chega de Saudade is widely regarded as the piece that launched the bossa nova era. The first recording appeared on Elizete Cardoso's 1958 album Canção do Amor Demais, with João Gilberto providing the now-iconic understated guitar pulse. Gilberto's own 1958 single (and his 1959 debut LP titled Chega de Saudade) then popularized the tune throughout Brazil and beyond.
The song's English lyric, No More Blues, by Jon Hendricks helped the piece enter the American jazz repertoire, where it became a favorite bossa nova standard among instrumentalists and vocalists alike. Jazz and educational lead sheets typically present a compact AABA, 32-bar form in a minor key with a bridge to the relative major, aligning with common bandstand practice.
Its gentle syncopation, elegant voice-leading, and lyrical melancholy exemplify the Jobim aesthetic. The tune has been recorded repeatedly by Brazilian pioneers and jazz greats, cementing its status as one of the core standards of the bossa nova canon.

form: 12-bar minor blues (AAB phrase design). Typically performed as a slow ballad; no modulation. Common variants include a 4-bar tag/turnaround or brief interlude between choruses in some Mingus/Big Band arrangements.
standard_key: F minor (concert); also heard in Eb minor and E minor on some arrangements/covers
history_and_facts:
Composed by Charles Mingus in 1959 as a memorial to tenor saxophonist Lester Young, who was known for wearing a pork pie hat. The piece first appeared on Mingus Ah Um (Columbia, 1959) and quickly became one of Mingus's most enduring compositions.
Mingus later revisited the tune (retitled Theme for Lester Young) on Mingus Mingus Mingus Mingus Mingus (1963), underscoring its importance in his repertoire. The song has since crossed stylistic boundaries: Jeff Beck's 1976 instrumental made it a fusion staple, and Joni Mitchell wrote and recorded celebrated lyrics (as Goodbye Pork Pie Hat) on her 1979 album Mingus, helping to introduce the piece to wider audiences.
On bandstands it is treated as a slow, mournful 12-bar minor blues, often with rich inner-voice movement and harmonized lines reflecting Mingus's gospel and blues roots. The title is a direct nod to Young's signature headwear and to Mingus's deep admiration for him.
chords: unknown

form: AABA, 32 bars in 3/4. Original Rodgers harmony is functional in E minor with a contrasting bridge; the widely played Coltrane arrangement simplifies to modal pedal vamps: A sections in E minor (often Dorian/Aeolian), bridge in E major (often Lydian), frequently with an extended vamp/tag.
standard_key: E minor (instrumental call); also D minor (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Richard Rodgers with lyrics by Oscar Hammerstein II for the 1959 Broadway musical The Sound of Music, My Favorite Things was introduced on stage by Mary Martin and quickly became one of the show's signature numbers. The 1965 film version, sung by Julie Andrews, cemented its popularity in the wider culture.
In jazz, the piece was transformed by John Coltrane's landmark 1960 recording (released 1961) on soprano saxophone, recasting the tune as a modal waltz built on pedal vamps and two tonal centers (E minor for the A sections, E major for the bridge). Coltrane's version became a signature for his quartet, spawning many live explorations and establishing the tune as a modern standard. Jazz performers often adopt his streamlined harmony and extend the closing vamp for solos.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Vernon Duke for the 1934 Broadway revue Thumbs Up!, Autumn in New York features both music and lyrics by Duke, who was born Vladimir Dukelsky and used his anglicized name for popular songs. Although the show itself was a modest success, the song endured, becoming one of the most beloved Great American Songbook ballads.

Its urban imagery and sophisticated harmony made it especially attractive to jazz musicians and singers. A 1949 hit recording by Frank Sinatra helped popularize the tune widely, and subsequent versions by Charlie Parker with strings and Billie Holiday cemented its status in the jazz canon. Today it remains a staple ballad on bandstands and in vocal repertoires, admired for its evocative portrait of New York and its elegant, chromatic writing.

form: 32-bar ABAC (8-bar sections). Commonly in F major; A sections outline diatonic motion with circle-of-fifths ii-V progressions back to tonic. B section uses brief tonicizations via ii-V sequences before returning to A/C material. Lead sheets often include a turnaround/tag in the final 2 bars; tritone substitutions on approach dominants are common performance variations.
standard_key: F major (instrumental call key); also Eb major and C major (common vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Henry Mancini with lyrics by Johnny Mercer for Blake Edwards' 1962 film of the same name, Days of Wine and Roses won the Academy Award for Best Original Song (ceremony in 1963). Mancini's single charted, and Andy Williams' hit vocal version helped propel the tune beyond the screen and into widespread popular and jazz repertoires.

Jazz musicians quickly adopted it for its singable melody and elegant harmonic movement. Notable early jazz recordings by Wes Montgomery and the Oscar Peterson Trio cemented it as a modern standard; Ella Fitzgerald's inclusion in her 1964 Johnny Mercer Song Book further solidified its status across the vocal canon.

The title phrase predates the film, coming from Ernest Dowson's 1896 poem “Vitae Summa Brevis,” a nod to the song's bittersweet lyric. On bandstands it's often played as a lyrical ballad or a gentle medium swing, frequently called in F for instrumentalists, with singers favoring keys like Eb or C.
famous_recordings: Frank Sinatra - 1964 (Days of Wine and Roses, Moon River and Other Academy Award Winners)

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). The bridge contrasts the A material and typically moves through a circle-of-fifths sequence before returning to the tonic; standard lead sheets often show secondary dominants and optional tritone-sub turnarounds.
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1936 film Swing Time, Pick Yourself Up was introduced on screen by Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers in a memorable dance-studio sequence where Astaire feigns clumsiness before launching into a dazzling routine. Dorothy Fields' lyric-“Pick yourself up, dust yourself off, start all over again”-became an enduring American idiom of resilience.

The tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire and remains a bandstand favorite for its buoyant, hopeful lyric and Kern's tightly crafted swing-era refrain. While Swing Time's The Way You Look Tonight won the year's Academy Award, Pick Yourself Up has had a parallel life as a jazz and popular standard, recorded by generations of singers and instrumentalists.

form: AABA, 32 bars
history_and_facts: Wayne Shorter composed 'El Gaucho' during a highly prolific and creative period in his career, while he was a key member of both the Miles Davis Quintet and a leader for Blue Note Records. The tune is the opening track on his 1966 album *Adam's Apple*, which is widely considered one of his finest recordings as a leader. The title, which translates to 'The Cowboy' in Spanish, reflects the song's subtle Latin feel, particularly in its rhythmic phrasing. The tune's harmonic progression is a complex but logical structure that has made it a popular study piece for students and a challenging vehicle for seasoned improvisers. Its status as a jazz standard is affirmed by its frequent performance by a wide range of artists and its inclusion in multiple 'Real Book' editions.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Typically in a major key; bridge moves through a cycle-of-fifths/secondary-dominant sequence before returning to the tonic. Many lead sheets add a brief 2-4 bar tag/coda on the final A in ballad performances.
standard_key: Eb major; also commonly called in F major
history_and_facts:
Written in 1940 by Jimmy Van Heusen and Johnny Burke, the song was introduced by Tommy Dorsey's orchestra with a young Frank Sinatra-one of his first notable ballad features-helping to establish Sinatra's national profile. Its gentle, romantic lyric (“a pug-nosed dream”) and flowing melody quickly made it a favorite across the big-band and pop markets.
Over time it became a core jazz ballad, prized for its lyrical A sections and harmonically rich bridge. Bill Evans's 1962 recording on Moon Beams (the album title nods to the song) is widely cited by musicians for its introspective reharmonizations and touch, further cementing the tune's standing on bandstands.
famous_recordings: Tommy Dorsey and His Orchestra featuring Frank Sinatra - 1940

tune_name: It Don't Mean a Thing (If It Ain't Got That Swing)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). Standard lead sheets keep the form in one key; the bridge typically moves through secondary dominants without a fixed modulation. Many arrangements add a short tag on the last A.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Duke Ellington in 1931 with lyrics by Irving Mills, this piece quickly became one of the earliest and most emblematic statements of the swing aesthetic. The first recording in 1932 by Ellington's orchestra featuring vocalist Ivie Anderson helped popularize both the tune and the very term “swing,” just ahead of the Swing Era.
A concise 32‑bar AABA design, it's often taken at a bright tempo and invites call‑and‑response figures and scat syllables (“doo‑wah, doo‑wah”), elements baked into Anderson's influential performance. The oft‑quoted title phrase has been widely attributed in jazz histories to Ellington trumpeter James “Bubber” Miley, underscoring the bandstand origins of the song's slogan.
The piece has remained a core standard for big bands and small groups alike, with numerous canonical renditions-particularly Ella Fitzgerald's later versions-cementing it as a perennial opener or encore that encapsulates the feel of swing.
famous_recordings: Duke Ellington and His Orchestra (feat. Ivie Anderson) - 1932

form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Typical in concert C: A sections emphasize a I-VI7-II7-V7 cycle (Cmaj7-A7-D7-G7) with secondary dominants; bridge continues circle-of-fifths dominants (E7-A7-D7-G7) resolving back to I. Common variations include tritone substitutions on the dominant chain and an extended turnaround/tag on the last A.
standard_key: C major (common instrumental call key); Ab major (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Blue Room was written by Richard Rodgers and Lorenz Hart for the 1926 Broadway musical The Girl Friend and quickly crossed into the popular and jazz repertoires. Early dance-band recordings made it a hit, helping to cement the tune's longevity among swing-era musicians.
Miles Davis's early-1950s Prestige sessions gave the song a modern small‑group reading that many jazz musicians reference, and Ella Fitzgerald's Rodgers & Hart Song Book brought it renewed attention among vocalists. Instrumentalists value the tune for its clean AABA design and circle‑of‑fifths motion, which invite creative dominant substitutions and turnarounds.
On bandstands it is commonly called in C for instrumental settings, while singers often favor Ab. Its straightforward harmonies and memorable melody make it a frequent choice for teaching secondary dominants and classic 32‑bar song form.

form: 12-bar blues
history_and_facts:
Composed by saxophonist Jimmy Heath, the tune was introduced on his album On the Trail (1964). It became widely known after Miles Davis's Second Great Quintet recorded it on Miles Smiles (recorded 1966).

A modern, riff-based 12-bar blues typically played in F, it features chromatic dominant movement and is a popular vehicle for hard-bop improvisation. It is commonly included in real-book collections and called at jam sessions.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8); A sections in tonic major; bridge typically tonicizes IV major and moves through circle-of-fifths ii-V progressions before resolving back to I; common jazz harmonizations employ substitute dominants and passing ii-V's.
history_and_facts:
Written for the short-lived 1938 Broadway musical Right This Way, I'll Be Seeing You was introduced on stage by Tamara Drasin. Although the show closed quickly, the song endured and became one of the most emblematic wartime ballads. Lyricist Irving Kahal died in 1942, just before the tune's massive popularity.
The song surged in 1944 with hit recordings by Bing Crosby and Billie Holiday, resonating deeply with listeners separated by World War II. It also served as the title song of the 1944 film I'll Be Seeing You, further cementing its place in American popular and jazz repertoires.
In jazz settings it is most often treated as a slow ballad in Eb, though vocalists commonly choose C. Its enduring appeal lies in the wistful lyric and a classic 32-bar AABA design that invites rich reharmonization while keeping the melody front and center.

form: 32-bar ABAC. A sections (8 bars each) in minor with an Afro‑Cuban/Latin feel; B section (8 bars) typically swings and moves through dominant cycles to the relative major before returning; C section (8 bars) resolves back to the tonic minor, often ending with a Latin tag. Common practice: Latin A sections with a swing bridge; some bands play the whole form in swing.
history_and_facts:
Horace Silver wrote Nica's Dream as a tribute to Baroness Pannonica de Koenigswarter (“Nica”), the famed jazz patron and confidante of artists such as Thelonious Monk and Charlie Parker. The tune quickly became a hard‑bop staple for its memorable minor‑key theme and characteristic blend of Latin groove and swing.
The breakout recording was by Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers in 1956, with a definitive arrangement that highlighted the Latin A sections against a swinging bridge. Its infectious vamp figures and strong minor sonority made it a favored vehicle for solos and for big‑band adaptations in the late 1950s and beyond.
Alongside Monk's Pannonica and Gigi Gryce's Nica's Tempo, Nica's Dream is one of the most enduring jazz tributes to the Baroness, and it remains a frequent bandstand call in small‑group and large‑ensemble settings.
famous_recordings: Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers - 1956

history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote It's All Right with Me for the 1953 Broadway musical Can-Can, where it quickly stood out for its urbane lyric and sophisticated harmonies. The song moved rapidly from the stage into the jazz repertoire and remains a favored vehicle for medium-up swing tempos and reharmonization. 

Ella Fitzgerald helped cement its status with her Cole Porter Song Book (1956), while Frank Sinatra's performance in the 1960 film version of Can-Can brought the tune to a wide popular audience. Instrumentalists, notably Oscar Peterson, have long used its chromatic motion and rich dominant cycles as a canvas for improvisation.

form: AABA, 32 bars total. Each section is 8 bars. Typical charts place the A sections in the tonic major and conclude with a dominant that tonicizes a key a major third above (I to III). The bridge briefly tonicizes that remote key before cycling back to the home key via sequential ii-V progressions. Common variations include tritone substitutions on the dominant leading to the remote key and alternate bridge harmonizations.
standard_key: G major (most common instrumental call key); F major (common alternate); Eb major (frequent vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1931 by Johnny Green with lyrics by Edward Heyman, Out of Nowhere became a major pop hit through Bing Crosby's recording and quickly entered the jazz repertory. Its striking harmonic device-an unexpected modulation by a major third-made it a favorite vehicle for improvisers from the swing era into bebop and beyond.
Coleman Hawkins and later Charlie Parker helped cement the tune's status as a jam-session staple, exploring its chromatic mediant motion and plentiful ii-V opportunities. The tune also inspired notable contrafacts, most famously Lennie Tristano's 317 East 32nd Street (1949), underscoring how its progression invited modernist reimagining while remaining a core standard on bandstands.

form: 32-bar AABA. Each section is 8 bars. A sections in the home major (commonly Eb), using rich secondary dominants and II-V movement (e.g., I - III7 - vi - II-V). The bridge tonicizes a series of remote keys via II-V's-typically moving through Ab, Gb, A, and C-before a II-V returns to the tonic. Common variants include tritone-sub II-V's in bars 5-8 of A and an I-VI-II-V turnaround/tag on the final A.
standard_key: Eb major; C major (vocal); Bb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote All of You for the Broadway musical Silk Stockings, introduced on stage in 1955 and later featured by Fred Astaire in the 1957 MGM film adaptation. Porter's lyric is a suave catalog of admiration, paired with harmonies that invite sophisticated jazz interpretation.
The tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire. Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Cole Porter Song Book helped cement its popularity, Bill Evans's 1961 Village Vanguard performances showcased its harmonic depth, and Miles Davis's 1964 concert recording turned it into a brisk modern-jazz vehicle. On bandstands it's typically called as a medium swing in Eb, prized for its chromatic A sections and modulating bridge that encourage creative reharmonization.

history_and_facts:
Composed by bassist Ron Carter, First Trip was first recorded by Herbie Hancock on the Blue Note album Speak Like a Child (1968), with Carter on bass. The tune has since entered the modern jazz repertoire.
It is often approached as a medium swing piece and is favored by small-group settings.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts: Wayne Shorter's Nefertiti entered the canon via the Miles Davis Quintet's 1967 Columbia sessions that became the album of the same name. The track is renowned for inverting standard jazz practice: the horns repeat the theme throughout while the rhythm section (Hancock, Carter, Williams) provides the improvisational motion underneath. The title references the ancient Egyptian queen, in keeping with Shorter's poetic, evocative titling during this period.  The piece quickly became a modern standard associated with Miles's Second Great Quintet and is widely studied for its haunting, motivic melody and modal/post-bop harmonic language. Tony Williams is often credited with proposing the arrangement concept of keeping the melody cycling while the rhythm section improvises, a hallmark of the original performance's mystique.

form: 32-bar AABA; 8 bars per section. A sections center on a minor tonic; bridge moves through a series of ii-V progressions and temporary key centers before cadencing back to the tonic minor. Common lead sheets show minor-key A sections with optional tritone substitutions in the turnaround.
standard_key: G minor; C minor also common (vocal keys vary by singer)
history_and_facts:
Benny Golson wrote Whisper Not in 1956 during his hard-bop period; it quickly entered the jazz repertoire after an early recording by Dizzy Gillespie's band and subsequent small-group versions. Its moody minor tonality and lyrical, singable line helped it spread rapidly among hard-bop ensembles.
Lyricist and critic Leonard Feather later supplied lyrics, and Ella Fitzgerald's 1966 Verve album titled “Whisper Not” helped establish the song as a vocal as well as instrumental standard. The tune's harmonic design-minor-key A sections contrasted with a bridge of cycling ii-V's-makes it a favorite vehicle for modern changes playing.
The piece remains one of Golson's most recorded compositions alongside I Remember Clifford, Along Came Betty, and Stablemates; decades later, the Keith Jarrett Trio named a 1999 live album after the tune, underscoring its enduring status in the canon.

composer: Benny Goodman, Lionel Hampton (lyrics: Sid Robin)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Benny Goodman and Lionel Hampton in 1939, reportedly conceived during air travel-hence the title. The first recording was by the Benny Goodman Sextet in 1939.
Lionel Hampton's 1942 Decca recording became a major hit and is famed for Illinois Jacquet's influential tenor sax solo, a precursor to R&B and rock-and-roll sax styles. Lyrics were later added by Sid Robin, and Ella Fitzgerald made a celebrated scat rendition in the mid-1940s.
The tune is a widely played swing-era standard, often performed at brisk tempos and built around memorable riff figures.

tune_name: Do You Know What It Means to Miss New Orleans
form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8); commonly performed as a slow ballad feel; no standard modulation indicated in authoritative lead sheets.
standard_key: F major (common instrumental call key); Eb major (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Louis Alter with lyrics by Eddie DeLange for the 1947 film New Orleans, the song was introduced on screen by Louis Armstrong and Billie Holiday. Its wistful lyric and hymn-like melody quickly made it a signature New Orleans anthem and a staple ballad in traditional and mainstream jazz repertoires.
Armstrong's 1947 recording helped cement the tune's status, and Holiday's version the same year gave it a definitive vocal reading. Since then, it has been a frequent choice for New Orleans ensembles and modern interpreters alike, often performed at a relaxed tempo with a two-feel or 12/8 lilt, showcasing trumpet or clarinet melody.
The song remains closely associated with the city's musical identity and is frequently called on bandstands as a reflective closer or feature ballad.
chords: unknown

form: 12-bar minor blues in C minor. Common lead-sheet changes: | Cm7 | Cm7 | Cm7 | Cm7 | Fm7 | Fm7 | Cm7 | Cm7 | Ab7 | G7 | Cm7 G7 | Cm7 G7 |. Variants often use iiø-V7alt to i in bars 9-10 (Dø-G7alt→Cm) and a quick V7 turnaround in bar 12; occasional tritone subs appear in modern charts.
history_and_facts:
Coltrane wrote this up-tempo minor blues in 1959 as a tribute to bassist Paul Chambers-hence the title, “Mr. P.C.” It was recorded during the Giant Steps sessions (with Tommy Flanagan, Paul Chambers, and Art Taylor) and released on the 1960 Atlantic album Giant Steps, where it quickly became a jam-session staple.
The tune's durability comes from its straightforward 12-bar minor blues form in C minor, typically driven at a brisk clip that challenges articulation and time. While many players use the simple blues changes, modern rhythm sections frequently color bars 9-10 with a iiø-V7alt and add aggressive V7 turnarounds, making it an ideal vehicle for extended solos-especially for bassists, in the spirit of its namesake.

history_and_facts:
Composed by bassist Steve Swallow in the 1960s, Falling Grace became one of his signature tunes and is widely included in modern Real Book editions, making it a recognized jazz standard. It was first popularized by Gary Burton's groups and has remained in the core repertoire of Burton/Swallow collaborators.

Often played as a medium-tempo jazz waltz, it is noted for elegant, non-functional harmonic motion and smooth voice-leading, which make it a favorite study piece for improvisers.

form: 16-bar AB (8+8). Typically in F major at medium swing with a gospel/second-line feel; common arrangement includes a 4-bar stop-time intro and an ending tag. Solos use the same 16-bar chorus; no key changes in the standard form.
history_and_facts:
Horace Silver's The Preacher became an early hard-bop anthem, celebrated for its churchy, gospel-inflected melody and backbeat feel. Blue Note co-founder Alfred Lion reportedly hesitated to record it, but Silver insisted; the tune then became a jukebox hit and a calling card for the nascent Jazz Messengers.
First recorded in 1955 by Horace Silver with Kenny Dorham, Hank Mobley, Doug Watkins, and Art Blakey, it helped define the Silver/Blakey aesthetic-earthy grooves, singable riffs, and bluesy harmonies. The tune remains a bandstand favorite for its simple, crowd-pleasing head, stop-time figures, and accessible 16-bar solo form.
famous_recordings: Horace Silver and the Jazz Messengers - 1955
chords: unknown

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Antonio Carlos Jobim)
form: Through-composed; typically realized as two 16-bar verses (no bridge), often repeated with an optional final modulation up a semitone; lyric-driven chain of 2-bar motifs with common tag ending. Exact bar counts and modulations vary by arrangement and lead sheet.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antônio Carlos Jobim in 1972, Águas de Março is one of his most celebrated songs; Jobim wrote both the original Portuguese lyric and his own English adaptation. The title refers to the torrential March rains in Brazil that signal the end of summer, and the text is built as a cascading list of images that mirrors the song's forward, flowing motion.
A key early recording was Elis Regina's 1972 version, followed by Jobim's own 1973 take. The definitive and most widely cited rendition is the 1974 Elis & Tom duet, recorded in Los Angeles, which cemented the tune's global stature and is frequently referenced by educators and discographies as the standard.
Though widely performed by jazz and Brazilian artists alike, the song's structure is not the usual AABA or blues; it is essentially through-composed, commonly presented as two 16-bar verses with repeats and, in some arrangements, a final half-step lift. The English lyric subtly reframes the seasonal imagery for northern-hemisphere listeners while preserving the song's hypnotic, mantra-like flow.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Composed by Harry Warren with lyrics by Mack Gordon for the 20th Century-Fox film Diamond Horseshoe (1945), where it was introduced by Dick Haymes. The song quickly entered the American songbook and has been favored as a lyrical ballad by jazz musicians.
John Coltrane's reading on his Ballads album (recorded in 1962, released 1963) helped cement the tune's modern-jazz status. Not to be confused with Billy Taylor's later civil-rights era song I Wish I Knew How It Would Feel to Be Free, this Warren/Gordon number is a classic Tin Pan Alley ballad that continues to circulate on bandstands and in ballad medleys.

history_and_facts:
Composed in 1925 by four musicians associated with the Chicago dance‑band scene, Bye Bye Blues became a durable jazz and popular tune, especially favored in traditional and Dixieland repertoires. Its cheerful, easily singable chorus and straightforward harmony made it a jam‑session staple.

The song enjoyed a major revival in the early 1950s through hit pop/jazz interpretations (most notably the 1952 Les Paul & Mary Ford recording), which helped cement its long‑term visibility with both audiences and working bands.

history_and_facts:
Written by Arthur Schwartz with lyrics by Howard Dietz for the 1937 Broadway revue Between the Devil, By Myself is a reflective torch song about self-reliance after heartbreak. The tune later gained renewed prominence when Fred Astaire sang it in MGM's 1953 film The Band Wagon, which helped cement it in the American Songbook.

Often performed as a slow ballad by jazz-influenced vocalists, the song's sophisticated Dietz & Schwartz craft-lyrical introspection paired with rich harmony-has kept it in circulation. Judy Garland's 1957 recording further underscored its status as a definitive torch standard.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section); typically no full-key modulation. Characteristic harmonic color includes a borrowed iv (minor) in the A sections; often performed either as a rubato/straight ballad or at a gentle bossa feel.
standard_key: C major; also Bb major, Eb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Johnny Mandel with lyrics by Paul Francis Webster for the 1965 film The Sandpiper, “The Shadow of Your Smile” quickly transcended its soundtrack origins to become a core ballad in the jazz repertoire. The tune won the 1966 Academy Award for Best Original Song and earned major Grammy recognition, helping cement its rapid adoption by both vocalists and instrumentalists.

Tony Bennett's 1966 version (arranged by Mandel) became one of the defining interpretations, while Astrud Gilberto popularized a gentle bossa-nova reading the same year the film appeared. Wes Montgomery's 1965 orchestral take and Stan Getz's 1966 recording further established the song's place among modern standards. In the film score, the melody is featured prominently on trumpet, famously associated with Jack Sheldon.

Musicians value the song's lyrical contour and Mandel's sophisticated harmony-especially the expressive iv-minor color in the A sections. On bandstands it's commonly called in C for instrumental settings, though singers frequently select Bb or Eb to suit range.

form: 32-bar AABA; 8 bars per section. Commonly arranged with a short pickup and an optional 2-4 bar tag; bridge typically uses circle-of-fifths dominants to return to I.
standard_key: F major (common instrumental call key); Eb major (vocal); Bb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1926 by Fred Rose with lyrics by Walter Hirsch, Deed I Do became a popular Tin Pan Alley hit that quickly entered the jazz repertoire. Early recordings by Ruth Etting and Ben Bernie helped establish it, and its singable 32-bar AABA design made it a natural for swing-era bands and later small-group jazz.

The tune's harmony is a classic cycle-of-fifths progression with a buoyant, blues-tinged melody, lending itself to medium swing tempos and extended tags. Though Rose later became a foundational figure in country music publishing (co-founding Acuff-Rose), Deed I Do stands as one of his most enduring contributions to the jazz songbook.

It remains a favored vehicle for both instrumentalists and vocalists, often called in F for jam sessions, with singers choosing keys to suit range.

history_and_facts:
Also known as "She's Funny That Way (I Got a Woman Crazy for Me)", this Tin Pan Alley ballad features music by Neil Moret (pseudonym of Charles N. Daniels) and lyrics by Richard A. Whiting.
The song became a standard through numerous 1929 hit recordings and was later immortalized in jazz by Billie Holiday's 1937 version, which popularized the gender-flipped title "He's Funny That Way."
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Also known as "Budo," this Bud Powell bebop line was first recorded by the Miles Davis Nonet during the Birth of the Cool sessions. On some releases it is co-credited to Miles Davis, but the composition is widely recognized as Powell's.
The tune is included in major jazz fake books under the title "Hallucinations (aka Budo)" and is a recognized bebop standard, though its most famous recording is the arranged nonet version.
Note: Do not confuse this piece with Powell's different composition "Glass Enclosure," which has also been referred to as "Hallucinations" on certain releases.

form: AABA, 32 bars total (8-bar sections). Typically centered in Db major; the bridge moves through distant tonal areas via planing dominant sonorities before returning to the tonic. Many Ellington/Webster treatments add a brief tag/coda on the final A.
standard_key: Db major (most common); C major (also used)
history_and_facts:
Billy Strayhorn wrote Chelsea Bridge in 1941 for the Duke Ellington orchestra, and the band introduced it on record the same year. The work is often described as a moody, impressionistic tone poem, notable for its parallel dominant sonorities and lush, Debussy/Ravel-influenced colors.
A widely cited anecdote holds that Strayhorn was inspired by James McNeill Whistler's nocturnes and believed the painting he admired depicted Chelsea Bridge; in fact, it was Old Battersea Bridge, but the misnomer stuck. Ben Webster's luxuriant ballad readings-both with Ellington and later on his own-helped make the piece a canonical jazz ballad and a staple of band books and education materials.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Common practice includes an optional rubato verse/intro from the musical and a short tag on the last A; no standard key change in the chorus.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Jerome Kern with lyrics by Oscar Hammerstein II for the 1932 Broadway musical Music in the Air, The Song Is You quickly outlived its stage origins to become a favored jazz vehicle. Although prominent in the stage show, it was not featured as a full vocal set piece in the 1934 film adaptation, a quirk often noted in histories of the tune.

Its buoyant melody and forward-driving chain of secondary dominants have made it a popular up-tempo closer on bandstands. The song's standing in the jazz canon was cemented by mid-century recordings from major vocalists and, later, by modern-trio performances that highlighted its harmonic clarity and momentum.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). Typically remains in the home key with brief tonicizations and secondary dominants in the bridge; common lead sheets present it as a straight 32-bar ballad with optional tag ending.
standard_key: Eb major; also common: F major (vocal), Ab major
history_and_facts:
Composed by Richard Rodgers with lyrics by Lorenz Hart for the 1940 Broadway musical Higher and Higher, the song outlived its modest show origins to become one of the most enduring Rodgers & Hart ballads in the jazz repertoire.
Frank Sinatra's 1955 reading on In the Wee Small Hours helped reintroduce the tune to a postwar audience and cemented its identity as a late-night torch song. Miles Davis's spacious, muted-trumpet version with the Prestige quintet (recorded 1956) made it a staple instrumental ballad for modern jazz players, emphasizing the tune's chromatic inner lines and lyrical economy.
Chet Baker's intimate vocal approach and Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 Rodgers & Hart Song Book further solidified the standard's status on bandstands and in classrooms. The tune is frequently performed rubato into tempo and prized for its elegantly voiced AABA design and emotionally self-reflective lyric.

form: 32-bar AABA. A sections (8 bars each) use harmony derived from Honeysuckle Rose in F; bridge (8 bars) uses Rhythm Changes dominants (A7-D7-G7-C7) resolving to F. Common bebop variations include added passing dominants, tritone substitutions, and ii-V turnarounds back to F at the end of each A.
history_and_facts:
Charlie Parker's Scrapple from the Apple is a classic bebop contrafact from 1947, combining the A-section harmony of Honeysuckle Rose with a Rhythm Changes bridge. It quickly became a jam-session staple for its tight 32-bar AABA form and its inviting, singable head paired with brisk bebop language.

The first recording was made in 1947 by Parker's small group, helping cement the tune in the bebop canon. The title is a playful pun-“scrapple” (a dish assembled from scraps) nods to the contrafact approach, while “the Apple” points to New York City.

Instrumentalists commonly call it in F concert; lead sheets in major anthologies and educator resources reflect that practice. Its hybrid harmonic design makes it a perennial study vehicle for bebop vocabulary, dominant-cycle navigation, and tritone-sub usage.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Commonly performed in 3/4 (waltz feel); some 4/4 ballad interpretations exist.
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1943 by guitarist Karl Suessdorf with lyrics by John Blackburn, Moonlight in Vermont quickly became a pop and jazz staple after Margaret Whiting's hit 1944 recording with Billy Butterfield's orchestra. The tune's reflective, pastoral imagery made it an enduring standard for vocalists and instrumentalists alike.

The lyric is famous for being unrhymed and structured in haiku-like 5-7-5 syllabic stanzas in each A section-highly unusual for an American popular song of the era. Jazz players often render it as a slow waltz, though 4/4 ballad versions are also common.

Johnny Smith's 1952 quintet recording (with a breakout solo by Stan Getz) cemented the song's place in the jazz canon, and later versions by Ella & Louis (1956) and Frank Sinatra (1958) further solidified its status. The song is widely regarded as an unofficial musical emblem of Vermont and remains a frequent ballad call on bandstands.

form: 32-bar AABA (8 bars per section). Often preceded by a rarely-performed 16-bar verse in vocal renditions; many arrangements add a 2-4 bar tag at the end. No inherent key change within the refrain.
standard_key: C major (instrumental call key); Ab major (common vocal/original film key)
history_and_facts:
Harold Arlen and E.Y. “Yip” Harburg wrote Over the Rainbow for MGM's The Wizard of Oz (1939), where Judy Garland's performance indelibly linked the song to her career. It won the Academy Award for Best Original Song in 1940 and has topped numerous all-time song lists, including the American Film Institute's ranking of movie songs. The tune's iconic opening octave leap makes it a favorite example for interval training among musicians.

Jazz artists embraced it early: Art Tatum's virtuosic solo versions helped codify it as a jazz vehicle, and it became a staple for ballad interpretation by pianists and singers alike. Ella Fitzgerald and Oscar Peterson both recorded definitive versions in their Harold Arlen songbook projects, and Keith Jarrett has often used it as a lyrical encore, underscoring its enduring place in the jazz repertoire.

The refrain most commonly performed by jazz musicians is a 32-bar AABA form; the original film version includes a preceding verse (intro) that is frequently omitted in instrumental settings.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Commonly performed as a ballad with a 2-4 bar tag on the final A; no sectional key change in standard lead sheets.
history_and_facts:
Published in 1938, this Coots-Gillespie collaboration quickly became a musicians' ballad for its sophisticated, chromatic melody and lush harmonic movement-traits often noted by American popular song scholars. Billie Holiday's 1938 recording helped cement its status in the jazz canon, and it soon became a favored vehicle for interpreters who prize lyrical nuance and subtle harmonic shading.
Frank Sinatra's 1945 Columbia single (with Axel Stordahl) brought the tune wider popular attention, while Charlie Parker's 1950 “with Strings” version showcased its flexibility for modern jazz phrasing and linear improvisation. Louis Armstrong's intimate 1957 reading with Oscar Peterson further reinforced its place as a timeless ballad.
The lyric's cocktail imagery and urbane metaphors mirror the tune's harmonic sophistication, making it a perennial choice on bandstands and in vocal repertoires. It remains a standard in Real Book-type sources and jazz education syllabi.

history_and_facts:
A contemplative Ellington ballad from 1937 with lyrics by Irving Mills, Azure belongs to the composer's series of color-evoking mood pieces. Its languid melody and rich, chromatic harmonies make it a favorite among interpreters of Ellington's more impressionistic writing.

The Ellington Orchestra's 1937 recording established the piece, and Ella Fitzgerald's treatment in her Duke Ellington Song Book sessions further cemented its place in the repertoire. While not called as frequently on jam sessions as some Ellington staples, it remains a respected standard among arrangers and vocalists for its evocative atmosphere and nuanced harmonic palette.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
McCoy Tyner introduced Search for Peace on his Blue Note album The Real McCoy, recorded at Van Gelder Studio on April 21, 1967, with Joe Henderson, Ron Carter, and Elvin Jones. The piece quickly became his signature ballad, prized for its spacious, meditative character and rich, quartal-based harmonies.

Its lyrical, modal harmony and unhurried pacing make it a favorite ballad selection among modern jazz players and educators. The tune is widely counted as one of Tyner's most enduring compositions from his post-Coltrane period and is frequently programmed alongside other Real McCoy staples such as Passion Dance and Contemplation.

form: 32-bar AABA. A sections in the home key feature extended chains of secondary dominants (back-cycling V-of-V movement) resolving to tonic; bridge moves through ii-V cycles/temporary tonicizations before returning to the A material. Common lead-sheet variations include tritone substitutions and altered dominants in the back-cycling sequence.
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote I Love You for the 1944 Broadway musical Mexican Hayride, providing both words and music. The song quickly became a hit in popular music and crossed over to jazz bandbooks soon after its debut.

Its harmony-built on chains of secondary dominants and classic back-cycling ii-V motion-made it a favorite vehicle for bebop and post-bop improvisers. It is widely included in authoritative jazz fake books and is commonly called as a medium-up swing number in F on jazz bandstands.

Vocal and instrumental versions abound; Ella Fitzgerald's Cole Porter Song Book helped cement its jazz-standard status, and instrumentalists frequently explore tritone-sub and altered-dominant options over the tune's characteristic back-cycling.

form: 16-bar blues-derived riff form; typically a single 16-bar chorus built from I7-IV7 movement with a dominant-function turnaround (often V) and, in some lead sheets, a backdoor bVII-V approach; commonly preceded/followed by a groove vamp intro/outro (expanded in the 1973 version).
history_and_facts:
Herbie Hancock wrote Watermelon Man for his Blue Note debut, Takin' Off (1962), drawing on the street cries of produce vendors he remembered from his Chicago childhood. The riff-driven, bluesy melody over a boogaloo groove quickly became a club favorite among hard-bop and soul-jazz players.

A chance run-through at a Bronx gig led Mongo Santamaria to record a Latin boogaloo version that became a 1963 crossover hit, bringing the tune to a mass audience and giving Hancock one of his first major royalty successes. Hancock reimagined it a decade later on Head Hunters (1973), with an iconic funk arrangement featuring Bill Summers' bottle-blown “hindewhu”-style intro and an extended vamp, cementing the tune's status across jazz, Latin, and funk traditions.

Today Watermelon Man is a jam-session staple, commonly called in F, and is widely used to teach boogaloo feel, riff-based melody writing, and a compact 16-bar blues-derived form.

Originally an instrumental titled "I'm Free" written by bassist/arranger Bob Haggart for Bob Crosby's band in 1938, the piece received lyrics by Johnny Burke in 1939 and was retitled "What's New?". It quickly became a popular song and then a jazz standard.

Bing Crosby helped popularize the Burke-lyric version in 1939, and the tune subsequently became a staple ballad for both vocalists and instrumentalists. Definitive jazz interpretations include Frank Sinatra's 1955 reading with Nelson Riddle and John Coltrane's version on Ballads (recorded 1962, issued 1963). The song's enduring appeal even crossed back into mainstream pop when Linda Ronstadt used it as the title track of her 1983 collaboration with Nelson Riddle.

On bandstands it is typically treated as a slow, lyrical ballad that showcases expressive melody and nuanced phrasing.

form: 32-bar AABA (8–8–8–8). In common C-major charts, the A sections are in C; the bridge (B) modulates to F major, often briefly tonicizing D♭ via E♭m7–A♭7 → D♭maj7 before cadencing back to C for the final A. Some lead sheets show minor 1st/2nd-ending variations, but the large-scale form remains AABA.

AABA, 64 bars (16-bar sections). Typically in a minor key; A sections center on the minor tonic, while the bridge modulates away via extended secondary-dominant/cycle-of-fifths motion before returning to the tonic for the final A. Common arrangements may vary reharmonizations but retain the 64-bar AABA outline.
history_and_facts:
Cole Porter wrote Love for Sale for the 1930 Broadway musical The New Yorkers. Its provocative lyric-sung from the perspective of a streetwalker-triggered radio bans and notoriety; the number was ultimately associated with Elisabeth Welch in the stage production after initial controversy. Despite early censorship, the song's sophisticated minor-key melody and urbane wit made it a natural for jazz interpretation.

By the mid-20th century it had become a staple, with definitive readings by Ella Fitzgerald in her 1956 Cole Porter Song Book and hard-swinging modern jazz versions by Miles Davis and Cannonball Adderley in 1958 that cemented its place on bandstands. The tune is commonly called in D minor, often at a medium-up swing tempo, and many arrangements alternate Latin and swing feels while preserving the 64-bar AABA form.
