form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Bridge features circle-of-fifths/secondary-dominant motion with temporary tonicizations, returning to the home key for the last A; many jazz charts omit the operetta verse and may add a brief tag or break into solos.
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1928 operetta The New Moon, this Romberg/Hammerstein song quickly migrated from the stage to the bandstand. Its propulsive circle-of-fifths motion and clear AABA design made it a natural for swing-era arrangers and later small-group jam sessions.
Artie Shaw's late-1930s big-band treatment helped cement the tune's status as a flag-waver, and Billie Holiday's Decca-era reading further established it in the vocal jazz repertory. The refrain is the section most commonly performed in jazz; the original verse from the operetta is often omitted.
The song also gained longevity through screen adaptations of The New Moon (1930 and 1940), after which it remained a reliable up-tempo showcase, frequently called by bands for bright swing tempos and tenor battles.

history_and_facts:
Written in 1933 by Bernice Petkere-who penned both words and music-Close Your Eyes emerged from the Tin Pan Alley tradition and has since become a staple for jazz vocalists and small-group swing settings. Petkere, sometimes dubbed the "Queen of Tin Pan Alley," crafted a lyric and melody that lend themselves to both intimate ballad treatments and medium swing grooves.

The song's enduring appeal lies in its straightforward, singable line and approachable harmony, making it a frequent inclusion in vocal books and bandstand repertoires. While interpretations vary in tempo and feel, its core character remains a relaxed, romantic standard that adapts well to diverse arranging styles.

tune_name: Spring Can Really Hang You Up the Most
history_and_facts:
Written in the mid-1950s by St. Louis collaborators Tommy Wolf and poet-lyricist Fran Landesman, the song emerged from the cabaret scene centered around Jay Landesman's Crystal Palace club. Landesman's lyric-often noted as a witty, melancholic echo of T. S. Eliot's line “April is the cruellest month”-helped the piece become a quintessential modern torch song.

The tune quickly migrated from cabaret to the jazz world, becoming a favored ballad vehicle for singers and instrumentalists alike. Its rich, reflective lyric and harmonically nuanced setting have sustained its status as a standard across decades and styles.

form: Through-composed; typically performed exactly as written (no improvisation), often stated twice with a coda; not a standard 32-bar song form.
history_and_facts:
Monk composed this piece in 1957 for his wife, Nellie, while she was recovering from surgery. The title uses the French word for “twilight,” reflecting the tune's contemplative character. Unlike most jazz standards, Monk intended it to be played strictly as written, with no improvisation.
A definitive early studio version appears on Monk's Music (1957), and a historically significant live performance with John Coltrane was captured at Carnegie Hall in 1957 (the tapes were rediscovered and issued decades later). The work also received a notable large-ensemble treatment in Hall Overton's arrangement for Thelonious Monk Orchestra at Town Hall (1959), which highlighted the piece's intricate, through-composed architecture.
famous_recordings: Thelonious Monk Quartet with John Coltrane - 1957

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Commonly arranged as a light, chamber-like feature with brushes and flute; Basie chart often adds written interludes/shout and a tag, but no standard key change in the core 32-bar form.
history_and_facts:
Neal Hefti wrote Cute for Count Basie, and the first recording appeared on Basie's landmark 1958 Roulette album often called The Atomic Mr. Basie. The original track quickly became one of the album's signature lighter moments, contrasting the record's high-energy charts with a deft, witty miniature.
The Basie arrangement spotlights brushes (a drummer's showcase) and flute (famously Frank Wess), with airy textures and antiphonal riffs that typify Hefti's late-1950s writing for the band. Cute became a staple in big-band books and an oft-called feature for drummers, subsequently covered by many touring orchestras and tribute projects, including Quincy Jones's Basie tribute in the early 1960s.

form: 16-bar minor blues; most lead sheets and bandstand practice treat solos as 16 bars in a minor key (no key changes). Nelson's 1961 arrangement often includes an 8-bar vamp intro and matching coda derived from the recorded head arrangement.
history_and_facts:
Oliver Nelson first recorded the piece in 1960 under the title “The Stolen Moment” for Eddie "Lockjaw" Davis' big-band album Trane Whistle, then reworked and retitled it for his landmark 1961 Impulse! album The Blues and the Abstract Truth. The 1961 version-featuring Freddie Hubbard, Eric Dolphy, Bill Evans, Paul Chambers, Roy Haynes, and George Barrow-became the canonical arrangement and propelled the tune into the standard repertoire.

Musicians prize Stolen Moments for its distinctive, lyrical melody set over a 16‑bar minor‑blues framework that invites both modal and functional approaches. It is a staple of jazz education ensembles and jam sessions, commonly called in C minor.

Mark Murphy later wrote lyrics to the tune, helping it enter the vocal canon and further cementing its status as a modern standard.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Commonly published as a major-key ballad; the bridge employs chromatic approach chords/secondary dominants and is often reharmonized. Variations exist across authoritative lead sheets, but no single standard modulation is universally fixed.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Jimmy Van Heusen with lyrics by Eddie DeLange for the short-lived 1939 Broadway musical "Swingin' the Dream"-a swing-era reimagining of Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream-the song quickly outlived the show. A hit recording by Benny Goodman's organization in 1940 helped establish it on the American songbook map.
Miles Davis's 1950 Birth of the Cool session (with Kenny Hagood on vocal) further cemented the tune as a jazz ballad staple, often approached with lush voicings and spacious phrasing. Its lyrical, diatonic A sections contrasted with a more chromatic, reharmonization-friendly bridge make it a favorite vehicle for ballad interpretation among instrumentalists and vocalists alike.

history_and_facts:
Cuban percussionist Mongo Santamaria introduced Afro Blue in 1959, creating one of the first widely adopted jazz standards to foreground an African 3:2 cross-rhythm within a 12/8 feel. Oscar Brown Jr. later supplied lyrics that helped the tune cross into the vocal repertoire.
John Coltrane's 1963 performances and his Live at Birdland recording reframed the piece as a powerful modal waltz, with Elvin Jones's polyrhythms and McCoy Tyner's quartal harmony becoming touchstones for subsequent interpretations. Vocalists such as Abbey Lincoln popularized the lyric version soon after its debut, cementing the tune's status on both Latin-jazz and straight-ahead bandstands.
The piece remains a staple for exploring hemiola and polyrhythmic layering: bands often alternate between a 12/8 Afro-Cuban groove and 3/4 swing, highlighting the tune's rhythmic DNA while maintaining its modal, minor-centered harmony.

history_and_facts:
Composed by Italian singer-pianist Bruno Martino with lyrics by Bruno Brighetti, "Estate" (Italian for "Summer") originated around 1960; its original lyric famously lamented the season. The song later shed its early novelty image to become a sophisticated vehicle for jazz interpretation.
It entered the international jazz repertoire largely through Brazilian and American interpreters, notably João Gilberto, and has since been recorded by many singers and instrumentalists. Jazz musicians often render it as a bossa-inflected ballad, commonly in D minor.
The tune's rich harmony and lyrical melancholy have made it a modern standard, often listed under its Italian title.

form: ABAC (32-bar)
history_and_facts:
Irving Berlin wrote both music and lyrics in 1932; the song is often listed with its parenthetical subtitle, "How High Is the Sky." It became a major hit in its release year through Bing Crosby, helping establish it in the American popular song canon.
The lyric is notable for being built almost entirely from rhetorical questions, and the tune quickly entered the jazz repertoire as a favored ballad by singers and instrumentalists alike.

composer: Vernon Duke (lyrics: John La Touche, Ted Fetter)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8); typically no key change across sections
history_and_facts:
Composed by Vernon Duke with lyrics by John La Touche and Ted Fetter, Taking a Chance on Love was written for the Broadway musical Cabin in the Sky, which opened in 1940. It was introduced on stage by Ethel Waters and quickly entered the swing-era repertoire.

The song's broad popularity surged with Benny Goodman's 1943 hit recording featuring vocalist Helen Forrest, which topped the charts and cemented the tune as a standard. It was also featured in the 1943 MGM film adaptation of Cabin in the Sky, further amplifying its reach. Musicians prize its classic 32-bar AABA construction and its buoyant, optimistic lyric-hallmarks of a durable swing-era show tune.

form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Typically in a major key; A sections largely diatonic with common II-V motion. Bridge often tonicizes the IV and related minor areas before returning via II-V-I. Common lead-sheet variations include tritone substitutions on dominant chords and a turnaround tag at the end of the last A.
standard_key: F major (common instrumental call key); also Eb major (frequent vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed for the 1944 Columbia film Cover Girl, “Long Ago (and Far Away)” was introduced on screen by Rita Hayworth (her singing voice dubbed by Martha Mears) and Gene Kelly. The song was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Song, ultimately losing to “Swinging on a Star.”
It quickly became a major popular hit in 1944 with charting versions by Dick Haymes & Helen Forrest, Bing Crosby, and Jo Stafford, cementing its place in the American Songbook. Jazz artists later adopted it as a lyrical ballad vehicle, prized for Jerome Kern's graceful melody and Ira Gershwin's wistful lyric.
Notably, it represents one of the most enduring collaborations between Kern and Ira Gershwin, a pairing that occurred after the death of Ira's brother, George. The tune's elegant 32-bar AABA design and straightforward harmonic language make it a staple on bandstands, often called in F for instrumental settings and in Eb for singers.

history_and_facts:
Black Nile is a Wayne Shorter original first recorded on April 29, 1964 for his Blue Note debut as a leader, Night Dreamer, featuring Lee Morgan, McCoy Tyner, Reggie Workman, and Elvin Jones. The piece is a brisk, minor-key post-bop tune with Shorter's hallmark angular melody and shifting harmonic colors.

The composition quickly entered the modern jazz repertoire and is frequently called in conservatories and small-group settings for its challenging line and contemporary language. The title aligns with the album's evocative, imagistic themes (Night Dreamer, Virgo, Armageddon), reflecting Shorter's penchant for suggestive, poetic naming.

form: "Black Nile" has a complex form that moves beyond the typical AABA structure. It is essentially a 40-bar form that can be broken down into an ABC structure, which is then repeated with slight variations. The tune begins with a modal A section that is followed by a more harmonically active B section before a bridge-like C section. This entire structure is then repeated for the solo sections, showcasing Shorter's innovative approach to jazz composition.
12-bar blues
history_and_facts: The tune was first recorded by the Jay McShann Orchestra with vocals by Walter Brown in 1941, though it was originally titled 'I'm Confessin''. The composition's title was later changed to 'Confessin' the Blues' and credited to both McShann and Brown. Its raw, swinging Kansas City blues sound was influential, particularly on rhythm and blues and rock and roll artists. The tune experienced a major revival and broader recognition through its interpretation by rock and roll pioneers. The Rolling Stones, in particular, famously covered it on their 1964 album, 'The Rolling Stones No. 2,' exposing a new generation to this classic blues standard.

chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Bill Evans's Time Remembered is a contemplative, harmonically distinctive ballad that has become a core part of the modern jazz canon. Though composed in the early 1960s, some of Evans's studio takes were not issued until years later, helping the tune gain broader recognition posthumously. It is widely circulated in Real Book-style collections and called frequently by pianists and small-group ensembles.

Analysts (notably Jack Reilly) have pointed out the tune's unusual pitch collection and the near-absence of traditional functional cadences, contributing to its floating, timeless quality. Time Remembered is often performed as a very slow ballad, sometimes rubato, to highlight its nuanced harmonic color and introspective mood.

form: AABA, 32-bar chorus (8 bars per section), typically preceded by a 16-bar verse. Bridge uses circle-of-fifths motion and secondary dominants but generally does not modulate away from the tonic; many arrangements include a rubato intro and/or closing tag.
standard_key: E-flat major (most common instrumental call); also C major (frequent, especially for some vocal/instrumental settings)
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1926 Broadway musical Oh, Kay!, the song was introduced on stage by Gertrude Lawrence. Although originally conceived at a brighter foxtrot tempo, it quickly evolved into a slow torch ballad-the manner in which it is now almost universally performed. Lawrence famously sang it onstage to a rag doll, an image that helped cement the song's air of vulnerable, intimate confession.

Across the decades it became a core American Songbook standard embraced by jazz singers and instrumentalists alike. Defining recordings by Frank Sinatra (first in 1946) and Ella Fitzgerald (1959, on her landmark Gershwin Song Book) shaped the tune's ballad identity, while Sarah Vaughan's late‑1950s version further solidified its place in the repertoire. The piece is typically played AABA at ballad tempo with the optional verse, and it remains a favorite vehicle for rubato introductions and expressive lyric interpretation.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections)
standard_key: F minor (also called in G minor)
history_and_facts:
Written in 1939 by composer Arthur Altman with lyrics by Jack Lawrence, “All or Nothing at All” first appeared on a Harry James Orchestra side featuring a young Frank Sinatra. Initially it made little impact, but when it was reissued in 1943-after Sinatra's rise to fame-it became a major hit and one of his early calling cards.
The tune's minor-key contour and wide melodic intervals have made it a favorite ballad for interpreters from vocalists to instrumentalists. Sinatra revisited it in a slow, luxuriant 1957 Capitol version, while John Coltrane's reading on the Ballads sessions helped cement its status among modern players.
Over time it has become a recognized jazz standard, frequently performed as a ballad (sometimes moving to medium swing) and commonly called in F minor on instrumental bandstands.

tune_name: Our Love is Here to Stay
form: 32-bar AABA (8-8-8-8). Commonly played with an optional 2-bar tag repeating the final lyric phrase. Harmony features functional movement with secondary dominants; the bridge briefly tonicizes closely related areas before returning to the tonic. No standard modulation between sections in the lead-sheet norm.
standard_key: F major; also Eb major; vocal arrangements often in Bb major
history_and_facts:
The last complete song George Gershwin wrote, it was published posthumously in 1938 with Ira Gershwin's lyric and introduced in the Samuel Goldwyn film The Goldwyn Follies by tenor Kenny Baker. Although legally titled Love Is Here to Stay, musicians and listeners have long referred to it as Our Love Is Here to Stay, echoing its opening line.
Gene Kelly's 1951 performance along the Seine in An American in Paris helped cement the song's popular legacy, while definitive jazz readings by Frank Sinatra (Capitol era), Ella Fitzgerald (Gershwin Song Book), and Ella & Louis made it a jam‑session staple. The tune's balanced 32‑bar AABA design and lyrical Gershwin harmonies invite both ballad and medium‑swing treatments.
Ira Gershwin noted that he had considered other titles, but the publisher retained Love Is Here to Stay. The song has since become one of the most recorded standards in the Great American Songbook and remains a common call at jazz sessions.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically in a major key with circle-of-fifths motion and frequent secondary dominants; bridge sequences through dominants before resolving to the tonic. Common lead-sheet variations include tritone substitutions and ii-V turnarounds at cadences; no large-scale modulation.
history_and_facts:
Written in 1941 by Victor Schertzinger with lyrics by Johnny Mercer, I Remember You was introduced in the Paramount film The Fleet's In (released 1942), where it was sung by Dorothy Lamour. It quickly became a wartime favorite, spawning popular 1942 chart versions by leading big bands alongside the film performance.

The tune entered the jazz canon in the 1950s, prized for its lyrical melody and circle-of-fifths harmonic flow that lends itself to both ballad interpretations and medium-tempo swing. Notable jazz recordings include Stan Getz with the Oscar Peterson Trio (1957) and Ella Fitzgerald's rendition on her Johnny Mercer Song Book (1964), which helped cement its status among standards.

Biographers often note Mercer's lyric was inspired by a deeply personal romance of the era, adding to the song's enduring poignancy. Schertzinger, a film composer-director, died shortly before the movie's release, making this and its sister hit Tangerine among his final and most lasting contributions.

form: 32-bar AABA: four 8-bar sections; typically remains in the home major key throughout. Bridge commonly uses cycle-of-fifths dominants/secondary dominants; lead-sheet variations include reharmonized turnarounds and optional short tag endings.
standard_key: Bb major (most common instrumental call); also C major and F major (frequent vocal keys)
history_and_facts:
Originally an instrumental titled “Never No Lament,” the piece was recorded by Duke Ellington's orchestra in 1940. Lyricist Bob Russell added words in 1942, at which point it became “Don't Get Around Much Anymore.” The song quickly entered the popular and jazz repertoires.
In 1943 both Ellington and The Ink Spots scored major hits with the newly titled song, helping to cement it as one of Ellington's most enduring standards. It has since been covered widely by instrumentalists and singers alike and is a staple in swing-era and small-group jazz sets.
Musicians typically treat it as a 32-bar AABA standard in a bright-to-medium swing feel. While often called in Bb for jam sessions and instrumental settings, vocal arrangements vary widely by range, contributing to its presence in multiple common keys.
famous_recordings: Duke Ellington and His Famous Orchestra - 1940
Duke Ellington and His Famous Orchestra - 1943

tune_name: On a Slow Boat to China
form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections); typically performed as a medium swing; commonly presented without modulation in the chorus.
history_and_facts:
Frank Loesser wrote both words and music in 1948, and the song quickly became a hit and an enduring American popular song standard. Its title plays on the idea of an extremely long ocean voyage-perfect for a romantic serenade-capturing postwar audiences' imagination.
Early hit recordings in 1948 by Kay Kyser, Jo Stafford & Gordon MacRae, and others cemented its popularity, and jazz artists soon adopted it as a medium-swing vehicle. The tune's straightforward 32-bar AABA design and memorable lyric made it a favorite for both vocalists and instrumentalists.
Over the decades it has remained in the active repertoire, appearing on stages, broadcasts, and recordings across jazz and traditional pop contexts.

composer: Billy Bird / Teddy McRae / Henri Woode
form: 32-bar AABA; 8-bar sections. Commonly treated as a rhythm-changes variant: riff-based A sections in the tonic with a III-VI-II-V bridge leading back to I. Bandstand charts often include substitutions (e.g., tritone subs) on the bridge.
standard_key: F major; also commonly called in Eb major
history_and_facts:
Broadway is a swing-era riff tune credited to Billy Bird, Teddy McRae, and Henri Woode and was popularized by Count Basie's orchestra in 1940. The Basie side helped cement the piece as a big-band and small-group jam vehicle, thanks to its catchy melody and straightforward 32-bar AABA layout that invites solos.
Over time it became a bandstand staple, frequently called at medium-up tempos and often approached as a rhythm-changes variant. Although the tune appears in multiple keys, F and Eb are the most common concert call keys among instrumentalists, and the bridge typically follows a III-VI-II-V cycle back to the tonic.

history_and_facts:
Bob Dorough wrote and first recorded Devil May Care in the late 1950s, introducing it on his Bethlehem Records album of the same name. Its breezy, rapid-fire lyric and bebop-tinged lines made it a natural vehicle for scat and hip phrasing, and it has since been embraced primarily as a vocal showcase.
The tune gained renewed prominence through later recordings, most notably Diana Krall's 1999 version, which helped cement its place on modern bandstands. Earlier vocal champions like Teri Thornton also helped circulate the song among jazz singers in the 1960s.
Dorough-later widely known for Schoolhouse Rock!-crafted both words and music, and Devil May Care remains one of his most called originals among jazz vocalists.

history_and_facts:
Thelonious Monk's ballad Ask Me Now was first recorded for Blue Note in 1951 and quickly became one of his signature lyrical pieces. Its angular, wide-interval melody and chromatic inner-voice motion mark it as quintessential Monk, yet it's played at a tender, reflective tempo that has made it a favorite ballad on bandstands.

Jon Hendricks later supplied lyrics (often performed under the title "How I Wish"), helping the tune cross into vocal repertoire. Saxophonist Steve Lacy-one of the earliest and most devoted interpreters of Monk-cemented the piece's status with his all-Monk album Reflections (1959), and Monk himself revisited the tune in later Riverside-era recordings, ensuring its place in the core jazz canon.

composer: Benny Goodman and Edgar Sampson (lyrics: Mitchell Parish)
history_and_facts:
Originating with arranger-composer Edgar Sampson for Chick Webb's orchestra, “Don't Be That Way” was later adopted and popularized by Benny Goodman; Mitchell Parish subsequently supplied lyrics. The tune became a Swing Era staple, most commonly performed as an instrumental.
It is best remembered as the opening number of Benny Goodman's landmark 1938 Carnegie Hall Jazz Concert, a performance widely cited by jazz historians as a milestone in bringing swing to America's premier concert stage.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-bar sections). Contrafact on rhythm changes in B-flat; A sections use rhythm-changes harmony with Monk's characteristic altered dominants and chromatic approach ii-V figures; bridge cycles dominant chords (D7-G7-C7-F7) returning to Bb. Common bandstand variations include tritone substitutions and stop-time hits in the head.
history_and_facts:
A pun on “rhythm changes,” Rhythm-a-Ning is Monk's bebop contrafact built over George Gershwin's I Got Rhythm harmony. Although likely conceived earlier, its first known recording was in 1957, and it quickly became a staple of Monk's sets and a jam-session favorite.
Many scholars and educators note that the opening riff echoes a figure from Mary Lou Williams's 1937 Walkin' and Swingin'-a nod to Williams's influence on Monk and Bud Powell. The head's displaced accents, stop-time hits, and Monk's signature use of altered dominants make it a model study in rhythmic phrasing over a familiar 32-bar form.
Definitive documents include the 1957 date with Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers, blistering live takes with Johnny Griffin from 1958, and the polished Columbia studio version on Criss-Cross (1963), all of which helped cement the tune as a core modern jazz standard.

tune_name: One Note Samba (Samba de Uma Nota Só)
composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Newton Mendonça; English: Jon Hendricks)
form: AABA, 64 bars total (16-bar sections). A sections feature a repeated single-note melody over a cycling series of dominants/ii-V progressions; the bridge shifts through sequential ii-V movements before returning to the tonic. Common variations include a vamp/tag on the final A or a brief coda.
standard_key: Bb major (concert) - most common instrumental call key; also played in C major; vocal renditions often in D or F
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antonio Carlos Jobim with original Portuguese lyrics by Newton Mendonça, One Note Samba encapsulates the bossa nova aesthetic: understated rhythm and harmonic sophistication under a deceptively simple melodic idea built on a single pitch. The English lyric by Jon Hendricks cleverly comments on the tune's one-note premise, turning the concept into witty, meta-textual wordplay.

The song gained international prominence during the early 1960s U.S. bossa nova craze. João Gilberto's 1960 recording established it in Brazil; the 1962 Stan Getz/Charlie Byrd recording helped introduce it to American jazz audiences, and Jobim's own 1963 orchestral album further cemented its status. Ella Fitzgerald's later version showcased the tune's potential for scat and rhythmic nuance.

Its harmonic motion-cycling dominants and ii-V chains under a static melodic pitch-makes it a favorite classroom example of melodic economy over rich changes, and a staple on jazz bandstands worldwide.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically remains in the home key throughout; some arrangements add a short tag or optional introductory verse from the film.
standard_key: Db major (common instrumental call key); C major also encountered
history_and_facts:
Richard Rodgers and Lorenz Hart wrote You Are Too Beautiful for the 1933 film Hallelujah, I'm a Bum, where it was introduced by Al Jolson. Like many Rodgers & Hart ballads, it features a memorable chorus and an optional verse that is occasionally performed in concert settings.

The song entered the jazz canon largely through later interpretations, most famously the 1963 recording by John Coltrane and Johnny Hartman, which is widely cited by jazz educators and discographies as a definitive modern reading. Since then it has remained a staple ballad for instrumentalists and vocalists, often called in Db for its lush sonority on horns and piano.

tune_name: On a Clear Day (You Can See Forever)
history_and_facts:
Written by Burton Lane with lyrics by Alan Jay Lerner for the 1965 Broadway musical On a Clear Day You Can See Forever, the title song quickly crossed from stage to the jazz repertoire. Tony Bennett's 1965 single helped popularize it beyond the theater audience, and its buoyant melody and functional harmony made it attractive to jazz arrangers and improvisers.
The tune has since been interpreted by numerous jazz artists; organist Shirley Scott even titled a 1966 album after it, and Bill Evans recorded a notable solo piano version in 1968. The 1970 film adaptation starring Barbra Streisand further cemented the song's place in popular culture, ensuring its continued presence on bandstands.

history_and_facts:
Benny Golson wrote Are You Real? during his tenure with Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers. The tune was introduced on the landmark Blue Note album Moanin' (recorded in 1958), in the quintessential Messengers lineup with Lee Morgan, Benny Golson, Bobby Timmons, Jymie Merritt, and Art Blakey.

Its memorable, soulful hard-bop melody and logical harmony helped cement it as part of the Jazz Messengers' core book and a staple of the modern jazz repertoire.

composer: Duke Ellington (lyrics: Irving Gordon, Irving Mills)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8 bars per section). The A sections are centered on Db with dense chromatic voice-leading; the bridge moves through remote tonal areas before returning. Authoritative lead sheets sometimes differ in specific passing chords and optional tags/codas.
standard_key: Db major; C major; Bb major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Ellington introduced Prelude to a Kiss in 1938, crafting one of his most harmonically sophisticated ballads. The piece marries chromatic inner lines with a lyrical, songlike melody, and its published lyric by Irving Gordon and Irving Mills quickly positioned it for both instrumental and vocal interpretations.
Johnny Hodges' 1938 recordings helped cement the tune's status, showcasing the alto saxophone's capacity for plush, vocal phrasing on Ellington ballads. In the modern era, Ella Fitzgerald's 1957 Duke Ellington Songbook version reintroduced it to a wide audience, and tenor greats like Ben Webster made it a signature vehicle for lush, breathy ballad playing.
On the bandstand, it's widely treated as a slow ballad in Db, though singers often transpose for range. Its chromatic motion and non-diatonic modulations make it a favorite for advanced harmony study and a benchmark test of intonation and voice-leading for both horn players and vocalists.

form: 24-bar through-composed (no standard AABA). Commonly grouped as three 8-bar phrases; opens in E minor and moves through remote tonal centers before returning. Many bandstand versions add an optional E minor pedal vamp for intro/outro.
history_and_facts:
Joe Henderson introduced Inner Urge on his Blue Note album of the same name, recorded November 30, 1964 at Van Gelder Studio with McCoy Tyner, Bob Cranshaw, and Elvin Jones. The piece quickly became one of Henderson's most enduring originals from his Blue Note period.

The tune's reputation rests on its stark, intervallic melody and demanding harmony that shifts through distant tonal centers-traits that make it a rite of passage on modern jam sessions and in conservatory curricula. While often described as modal, its non-functional motion and quartal-flavored voicings give improvisers a wide canvas for outside/inside play.

Many players set up Inner Urge with a short E minor pedal vamp and tag the same vamp at the end. Lead sheets typically present a continuous 24-bar form rather than a conventional AABA layout.

form: 32-bar AABA contrafact on “I Got Rhythm” (rhythm changes) in B-flat. A sections: 8 bars each, diatonic in Bb with Ellington's riff-based melody. Bridge: 8 bars cycling dominant thirds III7-VI7-II7-V7 (D7-G7-C7-F7), often realized as four ii-V pairs (Am7-D7 | Dm7-G7 | Gm7-C7 | Cm7-F7). Many arrangements add Ellington's original intro and shout tag as optional.
history_and_facts:
Duke Ellington's 1940 “Cottontail” is a high-velocity riff tune built on the chord changes of Gershwin's “I Got Rhythm,” and a hallmark of the Blanton-Webster era of Ellington's orchestra. The first recording, cut in 1940, features a celebrated Ben Webster tenor saxophone solo that helped cement the piece as a jam-session staple and a proving ground for saxophonists.

The tune's later life in vocal jazz is equally storied: Jon Hendricks penned vocalese lyrics to Webster's solo, and Lambert, Hendricks & Ross popularized that version in 1960. “Cottontail” remains one of the definitive up-tempo rhythm changes vehicles in the jazz repertoire, frequently called at brisk tempos and often performed with Ellington's original intro and shout-chorus tags.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). A sections in the home major; bridge typically cycles through dominant-function changes (III-VI-II-V) to return to I. Many arrangements add a brief tag (2-4 bars) on the final A.
standard_key: Bb major (instrumental call); also common in F major and G major for vocalists
history_and_facts:
Written for the 1947 Broadway musical Brigadoon, Almost Like Being in Love quickly crossed from the stage to the pop and jazz worlds. Frank Sinatra's 1947 Columbia single helped popularize it beyond the theater, and the tune soon became a flexible vehicle for jazz interpretation.
It reappeared prominently in the 1954 MGM film version of Brigadoon, sung by Gene Kelly, and later entered the jazz canon through instrumental readings such as Oscar Peterson's 1959 Lerner & Loewe songbook and numerous small-group treatments. Jazz musicians often favor a bright swing tempo and the classic AABA layout, sometimes adding a short tag on the title phrase to close.
On bandstands the tune is frequently called in Bb for instrumentalists, while vocal renditions vary by range, with F and G commonly used. Its straightforward 32-bar design and cycle-of-fifths bridge make it a friendly standard for improvisers.

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Newton Mendonça; English: Norman Gimbel)
form: 32-bar ABAC. A=8 bars, B=8 bars, A=8 bars, C=8 bars; typically in a major key (commonly C). Hallmarks include a borrowed iv minor (often Fm6 in C) and a minor ii-V (e.g., Em7b5-A7) resolving to ii; many lead sheets/show arrangements add a 4-8 bar tag on the ending.
standard_key: C (instrumental call key); D (common vocal key)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antônio Carlos Jobim with original Portuguese lyrics by Newton Mendonça, Meditação emerged at the height of the bossa nova movement and was circulating by 1961, when João Gilberto's recording helped establish it in Brazil. The English lyric by Norman Gimbel soon broadened its reach in the U.S. market.
Jobim's own 1963 instrumental version on The Composer of Desafinado, Plays and the 1963 U.S. exposure via Stan Getz/Luiz Bonfá cemented the tune as a core bossa standard. Astrud Gilberto's 1965 rendition popularized the English version, and the Sinatra-Jobim collaboration further ingrained it in the jazz and pop repertoire.
Musicians prize the tune's lyrical melody and characteristic Jobim harmony-major tonic moving to the borrowed iv minor and elegant minor ii-V motion-making it a staple on jazz bandstands and in Real Book collections.

history_and_facts:
Written by Sammy Fain with lyrics by Bob Hilliard for Walt Disney's 1951 animated film Alice in Wonderland, the title song quickly attracted jazz musicians for its lyrical melody and natural fit as a 3/4 waltz. It entered the jazz repertoire soon after the film's release.
Dave Brubeck's 1957 reading on Dave Digs Disney helped establish Disney material as fertile ground for modern jazz, while Bill Evans's 1961 Village Vanguard performances cemented the tune's status as a core jazz waltz. Evans's harmonically rich trio versions, often called in C, remain particularly influential among pianists.
The piece is commonly performed as a ballad-to-medium waltz on bandstands, with vocalists sometimes favoring keys like F to suit range.

history_and_facts:
Bud Powell's "Parisian Thoroughfare" is a high-velocity bebop tune first recorded in 1951 for Blue Note, later issued on The Amazing Bud Powell, Vol. 1. The piece is famous for its vivid introduction and tag that imitate the hustle of a busy city street with horn-like clusters and whole‑tone gestures, leading into a bristling bebop head.

Clifford Brown and Max Roach's 1954 recording helped cement the work as a modern jazz standard, and many bands have adopted the playful "street sounds" intro/outro as part of the arrangement. The tune remains a favored vehicle for showcasing precise bebop articulation and up‑tempo ensemble cohesion.

composer: Herb Ellis, Lou Carter, Johnny Frigo
history_and_facts:
Written by the Soft Winds (guitarist Herb Ellis, pianist Lou Carter, and bassist/violinist Johnny Frigo) in the late 1940s, Detour Ahead became a favorite among jazz vocalists for its cautionary lyric built on road and travel imagery. The trio introduced several songs that later became standards, with Detour Ahead among the most enduring.
Bill Evans's 1961 Village Vanguard performance helped cement the tune as an instrumental ballad staple, and subsequent vocal and instrumental interpretations have kept it active on bandstands. It appears in major jazz fake books and is widely regarded as a core ballad in the American jazz repertoire.

form: AB, 16 bars. The form is a continuous progression that does not repeat sections in the traditional AABA or ABAB manner. It is sometimes described as a series of ii-V progressions and modulations. The form is played twice to complete a full chorus.
history_and_facts: Joe Henderson's tune "Recordame" first appeared on his 1963 album 'Page One'. The tune's title, which is Spanish for 'remember me', is a nod to its strong Latin feel and is a testament to the influence of Latin jazz on the hard bop scene of the era. The tune is often cited in jazz pedagogy for its sophisticated harmonic structure, which moves through a series of key centers. While many jazz standards use a repeating form like AABA, 'Recordame' is a through-composed 16-bar form that is typically played twice to create a 32-bar chorus. The tune's harmonic and melodic characteristics make it a favorite for improvisers looking to explore modal and tonal concepts in a single composition.

form: 12-bar blues; typically played as a riff-based head with common blues variations (quick IV in bar 2 optional, minor iv in bar 9, and standard V-IV-I-V turnaround). No bridge; solos proceed over repeated 12-bar choruses.
history_and_facts:
Soft Winds is widely treated as a Goodman riff-blues associated with his small-group recordings featuring guitarist Charlie Christian, becoming a jam-session staple for swing-era and modern players alike. The tune's head is a concise, swinging riff that sets up blues-chorus improvisation.

Later generations-most notably piano trios-kept the piece in circulation; Oscar Peterson's versions helped cement its status among instrumental standards. While vocal versions exist with later-added lyrics, the tune is primarily known and called as an instrumental blues on bandstands.
chords: unknown

history_and_facts:
Chick Corea wrote Windows in the mid-1960s during his early post-bop period. The first recording appeared on flutist Hubert Laws' album Laws' Cause (1966), with Corea at the piano. The tune gained wider attention the following year via Stan Getz's Sweet Rain (1967), also featuring Corea, and later appeared under Corea's name on the Atlantic compilation Inner Space (released 1973, from 1966-68 sessions).

Windows is a modern jazz waltz known for its spacious, intervallic melody and harmonically shifting, modal flavors-an exemplar of Corea's emerging voice that bridged hard bop and the more exploratory post-bop of the late 1960s. It has since become a staple among modern jazz musicians and is commonly called at jam sessions and in conservatory programs.

form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). A sections stay in the home key with diatonic movement and secondary dominants; the 8-bar bridge typically cycles through chains of dominants/ii-V progressions and resolves back to the tonic. Original show verse exists but is rarely performed in jazz settings; some arrangements add a tag or end with a modulation for a final chorus.
standard_key: Bb major (instrumental call); also common: F major (vocal), C major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Written by Richard Rodgers and Lorenz Hart for the 1938 Broadway musical The Boys from Syracuse-often cited as the first successful Broadway musical derived from Shakespeare (The Comedy of Errors)-“This Can't Be Love” quickly left the stage and entered the jazz repertoire. Hart's witty lyric flips the typical torch-song conceit, insisting it can't be love precisely because there are none of the usual symptoms.

The tune's buoyant, uncluttered AABA design and strong harmonic motion make it a favorite medium-to-up-tempo swinger for both vocalists and instrumentalists. Ella Fitzgerald's 1956 rendition on her Rodgers & Hart Song Book helped cement its jazz standard status, and Oscar Peterson's 1959 songbook reading showcased its potential as a hard-swinging trio vehicle.

In jazz performance, players commonly call it in concert Bb; singers frequently favor F or C. While the original show includes an introductory verse, most modern jazz charts go straight to the 32-bar chorus.

history_and_facts:
Composed by tenor saxophonist Eddie Harris, Cold Duck Time was introduced on the live album Swiss Movement, recorded with pianist Les McCann at the Montreux Jazz Festival on June 21, 1969 and released later that year on Atlantic Records. The album's breakout success (alongside the hit Compared to What) helped propel Cold Duck Time into the soul-jazz repertoire.

The tune's riff-driven, backbeat feel and bluesy harmony made it a jam-session favorite in the late 1960s and 1970s, and it remains a common call on bandstands. Harris's catchy melodic hooks and groove-oriented writing are emblematic of his crossover appeal during this period.

While widely performed and recorded, reliable published details on formal specifics and a single “standard” key vary across charts and bands; authoritative lead sheets and consensus call keys are not uniform in available sources.

tune_name: Dream a Little Dream of Me
composer: Fabian Andre / Wilbur Schwandt (lyrics: Gus Kahn)
form: 32-bar AABA (8-bar sections). Typical arrangements add a short tag/coda; some recordings modulate up a half- or whole-step for a final chorus.
standard_key: C major (most common); also Bb major, F major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed in 1931 by Fabian Andre and Wilbur Schwandt with lyrics by Gus Kahn, the song quickly became a hit in the dance-band era via recordings by Wayne King and Ozzie Nelson. Its gentle, lulling melody and straightforward AABA structure made it a natural for singers and band vocal features.
The tune has remained a vocal-jazz staple, typically rendered as a slow ballad with a light swing feel and a tagged ending. A major pop revival came in 1968 with Cass Elliot's hit recording with The Mamas & the Papas, which reintroduced the song to a new generation and helped cement its place in the standard repertoire.
famous_recordings: The Mamas & the Papas (feat. Cass Elliot) - 1968

composer: Antonio Carlos Jobim (lyrics: Aloysio de Oliveira; English: Ray Gilbert)
form: AABA, 32 bars (8-8-8-8). Typically in F major; the bridge uses transient tonicizations via secondary dominants and chromatic root motion. Many performances add a 4-8 bar tag/coda on the final A.
standard_key: F major; also common: E-flat major (vocal)
history_and_facts:
Composed by Antônio Carlos Jobim with Portuguese lyrics by Aloysio de Oliveira, Dindi emerged during the formative bossa nova years and quickly became one of Jobim's most cherished ballads. The title is widely reported to have been inspired by the name of a rural property (Dirindi) frequented by Jobim, with the word softened to Dindi in the lyric. English words by Ray Gilbert helped the tune travel internationally in the mid-1960s.
Early Brazilian recordings, especially by Sylvia Telles, set the intimate, floating bossa feel that later artists would emulate. The 1967 Sinatra-Jobim collaboration brought Dindi to a vast audience, solidifying its status on jazz bandstands alongside other Jobim staples. Performers often begin rubato before settling into a gentle bossa pulse, and many conclude with a short tag on the final phrase.
famous_recordings: Frank Sinatra with Antônio Carlos Jobim - 1967

form: 12-bar blues. Standard jazz blues progression with Monk's characteristic chromatic melodic approach. The angular melody loops back on itself in Monk's distinctive style.
history_and_facts:
Composed by Thelonious Monk and first recorded on July 23, 1951, for Blue Note Records as part of his 'Genius of Modern Music' sessions. The tune features Monk's distinctive melodic approach to the blues form, with a wobbly, angular melody that loops back on itself.

The composition became one of Monk's most widely recorded works and a jam session staple. Miles Davis's 1958 recording on 'Milestones' further popularized the tune and featured John Coltrane, Cannonball Adderley, Red Garland, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones.

Monk titled his 1967 Columbia album after the composition, and a 1988 documentary about Monk's life directed by Charlotte Zwerin also took its name from this iconic tune. The documentary was executive produced by Clint Eastwood and was selected for preservation in the National Film Registry in 2017.
